Plan: if I get a large raise I’ll give credit to sets/Kx and fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (press with A or blank cards, pot-control or fold to heavy aggression on K/7/3/paired turns).

On a board of Ks 3h 7d, I have Jack-high with no draws or meaningful equity. Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and is continuing with a bet of 400 into a 600 pot. This board favors her range, which includes many Kings and better pocket pairs. Since I missed the flop completely and have no path to improvement or a profitable bluff in this spot, folding is the most sensible play to preserve my stack.

I have Ace-Trey offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game. My stack is 9300. The action is currently on me as the dealer. Standard play here is to raise to attack the blinds, as A3o is a strong enough hand to open for a steal or for value against wider defensive ranges. Minni and Claude have been active, but positional advantage is key.

I’m in the SB with 7♦5♦ facing a button open to 300. This is a playable suited connector with good implied odds and multiway playability, and my stack is deep enough to realize that equity. Pot is 450 and I need to call 250 more (300 total), so I need ≈35.7% equity to break even (250/(450+250)). That’s reasonable against a button raising range, especially with backdoor flush/straight potential. 3-betting to 500 is too thin with this hand out of position and would bloat the pot without fold equity or a strong showdown hand. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to a meaningful 3-bet, and then play the flop cautiously OOP — pursue strong draws or discrete pairs, check/fold when completely missed, and apply pressure only when I have clear equity or a disguised made hand.

I'm in the big blind with 3c 2d, the worst starting hand in poker. Flash raised to 300 from the button and Minni called 250 from the small blind. While I only need to call 200 more, 3-2 offsuit has virtually no equity preflop and plays poorly from out of position. Both opponents have demonstrated aggressive tendencies. There's no value in playing this hand even with the discounted call. Preserving my stack (11,250 chips) for premium hands is the optimal strategy over 150 hands. This is a clear fold.

I check. I have 7♦5♦ and flopped a bottom pair on J♦-4♣-7♥. Heads-up and out of position, this is a marginal made hand — good to realize equity but not strong enough to build a big pot OOP against a preflop raiser who will have a lot of Jx/overcards and some pocket pairs. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me call small continuation bets to see a turn (I have backdoor diamonds and can improve to two pair/trips). Plan: call small c-bets (~≤1/3 pot) and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to large raises unless I improve or pick up a clear draw, and start betting/raising only if I pair the 5/7, complete a flush, or get a strong read.

On a dry board like Jd-4c-7h, my pre-flop raising range with A3o (from the button) has many air and marginal hands. Minni checked as the small blind caller. This board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, even if I don't have a piece of it yet. A continuation bet (C-bet) of approximately half the pot is standard here to take down the pot immediately or to set up a bluff on later streets if I pick up equity (like an Ace or running cards). If called, I have two overcards to the 7 and 4, giving me some equity.

Board J♦-4♣-7♥, I hold 7♦5♦ — second pair with weak kicker and backdoor diamond possibilities. Flash’s flop bet of 350 into the pot gives me very good pot odds: calling 350 here requires roughly ~25% equity to break even. I have ~5 clean outs to improve to two pair/trips on the turn (two remaining 7s + three 5s) and I’m up against a button c-bet range that contains a fair number of overcards and bluffs that I currently beat. Raising out of position with second pair is too thin — it bloats the pot and plays poorly when called — and folding surrenders good price and fold equity. Plan: call the 350 to see a turn, realize equity, and re-evaluate. If I miss and face heavy turn pressure I will fold; if I improve (5/7) or get a favourable runout/check from villain I will extract value.

The turn 5♠ paired the board and gives me two pair (7s and 5s) — a very strong holding on an otherwise dry board. Flash c-bet the flop small, which usually represents a wide range (Jx, overcards, small pockets and bluffs). I want to extract value from worse hands (Jx, Kx, smaller pairs, and missed draws) while keeping the pot controlled versus the few hands that beat me (flopped sets or turned trips). A value bet of about 1/2 pot denies a free river card, gets called by many worse hands, and avoids bloating the pot out of position if I’m raised. If Flash check-raises large I’ll put him on a set/trips and proceed cautiously (call smaller raises to extract, fold to highly polarizing shove sizes if the line is inconsistent). Betting ~700 into a 1400 pot is the right balance here.

On the turn, the 5s provides me with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6), but Minni's lead (donk bet) of 700 into 1400 is a sign of strength. She likely has a Jack, a set, or a straight draw that she wants to play aggressively. My Ace-high has very little showdown value here, and calling for a 4-outer (the 6) with 3:1 pot odds is mathematically incorrect. Since I have no significant piece of the board and the opponent is showing aggression into the pre-flop raiser, I will fold.

I’m on the button with A♣3♠. In 3-handed play this is a clear open-raise: I want fold equity and to play the hand in position rather than get priced into a multiway pot with a weak kicker. A standard sizing (~3x BB) applies pressure to the blinds while keeping postflop decisions manageable and is consistent with table tendencies. I’ll raise to 300 total to isolate and take control of the hand; if I face a large 3-bet I can fold or proceed based on pot odds and ranges.

I'm holding K7 of spades from the small blind facing Minni's raise to 300 from the button. The hand has merit despite being marginal: K7 suited provides decent equity against a wide button-raising range, and the two spades give me backdoor flush potential. 

Pot odds: I'm calling 250 to win 450, getting 1.8:1, requiring ~33% equity. K7 suited should have approximately 35-40% equity against Minni's likely wide range given her aggressive button-raising tendencies shown in previous hands. This is a borderline profitable spot mathematically.

Flash (Big Blind) still has to act, but this doesn't significantly change the equity calculation. The spades give this hand more playability than typical K7o. I'll call and play positionally on the flop. This is early in the tournament (hand 19 of 150), so building a solid range while staying disciplined is important, but K7s is hand where calling is justified.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Minni, who is playing quite aggressively (stack of 19,800), raised to 300 from the button, and Claude called from the small blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 700. This gives me 3.5:1 pot odds. However, Q2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against two opponents. Given the previous hands, both Minni and Claude have shown willingness to push hands. Calling here would likely lead to losing more chips on future streets. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Checking on the flop with K♠7♠ on A♥T♠8♦. This board heavily favors Minni's pre-flop raising range (which likely includes pairs, broadway holdings, and Ax combos). My hand is currently just king-high with backdoor flush and gutshot draws. Checking allows me to avoid committing more chips to a weak hand while keeping the possibility of folding if Minni bets, or improving on the turn if she checks.

I have A♣3♠ on A♥ T♠ 8♦ — top pair but a very weak kicker. Heads-up after my preflop raise, Claude checked the flop, so he’s likely on a fairly wide calling range (weaker A-x, T/8, medium pocket pairs, some broadways). I want to extract value from worse A-x and T/8 hands while keeping the pot manageable and denying free cards to hands that can improve. In position I can control the hand postflop, so a sized value bet is best: about half the pot will get called by worse hands and folds out pure floats and small pairs, but won’t commit me to huge pots if Claude check-raises. Plan: bet ~350 to get value; if Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously with my weak kicker. If he check-raises small I’ll often call and probe the turn; if he check-raises very large (polarized, likely set or A with a better kicker) I’ll fold to preserve my stack.

K7s is currently just king-high on A-T-8 - an extremely weak holding. While I have some outs (backdoor flush draw, gutshot), my equity is only around 18-22% against Minni's typical c-betting range. Additionally, Flash (BB) hasn't acted yet, creating significant uncertainty. At hand 19 of 150 with a healthy stack of 10,950, there's no reason to get involved in a marginal multiway pot. This is a clear fold - preserving chips for better spots is the fundamentally sound approach early in the tournament.

I'm holding 9d6h on the button, facing a 100 chip call. While this is a weak unpaired hand, the button position is advantageous and I only need to call 100 chips to see a flop. Key considerations: (1) I have positional advantage over the blinds and will act last postflop, (2) The pot odds are favorable at 250 chips to call 100, (3) 9-6o can flop broadway draws, pairs, or strong two-card hands that give me plenty of options. Against likely 1-2 opponents, position-based play with the ability to fold on the flop if needed makes this a standard call. I'll evaluate the flop texture and opponent action before committing further chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h2h. Claude has limped from the Button, and I have a very attractive price to complete (50 chips to win a pot of 250). While 82 suited is a weak hand, the pot odds (5:1) are sufficient to see a flop, especially since the stack depths are large and I can potentially outplay or hit a disguised hand against a limping range. I will complete the small blind.

I will check. K♠2♦ is a weak, poorly playable hand in a multiway limp pot — easy to be dominated by better Kx hands and has almost no showdown value against two callers. Stacks are deep enough that building the pot with a marginal hand is unnecessary and risky; a raise to isolate is exploitable and out of position postflop. Checking lets me see a cheap flop and keep pot control. Plan: fold to sizable bets on most flops (especially anything with an Ace or King), consider calling small continuation bets on very dry boards or backdoor opportunities, and only proceed aggressively if I improve to a strong pair or better.

I have hit bottom pair (eights) on a board of Kd-Ts-8d. While I have a piece of the board, this texture is quite heavy with broadway cards and a flush draw, which likely hits the ranges of my opponents. Since this was a limped pot and I am out of position, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to see how the button and big blind react to the flop without committing more chips with a marginal hand.

Three-way limp pot and I have top pair with the worst possible kicker (K♠2♦) on K♦ T♠ 8♦. This is a marginal hand in a multiway pot — easy to be dominated by better Kx (KQ/KA/KJ) or to run into tens/eights/sets, and there are diamond draws out there while my single diamond only gives a very slim backdoor possibility. Pot is small and stacks are deep, so I want to keep the pot under control, avoid building a big pot OOP with a weak kicker, and see a free turn. Plan: check and call small continuation bets if they appear for pot odds, but fold to large raises or heavy pressure. If I improve (two pair or better) I'll start getting aggressive.

Checking to take a free turn card. With 9d6h on 8d-Ts-Kd, I have marginal equity (flush draw and straight draws) but no made hand. Both opponents showed weakness by checking, so there's no value in betting. Getting a free card to improve is the optimal play from the button in this spot. I can reassess on the turn based on the new card and opponent action.

The turn card is a Queen, making the board 8d-Ts-Kd-Qc. This board is now extremely connected, with any Jack completing a straight. My bottom pair of eights is very weak in this context. Since the pot is small and I have little showdown value, checking is the best option to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold if facing significant aggression.

I have top pair (K♠) with the absolute worst kicker (2♦) on a fairly wet-ish board (8♦ T♠ K♦ Q♣). This is a three-way limp pot and both opponents checked the turn, which signals weakness or willingness to see a cheap river. Betting here would build a pot out of position and can be exploited or raised by any better Kx (KQ/KA/KJ) — hands that already dominate me — while only extracting small value from holdings I beat (T, 8, some Qx that check). With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with such a vulnerable one-pair hand. I’ll keep pot control, check and let the action go to the river; I’ll fold to heavy river aggression from opponents and only call small bets for pot odds or value if I improve.

I'm holding 9d6h on the button after a flop check-through of 8d-Ts-Kd and turn card of Qc. Both opponents checked the flop and turn, indicating weakness.

Hand evaluation: I have a strong drawing hand with multiple outs - a gutshot straight draw (any J completes to a 9-high straight for 4 outs) and a flush draw (9 remaining diamonds besides 8d, Kd, and my 9d for ~10 outs). Combined, I have approximately 13 unique outs (~52% equity on the river).

Betting rationale:
1. **Opponent weakness**: Both Flash and Minni checked through, showing they lack strong holdings
2. **Draw strength**: My 13 outs represent significant equity against their likely holdings
3. **Position control**: On the button, I can take down the pot immediately or build it if called
4. **Semi-bluff value**: Betting represents a balanced strategy that wins now or improves on river
5. **Pot dynamics**: Current pot of 300 is small enough to apply pressure without overcommitting

A bet of 200 chips (~2/3 pot) applies pressure while keeping the pot manageable. This forces opponents to either fold their marginal holdings or call with vulnerability. Even if called, I retain significant outs to improve on the river.

This is the optimal aggressive line given the situation.

The board (Kd-Ts-8d-Qc) is extremely coordinated and heavy with broadway cards. I have bottom pair (eights) with a very weak kicker. After checking the flop and turn, Claude's 200 bet into a 300 pot indicates strength or a semi-bluff on a board where many hands in a button's limping range (like Jx, Qx, or even Kx) have improved. My hand has very little showdown value against his range and the Big Blind still has to act behind me. Calling is not profitable given the board texture and the likelihood of being behind.

Call 200. Claude’s small turn raise in a checked limp-pot is a thin probe or a value bet from Kx/Tx/Qx rather than a massive range polarizing raise. I have top pair but the absolute worst kicker (K♠2♦), so raising here is unnecessary and often punished by better Ks or induces folds from weaker hands I beat. Folding is too weak given how often top pair is still best and the price is cheap: 200 into a 500 pot (~28.6% equity required). I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, see the river, and then fold to large river aggression while calling small-to-medium river bets for pot odds (or becoming aggressive only if I improve).

Board: 8♦ T♠ K♦ Q♣ J♣. I have top pair with the worst possible kicker (K2). Claude bet small on the turn (a thin probe or small-value raise) and then checked the river — that looks like weakness or a desire to get to showdown cheaply rather than a strong, polarized line. The runout is dangerous (any A or 9 makes the Broadway straight; there are lots of two‑pair/two‑card combos that beat me), and betting myself out of position just bloats the pot and gets me check‑raised or called by many hands that already beat me. There’s little thin value to extract that’s worth the risk. I’ll check back to keep the pot small and go to showdown; if Claude now leads a small bet I’ll consider a call for pot odds, but I’ll fold to large river aggression.

I have a 9-high straight (9-10-J-Q-K) after the river Jc completed my draw. Minni has checked to me on the river after calling my turn bet, indicating a weak holding. With a made hand this strong and the opponent showing weakness, I should bet for value. A 350-chip bet (approximately half the current 700-chip pot) extracts value while giving Minni reasonable odds to call with weaker hands like pairs or missed draws. This is a straightforward value bet on the river with a strong made hand.

Board: 8♦ T♠ K♦ Q♣ J♣. I hold K♠2♦ — top pair with the absolute worst kicker. Pot is 1,050 and Claude leads for 350, so calling costs 350 to win a 1,400 pot after the call (~25% equity needed). Claude limped pre, made a small turn probe and now a small river lead — a line that looks thin and largely non‑polarized (Kx, Qx, Tx, Jx and some missed draws/bluffs). Straights (A or 9) that beat me exist but are relatively limited; many of Claude’s value combos (Q/T/J) I still beat. Raising OOP with a 2‑kicker is reckless — I’d get check‑raised or called by better Ks and give up against the hands that already beat me. Folding surrenders too much value. Given the pot odds and his line, a call is the correct, low‑risk line to reach showdown.
I am on the button with K6o in a 3-handed game. This hand is strong enough to open for a raise to try and take down the blinds or play a pot in position. I will make a standard raise to 300.

Hand: 2♥ T♣ — a very weak, poorly-playable offsuit hand. Facing a standard 3bb open from the Button and with the Big Blind yet to act, I would be out of position postflop either heads-up or (worse) 3-way. This hand is dominated by a large portion of the opener’s range and has almost no postflop maneuverability or showdown value. A call (250 more) simply to see a flop OOP is a low-EV play; a 3-bet bluff to 500 is also unattractive because I have limited fold equity versus the Button and risk getting squeezed or playing a bloated pot OOP. With deep stacks and many hands left to play, preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I'm in the big blind with J9d, a suited connector with decent potential. Flash raised to 300 from the button, and Minni folded. I'm getting 2.25:1 odds (200 to call into a 450 pot), which is favorable for a hand with both straight and flush draws. J9d plays well in multi-way pots and has good implied odds. While I'm out of position post-flop, the pot odds justify a call. I could consider raising, but that risks a 3-bet from Flash's button range without improving my hand's equity. Calling allows me to see the flop and make position-aware decisions with what should be a favorably positioned hand."

Checking with J♦9♦ on a 3♠3♦8♣ flop. I have approximately 15 outs (four 7s, four 10s, and nine remaining diamonds for a flush), giving me 50-55% equity with two cards to come. Flash's check indicates a weak or slowplayed hand. Checking keeps the pot manageable while holding a draw, allows me to see the turn card for free if Flash checks back, and avoids bloating the pot unnecessarily. This is a position-aware play that maximizes my draw's value by controlling pot size.

I opened K6o from the button and Claude called from the Big Blind. The flop is 3s-3d-8c, which is a very dry board. This texture generally misses most of a Big Blind's flatting range and is a good candidate for a continuation bet. Even though I only have King high, I have some showdown value and can often get folds from hands like JQ, JT, or small pocket pairs that don't want to deal with multiple streets of aggression. A standard 1/3 to 1/2 pot bet should suffice here to test Claude's hand.

With J♦9♦ on 3♠3♦8♣, I have approximately 15 outs (four 7s, four 10s, and nine diamonds) providing ~32% equity. Facing 250 to call into a 900 pot (3.6:1), my equity far exceeds the break-even requirement of 21.7%. Calling is mathematically sound and strategically optimal—it allows me to see the turn with good pot odds while controlling the pot from an out-of-position situation. This approach balances my draw's value against Flash's unclear range.

Checking with J♦9♦ on a 3♠3♦8♣5♣ turn. I have ~14 outs (four 7s, four 10s, six additional diamonds) providing approximately 28% equity. Being out of position and first to act, checking is the optimal play: it controls pot size, gathers information about Flash's hand strength, and allows me to see the river while maintaining the flexibility to make an informed decision. My draw is strong enough that I don't need to take the lead in the pot here, especially with position disadvantage.

On the turn card 5c, the board remains very dry (3s-3d-8c-5c). I continuation-bet the flop and was called by Claude. After Claude checks the turn, I have a choice between checking back to realize my King-high showdown value or betting again. Given the board texture, it is unlikely Claude has a 3. He could have an 8, a pocket pair, or a float. By checking back, I keep the pot manageable and can potentially win at showdown or induce a bluff on the river. Betting again might force out some of his air, but I'm not sure I'll get better hands to fold often enough here. I will check back to see a free river.

Checking on the river with J♦9♦ making a pair of nines. Flash's turn check indicates weakness, and I'm out of position. My pair has showdown value without needing to risk additional chips. Checking controls pot size, avoids getting called down by stronger hands, and allows me to see Flash's action. If Flash bets, I can reassess; if Flash checks, my pair likely takes down the pot at showdown. This is the optimal position-aware play with a marginal hand.

I hold King-high (Kc 6d) on a board of 3s-3d-8c-5c-9h. After my flop continuation bet was called and the turn went check-check, Claude has checked the river as well. This sequence suggests Claude likely has a weak hand, either a small pair he is hoping to take to showdown cheaply or unimproved overcards. While I could attempt a bluff to fold out small pairs like 44 or 66, King-high has some showdown value in a 3-handed game against his potential missed floats (like QJ or JT). I will check back to realize my showdown value and avoid the risk of a check-raise.

2♠7♦ is the absolute worst unpaired offsuit hand. Even on the button it has very poor equity versus two defending ranges and will be dominated whenever called. Calling 100 to chase the pot requires ~40% equity (100/250) to break even, which 2‑7o does not have against typical SB/BB calling ranges; raising to try to steal invites multi-way action from players who have shown willingness to defend and to apply post‑flop aggression. With deep stacks I should conserve chips for spots with legitimate fold equity or realizable equity. Fold preflop and wait for a better opportunity on the button.

Heads-up against Flash in the Big Blind. A-2o is marginal but playable in Small Blind position with favorable 3:1 pot odds (150 to call 50). The Ace provides equity, and I can leverage post-flop position against Flash's cautious tendencies. Calling 50 more chips is profitable given stack sizes and game dynamics.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jc 5c. Minni folded on the Button, and Claude (Small Blind) completed his bet to 100 total. This means I have the option to check and see a flop for free. J5 suited has some post-flop playability (flush and straight potential), but it is not a strong enough hand to raise Claude's limp, especially since Claude has been playing relatively solid. Checking is the most standard and risk-averse play here to see a flop.

Heads-up against Flash who checked pre-flop from Big Blind, indicating weakness. I have A-2o with wheel draw potential (8 outs) plus Ace high. On a relatively dry 3-4-T board, a small bet of 125 chips applies pressure while maintaining fold equity. This wins the pot in many cases or builds it when I have equity. Flash's short stack (7,800) and cautious tendencies make this a favorable spot to apply pressure with reasonable draw equity.

I hold Jack-high on a flop of 3h-4s-Td. Claude limped pre-flop from the Small Blind and has now led out for 125 into a 200 pot. My hand has very little equity; I have no pair, no flush draw, and only a weak backdoor straight draw. While Claude could be stabbing at the pot with a wide range, J-5 suited is not strong enough to continue with here, especially without a clear path to improvement or a strong reason to bluff.

I'm holding 4d9d on the button facing a small blind and big blind. While this is a marginal holding, the cost to call is only 100 chips and I have excellent position with two players left to act. The pot odds are favorable (calling 100 into a pot of 150), and if both opponents fold, I win immediately. Additionally, if the flop is favorable (any diamond for a flush draw, any 9 or 4 for a pair), I have postflop playability. The button position gives me the advantage of seeing opponents' actions before making subsequent decisions. Given the modest investment required and my positional advantage, calling is the standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce of clubs (A2c) facing a limp from the Button (Claude). A2o is a reasonably strong starting hand in a 3-handed game, especially when the Button limps. Calling for 50 more chips is very attractive given the pot odds (5:1), and it allows me to see a flop cheaply without over-inflating the pot out of position. While I could raise to take control, Claude's limping range might be trapping or unpredictable based on previous hands. Calling ensures I maintain a manageable pot size with a hand that has good high-card value but mediocre kickers.

There are two limp callers (Button Claude and SB Flash) and I’m on the BB with 8c5c — a suited, connected but relatively weak hand that loses a lot of equity multiway and out of position. I want to maximize fold equity and either take the pot down now or play heads-up where my hand has reasonable post‑flop playability (backdoor clubs and low-straight potential). Flash is the shortest stack and likely to fold to a firm iso; Claude has shown a lot of passive calling, so a modest isolation raise is appropriate. I’ll raise to 900 total (I’ve already posted 100), big enough to price out many limp callers but small enough to keep the pot manageable if called. If everyone folds I win the pot; if called I’ll proceed to c-bet favorable flops and re-evaluate against aggression.

Fold 4d9d pre-flop. While this hand has flush potential, Minni's 900 raise from the big blind represents significant strength. Calling 800 more would commit 6.5% of my stack with a weak holding that's likely behind and will face post-flop pressure while out of position. Early in the tournament (hand 23 of 150), there's no reason to fight marginal spots when better opportunities will arise. Folding preserves stack for premium situations.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit (Ah2c). After I completed the small blind for 50 chips and the Button (Claude) limped, the Big Blind (Minni) made a very large raise to 900 (9x the big blind). This sizing is quite polarizing and suggests either a very strong premium hand or a significant squeeze attempt. Given my hand is A2o, it performs poorly against a range that wants to play for 900 chips pre-flop. I have no reason to believe Minni is bluffing frequently enough here to justify calling 800 more out of position with a weak Ace. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9d 7s. While 3-handed play often requires a wider opening range from the button, 97-offsuit is a marginal hand. Considering Minni's aggressive playstyle and large stack, and Claude's tendency to defend, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening opportunity rather than risking a confrontation with a weak holding.

I’m in the small blind with K♠7♥ heads-up vs the big blind (Claude). This is a playable heads-up hand and I want to seize initiative and apply pressure. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3×BB) — a standard steal size that buys fold equity, isolates the BB, and is small relative to the effective stacks. If he folds I take the 150 pot; if called I’ll continuation-bet most dry flops and fold to large 3-bets (K7o is not worth inflating pots out-of-position), adjusting on connected or two-tone boards where I pick up equity.

Folding Q5o from the big blind against Minni's raise. This is a weak hand with poor showdown value and no real potential. Minni has shown consistent aggression throughout the session and frequently raises from position. Playing Q5o out of position against an aggressive opponent who has me covered is not profitable. With multiple opportunities ahead in the tournament (hand 24 of 150), it makes sense to be selective and wait for stronger holdings where I have better equity and positional advantage.

I’m on the button with pocket sixes (6d6c) and only the blinds to act. With deep stacks (~201bb) and a good hand for both isolation and set-mining, I prefer to take the initiative and raise rather than limp. I’ll open to 300 (3xBB) — a standard, table-consistent sizing that generates fold equity vs the blinds, reduces multiway scenarios, and leaves plenty of postflop maneuvering room. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, control pot when overcards hit, and re-evaluate if I face a large 3-bet (likely fold to a polarized shove, call smaller 3-bets for set-mining depending on opponent tendencies).

8-Td is a marginal hand with poor playability out of position. I'm in the small blind facing Minni's raise from the button with a 250 chip call. In a 3-player game, this is still not a spot to invest chips with a weak hand where I'll be out of position post-flop. My stack is adequate (12,150), and good strategy dictates being selective from the small blind, especially against an active opponent like Minni who has position on me throughout the hand. Better to fold and wait for stronger holdings or premium spots.

I am in the Big Blind holding KJo. Minni (Button) has raised to 300, and Claude (Small Blind) has folded. KJo is a strong hand to defend my blind with, especially in a three-handed game. I have the option to call or 3-bet. Given Minni's frequent opening, a call is standard here to see a flop without bloating the pot unnecessarily out of position, although a 3-bet is also viable. I'll choose to call the additional 200.

On a board of Jd 4d 3h, I have top pair with a King kicker. The pot is 650. Minni, who raised pre-flop, will often continue with a bet here. Since I am out of position, checking to the aggressor is the standard play. I intend to call most standard-sized continuation bets and re-evaluate on the turn, as my hand is strong but vulnerable to diamonds or overcards.

I’m the preflop aggressor (66) on a 3h-4d-Jd flop, heads-up vs Flash who checked. Pocket sixes are an underpair to the J but still worth protection and initiative — the board is fairly dry aside from two diamonds (backdoor possibility) and a single overcard. Standard play is a continuation bet to fold out pure overcards, get value from worse pairs and medium Ax hands, and deny equity. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (350 into 650). This sizing keeps the pot manageable with a marginal hand, preserves fold equity, and leaves room to fold to a strong check-raise or to continue cautiously if called. If he raises big I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (barrel non-diamond low cards, be cautious to further aggression or diamond/Jack turn cards).

I have top pair with a King kicker (TPKK) on a board of Jd 4d 3h. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has placed a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 650. This is a standard sizing. My hand is strong enough to continue, but raising here might drive out her bluffs or weaker hands while potentially getting me into trouble against sets or AJ. Calling allows me to keep her range wide and re-evaluate on the turn. I will call the 350.

On the turn 10h, the board has become more connected. I still have top pair with a strong kicker (Jc Kh). In this spot, being out of position, checking to the pre-flop and flop aggressor (Minni) is the most prudent play. This allows me to see how she responds to the turn card. If she bets, I will likely call given the strength of my hand and the draws present, but checking keeps the pot manageable and allows her to continue bluffing with her air or draws.

Check. I’m on a J‑T‑4‑3 board with pocket 6s — an underpair. I c‑bet the flop and was called, and the turn (Th) is a significant overcard that improves many of Flash’s calling hands (T‑x, J‑x, KQ/QJ/A T etc.). There’s no realistic flush now and our hand has little showdown value or backdoor improvement. Betting here would be a thin bluff that will get called too often by better pairs and T‑combos, and checking keeps the pot manageable given the stacks. I’ll control the pot and reevaluate the river: if Flash leads big I’ll fold; if the river bricks we can consider a small value bet or a bluff depending on the card and his action, and if I hit a 6 I’ll extract value.

On the river 6h, the board texture is Jd 4d 3h Th 6h. There are no completed flushes and the most likely straights (like Q9 or 52) are unlikely given the betting sequence. My Jack-King (TPKK) is a very strong hand in this spot. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely doesn't have a monster like J10 or a set, as those would typically continue betting for value and protection. She is more likely to have a medium-strength hand (like a weaker Jack or a Ten) or a hand like 88/99. By betting here, I can extract value from these marginal hands that might check behind if I check to them. I will bet 600 into the 1350 pot, which is a sizing designed to get called by Tx or Jx hands.

The 6h on the river gives me trips (6d6c + 6h) — this turns my underpair into a very strong, likely best hand versus a single caller. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, small 600 probe on the river) reads as thin value from Jx/Tx or a small bluff; his sizing is small enough that many worse hands will call a modest raise. I should extract value. I’ll raise rather than just call: raise to 1800 total is big enough to charge top-pair/second-pair hands and get called by Jx/Tx or two-pair combos, but not so huge that it folds out too many of those hands. If he jams over the raise I’ll strongly consider calling — trips are near the nut on this board.

On the river 6h, the board is Jd 4d 3h Th 6h. I started with a value bet of 600, but Minni has responded with a significant raise to 1800. This is a polarising move. Looking back at the action, Minni checked the turn, which often indicates a medium-strength hand or a surrendered bluff. However, her raise on the river after I showed interest strongly suggests she either slow-played a monster (like a set of 3s, 4s, or JJ/TT, though TT/JJ would likely bet the turn) or she turned her hand into a bluff. Given that many of the draws missed (like the diamond draw), and my TPKK (Top Pair King Kicker) is quite high in my range, folding seems overly cautious against a player who has shown aggression in previous hands. However, a raise from Minni in this spot often indicates she can beat a single pair. Despite this, the pot odds are roughly 2.1 to 1. Given the line she took, it's possible she has a missed draw or a weaker Jack she's overplaying, but more likely she has me beat with something like Two Pair (J10). Since I am committed with a strong one-pair hand and she could be bluffing missed diamonds, I will call the raise, but I will not re-raise as that would only be called by hands that beat me.

I'm on the button with Q6o, a marginal hand that benefits significantly from position. With only 100 to call to see a flop and good positional advantage, this is a reasonable spot to call. My stack size is healthy at 12150, and I can play post-flop poker effectively from the button. Q6 is not premium enough to raise here, but it's definitely playable in position against these opponents. I'll have the last action on future streets, which allows me to play exploitatively based on my opponents' actions. Flash has a shallow stack (5200) and Minni is dominant (22500), but my position advantage makes this call profitable long-term.

I have 97o in the Small Blind. Claude limped on the Button. The cost to see the flop is only 50 more chips, giving me 5-to-1 pot odds. While 97o is a weak hand and I will be out of position, the price is attractive enough to complete the bet and see if I hit a favorable flop. I will play fit-or-fold on the flop.

I have A♠2♠ in the BB facing two limp callers (SB Flash and Button Claude). A2s is the best suited ace for both nut-flush possibilities and wheel outs and gives useful blockers to other Ax hands. Pot is only 300 and I have a deep stack (22.5k) relative to the table, so raising to isolate is +EV here: I want to take the initiative, cut down to a single caller, and earn fold equity rather than playing a dominated Ace multiway. I will make a sizable isolation raise but not commit too much — 900 total chips. That price is large enough to force folds from a lot of pure limps/speculative hands yet small relative to my stack. Plan post-flop: if heads-up I’ll continuation-bet on acey or spade-favorable textures and proceed cautiously on dry boards or heavy action; if I face a large 3-bet that commits a big portion of an opponent’s stack, I’ll fold A2s to avoid getting dominated, but I’ll consider calling a small 3-bet depending on pot odds and opponent tendencies.

Folding Q6o preflop against Minni's aggressive raise. While position is valuable, Q6 is too weak and marginal to warrant continuing. The hand has limited playability even with button position, and against Minni's wide but strong raising range, folding preserves chips for better spots. This is a straightforward fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 97o. I originally completed the bet after Claude limped, but Minni (Big Blind) has now raised to 900. Calling 800 more with such a weak hand out of position is not profitable, especially against a large raise that indicates a strong range or significant aggression. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9d 4h, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni (SB) and Claude (BB) are both involved. With the high stacks of my opponents, particularly Minni, and the poor quality of my hand, there is no value in calling the 100 to enter the pot or raising. Folding is the most prudent long-term strategy here to preserve my stack.

Heads-up vs Claude (BB) with stacks 22,750 (me) vs 11,950 (him). Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 more (total 100). My hand 4♠8♦ is weak and often dominated; out of position postflop I don't want to build a bigger pot with a marginal holding. Claude has shown a tendency to defend/call preflop rather than folding to small raises, so a min-raise to 200 is unlikely to pick up the pot and would just leave me OOP in a bloated pot. Calling the cheap 50 more lets me see a flop, realize equity on favorable textures, and keep the pot controllable — fold to heavy aggression if I completely miss, continue with pairs/draws. Action: call to 100.

I'm in the Big Blind with pocket fours (4c 4d), facing only Minni in the Small Blind since Flash folded. With a stack of 11,950 versus Minni's 22,700, I need to be thoughtful about my play.

Pocket fours is a small pair with good implied odds if I can hit a set. Key considerations:
- I have 0 to call, meaning I can check for free
- Minni just called from small blind, so she's likely committed to seeing the flop
- I'm in position if this goes to a flop
- Small pairs do well in heads-up situations against a single opponent when they hit
- I want to keep the pot small and try to hit my set cheaply rather than build a big pot preflop

By checking, I accomplish:
1. Get a free look at the flop with a pair that can improve dramatically
2. Avoid inflating the pot when I may need to fold postflop
3. Let Minni take initiative postflop, which I can exploit with position

This is a classic "set mining" scenario. The math supports checking and trying to flop a set rather than building the pot preflop with just a small pair.

I missed the flop: A-7-6 with my 8♦4♠ has no pair and only weak backdoor equity. I only have a one-card gutshot to a 5 (4 outs, ~8.5% on the turn, ~16.5% by the river) and a runner-runner spade flush if both turn+river are spades — effectively negligible. Out of position vs Claude and facing a checked flop, I should prioritize pot control and not invest with essentially air. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me fold to real aggression, and allows me to realize equity cheaply if a 5 or a favorable card appears. Action: check.

I'm in the big blind with pocket fours (4c 4d) on a flop of 6s 7c As. Minni checked from the small blind, indicating weakness. 

My hand is currently just a small pair with an overcard (Ace) on the board, making it very weak. Key considerations:

1. **Minni's check is significant**: She showed weakness, so betting here likely won't win the pot and risks running into a trap.

2. **Hand strength**: My pocket pair is dominated by any broadway card or overcard. Betting with this strength is marginal at best.

3. **Position and stack sizes**: While I have reasonable stack depth (11,950), I'm not deep enough to get creative with marginal hands. I need to be cautious.

4. **Set mining opportunity**: I can see the turn card for free. If I hit my set, my hand becomes very strong. If I miss, I can easily fold to aggression.

5. **Pot size**: At 200 chips, there's no urgent need to take down the pot right now.

Checking allows me to see another card for free while keeping my options open. This is optimal play with a small pair in a heads-up situation against a checking opponent.

I'm out of position with 4♠8♦ and I have no pair on a 6♠7♣A♠6♥ board — only a one-card straight draw to a 5 (4 outs, ~8.7% to hit the river). Pot is small (200) and both of us checked flop and turn, so checking again keeps the pot controlled and avoids throwing chips away with very little equity. A bet here is unlikely to fold out many better hands (A-x, 7x, pocket pairs) and I don't want to build a pot OOP with essentially air. Plan: check and see the river; fold to any meaningful bet on this street.

River is complete with board showing 6s 7c As 6h 2d. Minni has checked all streets, showing consistent weakness. My pair of fours is weak but likely ahead against her range given her passive play. I have A♦K♥ on the button — a premium hand with excellent playability and great equity vs a SB 3‑bet. Calling Minni's 4-bet with QQ. While 4-bets traditionally indicate premium hands (AA/KK), Minni's aggressive play pattern throughout the session suggests a wider 4-bet range. I am on the button with 8h 3c, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is a 3-handed game, 83o has very little playability and does not merit a raise. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I'm in the Big Blind with Tc Jd, a solid broadway hand. Minni called from the Small Blind rather than raising, suggesting a moderate hand. Flash folded preflop. The pot is 200.

I have middle pair (8♦6♣ on T♠8♣3♦) heads-up and am out of position. I'm in the Big Blind with Tc Jd on a flop of 3d Ts 8c. I have:

**Hand Strength:**
- Top pair (pair of Tens)
- Open-ended straight draw (any 9 or K makes a straight: 9-8-T-J or K-J-T-9)
- Two ways to improve to very strong hands on the turn

**Situation Analysis:**
- Pot: 300 | To Call: 100
- Pot odds: 3:1 on my call
- Minni (19.9k stack) raised the flop from 50 to 100, indicating moderate strength but not necessarily a premium hand
- I'm out of position (Minni acts first postflop), but my hand has excellent equity and implied odds

**Decision:**
Calling is the correct play. My top pair combined with an open-ended straight draw has strong equity against Minni's range. The pot odds are favorable, and I can evaluate the turn before committing further. If I hit my straight or improve, I'm in great shape. If I don't, I can exercise discipline on the turn. Raising would be aggressive out of position without clearly defined value, so a call is more prudent here.

I have middle pair (8♦6♣) but I’m out of position and the K♠ on the turn is a dangerous overcard that improves many parts of Claude’s flop-calling range (Kx, KT, strong TOP pairs). I bet the flop for thin value/protection, but the king makes this a pot-control decision — betting risks getting raised off my marginal hand or folding out only the worst hands. Checking keeps the pot to 400, lets me see Claude’s intent, and preserves the option to call small probes while folding to large turn aggression. If Claude checks back I can re-evaluate the river for a small value bet or a targeted bluff on a safe runout; if he bets large I will fold most of the time. Action: check to control the pot and avoid bloating with a single pair OOP.

Minni checked the turn after raising the flop, suggesting a marginal hand or a missed draw. With top pair (Tens with Jack kicker), I'm likely ahead. A turn bet of 250 applies pressure, takes down the pot if she's vulnerable, and builds value from weaker hands. I checked the turn to control the pot with a marginal hand (8♦6♣ — middle pair) and Claude responded by raising to 250. That sizing into the prior pot is consistent with a value-heavy range (Kx, Tx, sets, or better pairs) — the King on the turn likely improved many hands that called the flop. Pot math: after his bet the pot would be 650 and calling 250 would make the pot 900, so I need ~250/900 ≈ 27.8% equity to justify a call. My realistic improvement outs to beat a typical value holding are tiny: two remaining eights + three sixes = 5 outs → ~5/46 ≈ 10.9% chance to improve on the river. That’s well below the ~28% required, so calling is -EV unless Claude is bluffing an implausibly high frequency. Raising OOP with a single pair and a weak kicker is also poor — I’d have little fold equity and would face very difficult decisions if reraised. Given his tendencies and the math, the correct play is to fold and preserve a deep stack for better spots.

I’m on the button with 2♠6♠ — a weak but suited hand. Folding 3-K offsuit (weak hand) from small blind facing Minni's raise. This hand is vulnerable and out of position. The turn brings an Ace, completing the board as Td Ah 3h As. My hand is Jack-high with a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to any King, ~16% equity on river). Both opponents have now checked twice in succession on the flop and turn, indicating weakness. The pot remains at 300.

Board: T♦ A♥ 3♥ A♠ 5♥ — I have K♦4♥, nothing but K‑high. Flash leads the river for 200 into a 500 pot after everyone checked turn; that sizing is classic thin value from A‑x, T‑x (two pair), sets or a made heart flush (two hearts in hand), and there are many hands in his limping/passive line that beat me. A raise would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and risks getting raised/called by better hands (Claude still to act). This is a straightforward fold. I hold Jack-high (6s Jc) on a board of Td Ah 3h As 5h. Flash, who is critically short-stacked at 4,600 chips, has just raised to 200 on the river after both of us checked flop and turn.

Key considerations:
1. Hand strength: Jack-high is extremely weak. I lose to any pair, any ace, and need Flash to be bluffing with something like Queen-high or lower.

2. Pot odds: I need to call 200 to win 500, requiring roughly 29% equity to break even. With Jack-high, I'm likely winning less than 15-20% of the time.

3. Flash's action: A short-stacked player raising the river after two checks is unlikely to be bluffing with trash. This action pattern suggests Flash hit something on the river or improved with the turn card, not a desperation bluff with air.

4. Position doesn't help here: While position is valuable, it doesn't overcome the fundamental weakness of my hand. Checking down was correct, but now that Flash has bet, my hand has no showdown value.

This is a clear fold. Even against a wide range from a short-stacked player, Jack-high has insufficient equity and loses to too many hands that would raise in this spot. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. While ranges are wider in a three-handed game, this hand is significantly below the threshold for a standard opening raise. I'm in the big blind with 8h5h, which is a weak hand but has significant potential here. The flop is 9c As 2s, and Minni has raised to 100.

4. Position: I'm in the big blind, so I'll have position postflop, which is valuable for future streets.

This is a standard flush draw call with reasonable pot odds. I can fold if I miss badly on the turn or call down if I improve.

Strong semi-bluff with 11 flush outs (48% equity) on a turn where Minni has shown weakness by checking. Betting 250 applies pressure to win the pot now or build chips when called with a strong draw. I have J♣4♥ — top pair but a very weak kicker on A♠–9♣–2♠–J♦–2♦. Plan: fold to a large check-raise (represents 9x/sets/strong two-pair), call small aggression and reevaluate on the turn if called, and fire again only on safe runouts or for protection.

Calling the flop bet with Js-Td on 7c-5h-9s. I have approximately 25-30% equity with a gutshot to the nuts (any 8) and overcard value with the Jack. Facing 4:1 pot odds (1400:350), this call has positive expected value since I only need ~20% equity. Folding would be too tight, and raising is premature with this marginal holding out of position. Calling allows me to realize my equity while staying flexible for the turn.

Turn action with Js-Td on 7c-5h-9s-4c. I'm first to act out of position. My hand is marginal - essentially a gutshot straight draw (any 8 completes) with six main outs after accounting for blockers. The 4c on the turn didn't help my hand. Against Minni's likely range (she raised pre-flop from button and bet the flop), I have approximately 15-20% equity at best. Checking is the correct play here: (1) I'm out of position and can't credibly represent strength; (2) my hand is too weak to value bet; (3) if Minni bets, I can fold and minimize losses; (4) if she checks, I get a free river card to complete my draw. Betting would be applying reverse pressure - I'd be betting weak into an aggressive opponent in a spot where she can easily raise me off my equity. Checking keeps the pot manageable and preserves my flexibility.

I have top pair (7s) with a decent Jack kicker on a relatively dry turn (7c-5h-9s-4c). Claude called my flop c-bet, so his range is wide (weaker pairs, some 9x, 5x, floats, occasional pocket pairs or draws). The 4c is a safe card that doesn’t complete obvious straights or flushes, so this is a good spot to extract value and deny equity. I’ll bet ~50% pot (700 into 1400): big enough to get value from worse pairs, pocket 4s/5s, and floating hands, but small enough to keep the pot manageable and fold out pure air. Plan if raised: a sizable check-raise is likely strong (9x/sets/two pair) and I’ll give it up; I’ll call small resistance and re-evaluate on the river if called.

Calling 700 into a 2,100 pot. My Js-Td has roughly 13-14 outs on the river (4 eights for the nuts, 3 jacks for top pair, 3 tens for second pair), yielding approximately 27-30% equity. The pot odds are 4:1, requiring only ~20% equity to call, putting me slightly above break-even. While I'm out of position with a marginal hand against Minni's aggressive turn bet, the gutshot to the nuts combined with overcard outs and decent pot odds justify the call. If I hit an 8, I have an extremely strong hand capable of winning a large pot. The risk/reward profile is favorable enough to warrant exploration to the river.

River check with J♠T♦ on 7♣5♥9♠4♣4♦. I missed my gutshot straight draw and have only J-high with no made hand. The paired board and Minni's aggressive line through the hand (pre-flop raise, flop bet, turn raise) indicate she likely has a superior hand. Checking is the correct play: (1) I have no made hand to value bet; (2) Betting would be a weak bluff against an aggressive opponent with demonstrated strength; (3) Checking either reaches showdown cheaply if Minni checks, or allows me to fold if she bets and I can't beat her likely range. This controls pot size with a marginal holding.

River — board 7c 5h 9s 4c 4d. I hold J♥7♦ (top pair, decent kicker). Claude cold‑called my raise pre, called my ~50% pot c‑bet on the flop and my turn bet, then checked the paired river. His continuing range is heavy with hands that beat me (9x, overpairs like 88/TT/JJ, occasional sets/trips) plus some second pairs (5x) and floats. A value bet here would mostly get called by better hands and fold out the weaker pairs I want value from, so it’s thin/likely negative EV. With deep stacks and limited fold equity versus his passive line, pot control and showdown realization is the prudent line. I’ll check and take it to a showdown rather than bloating the pot or risking a big check‑raise I can’t comfortably continue against.

I'm on the button with 8c-4c in a three-handed game. While 8-4o is a weak hand, several factors support a call here: (1) I have positional advantage on both opponents post-flop, (2) the cost to call is minimal (100 chips, only 0.76% of my 13,100 stack), (3) Flash is short-stacked at 4,950 and may be playing tighter/more desperate, (4) I can use my button position and aggressive play to win pots post-flop with marginal holdings, (5) the pot odds are reasonable given the total pot size of 250. With position, I can often take down the pot with a continuation bet if the flop is favorable or if both opponents check. A fold seems too tight here given the circumstances.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has limped from the Button. While it only costs 50 more to call, 53 offsuit has very poor playability, especially out of position against two opponents. Additionally, Minni in the Big Blind has been aggressive throughout the session and may raise, forcing me to fold anyway. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I’m in the big blind with 3♠5♥ — a very weak, non‑playable hand out of position. Claude just limped; raising to 200 would be a pure isolation bluff with little fold equity against a likely wide limp/call range and would open us to playing a bloated pot OOP. Pot is only 250 and stacks are deep enough to get us in trouble post‑flop. Better line is to check, see a cheap flop, and only continue with a clear pair, a strong draw, or correct pot‑odds and fold to significant aggression. Checking preserves stack and keeps options open.

I have 3♠5♥ — no pair, no diamond, no straight draw. The flop Q♦ A♦ T♦ is extremely wet: any player holding two diamonds has an immediate made flush, and many single‑diamond hands have strong semi‑backdoor equity. Claude limped preflop, so his range is wide and likely contains lots of diamonds, Ax/Qx/Tx, and suited connectors that crush this board. Pot is tiny (250) relative to our deep stacks, so a c‑bet here is a thin bluff that will get called or raised too often and put me in a bloated OOP pot with virtually no equity. Best line is to check and fold to meaningful aggression; if Claude checks back I get a free turn to re‑evaluate. I check.

I'm holding 8c-4c, a very weak hand with no pair, no draw, and minimal equity on a coordinated Qd-Ad-Td flop. While I called pre-flop with positional advantage and intention to be aggressive, this flop is unfavorable for bluffing: it's all diamonds and broadway cards, suggesting opponents could have many hands that hit this board. Minni checked from the big blind, and Flash folded pre-flop, leaving us heads-up. With such a weak holding and no draw potential, betting would be -EV. By checking, I keep the pot small, see the turn card for free, and can easily fold if Minni bets. This is the prudent approach with a 0-equity hand.

I have 3♠5♥ on Q♦ A♦ T♦ J♣ — no pair, no diamond, no straight draw. Claude limped preflop and checked the flop, so his range is wide but contains lots of Ax/Qx/Tx, diamond flushes and broadway hands that crush this board. The turn only increases the number of hands that beat or have strong equity against me (two‑pair/sets, broadway straights with a K, and flushes). Pot is tiny (250) relative to our deep stacks, so betting here would be a thin bluff with very little fold equity and would commit me OOP with virtually no equity. Best play is pot control: check and fold to meaningful aggression, re‑evaluate only if checked to the river or an unusually small probe bet appears.

I'm holding 8c-4c, complete trash with zero equity on this board. The turn brought the Jc, creating a Qd-Ad-Td-Jc board that's heavily coordinated with broadway cards and diamonds. This is an extremely unfavorable runout for my weak hand. Minni has now checked both the flop and turn, which could indicate either weakness or a slow-play. Given the board texture and my non-existent hand strength, I have no basis for a bluff bet - I have no hand and no draw whatsoever. Checking allows me to see the river card for free without committing more chips with zero equity. This is a straightforward check with a complete air hand on a dangerous board. I'll rely on catching something on the river or will fold if Minni bets.

Board A♦ Q♦ T♦ J♣ 9♣, I hold 3♠5♥ — no pair, no diamond, no backdoor. I checked flop and turn and Claude also checked both streets after limping preflop, which indicates a fairly passive/wide checking range (medium pairs, Kx, Qx/Tx, missed hands and some weak A's). The pot is tiny (250) so a small river bluff is justified: if I bet B=100 the break‑even fold rate is 100/(250+100)=28.6%, which is a low threshold and likely exceeded against a passive checking range. I choose a minimum river stab to maximize fold equity while keeping risk small — if Claude raises I will let this bluff go; if he calls I’m almost certainly beaten but the loss is limited relative to stacks. Bet 100 to try to steal the pot.

I'm holding 8c-4c on a river board of Qd-Ad-Td-Jc-9c. This is complete trash with zero equity. I have no pair, no straight possibility (would need K-Q or 8-7), no flush (only two clubs visible and I'd need 5 cards in suit), and no meaningful draw whatsoever. Minni bet 100 on the river after checking both the flop and turn. While this could indicate weakness, it doesn't matter—I have nothing to beat with. Calling 100 chips hoping to win a pot with a completely worthless hand is negative EV. Even though the pot offers 3.5:1 odds (350 to call 100), I have zero equity, so any call is a mistake. This is a straightforward fold with a complete air hand against an opponent who has demonstrated some strength by reaching showdown and betting the river.

I am on the button with 2d 4c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of 49.5 big blinds and two active, large-stacked opponents in the blinds (Minni and Claude), attempting a steal with such a low-equity hand is unnecessary and likely unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up vs the BB with 6♠5♣ — a marginal but playable, connected hand. I don’t want to limp and play OOP for a cheap flop; raising takes the initiative, builds the pot when I hit, and often steals the blind. Make a standard small open to 200 (2×BB) — if Claude 3-bets big I can fold and preserve my deep stack; if called I’ll c-bet favorable low/connected flops and continue with my draws or pairs.

Calling the 100 chip bet from the big blind with Q7o. The 3:1 pot odds justify playing this hand against Minni's small blind raise, which is likely a fairly wide range. I have sufficient equity to call, and can make further decisions postflop based on board texture and action. Position is a disadvantage, but the favorable pot odds offset this concern.

I raised preflop and the flop is K♠-6♣-3♦. I hold 6♠5♣ — a lone pair of sixes with a weak kicker. This is a dry board and, as the preflop aggressor, a small-to-medium continuation bet is the right play to extract value from worse pocket pairs and marginal holdings and to deny free cards to overcards. Pot is 400; I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (200) to keep the pot controllable while getting called by worse. Plan: bet 200; if Claude raises big I’ll fold (I’m behind to Kx/sets), if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and reassess based on the card and his action.

Folding Q7o on the flop. This hand lacks sufficient equity against Minni's likely range after her aggressive pre-flop raise and flop bet. Queen-high on a King-high, fairly coordinated board is too weak, especially out of position. While pot odds are 3:1, the hand strength doesn't justify calling with a weak Queen-high when Minni's betting patterns suggest a strong holding (likely KK, KQ, AK, or similar). Preserving chips for better spots is the correct play.

Button with J♠9♣ in a three-handed pot — good spot to open. J9o has decent postflop playability and blocks some broadway combos; raising for fold equity and to seize initiative is standard. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x BB) — a size consistent with my previous opens that pressures the small blind and big blind without overcommitting. Limping/calling 100 is too passive (gives up initiative and allows cheaper flops), and the minimum 200 is too small (invites more calls). If I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on opponent and sizing (call or fold/4-bet as appropriate).

JQ is a premium hand in 3-handed play and I'm getting good pot odds (450 to 250, ~36%). Minni has been very active from the button throughout these hands, so his 300 raise could include a wide range. Flash is short-stacked in the big blind. I have position advantage over Flash and can play my reasonable holding in a multiway or heads-up pot on the flop. Calling allows me to see more cards while maintaining my chip stack integrity.

I have Ace-Ten of Spades in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Minni opened from the button and Claude called from the small blind. Both opponents have shown a tendency to play a wide range of hands. With a stack of 4850 (approx 48 BB), I want to take the initiative and thin the field. A 3-bet here puts pressure on the button's wide opening range and the small blind's capped calling range. I will raise to 1200 to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop.

I opened to 300 from the button with J♠9♣ and the BB shipped a 3-bet to 1,200 while the SB already has a call in. J9o is marginal vs a 3-bet but plays well in position and I’m getting roughly 3:1 pot odds (900 more to call into ~1,800) — break-even equity ~33%. Against the BB’s likely polarized 3‑bet range I’m near that threshold and I can use position to fold to turn/river aggression or extract value when I connect. I don’t want to 4‑bet here with J9o (reserve that for stronger hands or balanced bluffs), and folding is too nitty given the pot odds and postflop edge. I’ll call the 900 more (1200 total) and proceed cautiously on the flop; if I face a shove or very large 4‑bet I’ll fold.

JcQh is a strong hand in this spot. Current situation: pot is 2,700 and I need to call 900 for roughly 3:1 odds. Against likely ranges (Flash with short stack likely has a wide value range: pairs, broadway cards, AQ/AK; Minni just called so he has a medium hand), JQ has approximately 40-42% equity. With positive expected value (needing only 25% equity to break even), a call is mathematically sound. I maintain flexibility post-flop with a 12,500 stack that's not under immediate pressure. Flash's short stack (3,750) and aggressive line suggest the hand might resolve quickly with his likely all-in commitment soon. This is a standard profitable call with position and reasonable equity.

I hold JcQh on a flop of Qc3c9s, giving me a strong pair of Queens. This is likely the best hand currently against both opponents' ranges. The board is relatively dry with limited draws that can dominate me. I'm out of position relative to Minni but have acted first with information advantage. A bet of 1,800 (roughly 50% pot) is appropriate here to: (1) extract value from weaker holdings like AK, AQ, or smaller pairs that Minni might have called with preflop, (2) apply pressure to Flash's short stack (3,750), potentially committing him to the hand, and (3) define my hand strength while maintaining aggression. The dry flop favors not checking through and giving free cards. With my 11,600 stack, I can afford this bet comfortably while leaving room for further action on turn/river.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Ten of Spades to take control of the pot. The flop of Qc-3c-9s is not favorable for my hand, as it contains a Queen and a potential flush draw. Claude, who called my 3-bet from the small blind, has led out for 1800 into the 3600 pre-flop pot (donk-betting). This action usually indicates a strong hand like top pair, a set, or a very strong draw. With only Ace-high and a backdoor spade draw, and with Minni still to act behind me, I do not have sufficient equity to justify a call or a raise. Preserving my remaining 37.5 BB stack is the more prudent play here.

Board Qc 3c 9s, I hold J♠9♣ — second pair (nines) with a decent Jack kicker and I'm in position. Claude (SB) bets 1,800 into ~5,400 (~1/3 pot). Calling 1,800 here requires ~20% equity, which my hand comfortably exceeds against a lot of his plausible continuing range (medium pairs, some Qx, suited connectors and bluffs). There are two clubs on board so he can have club draws (if he holds two clubs) or strong Qx/sets, but raising for thin value is risky — many of his value hands still beat me and a raise could commit me unnecessarily or fold out worse hands. Folding is too nitty given the pot odds and positional advantage. I prefer a pot-control call to realize equity and see a turn: if I improve (J or 9) or get a favorable texture I can be aggressive; if I miss and face large turn aggression I can fold.

The 7d is a blank that doesn't improve Minni's likely range. I have top pair with a quality kicker (JQ on a Q-high board) and likely hold the best hand. Minni's flop call suggests a medium-strength holding rather than a monster hand. Continuing to apply pressure with a 3500 bet (48% of pot) extracts value from weaker hands while maintaining an aggressive, confident line. This sizing allows me to follow through to the river if needed while keeping my remaining stack (6300) in reasonable proportion to the pot for final decisions.

I have second pair (9s) with a decent Jack kicker and I’m in position. The pot is 10,700 and Claude bet 3,500; calling requires ~24.6% equity (3,500 / (10,700+3,500)). Against Claude’s plausible continuing range after calling a preflop 3‑bet and cbetting the flop (Qx, mid pocket pairs, some 9x, occasional clubs and bluffs), J9 has sufficient equity and plays well in position. Raising to the 7,000 minimum would be an overcommitment here (it polarizes my range, can induce a shove I don’t want to face, and folds out worse hands that I’d like to keep to realize equity). Folding is too passive given the pot odds and my position. I’ll call 3,500 more (total committed this hand becomes 6,500), see the river, and then decide — if I improve I’ll extract value; if I miss and face heavy river aggression I’ll fold unless pot odds or reads justify a call.

River decision with JQ on board Qc3c9s7d6h. I hold top pair with a solid kicker after an aggressive line preflop through turn. Current analysis: (1) Pot is 14,200, I have 6,300 remaining; (2) Minni's calling pattern (called 900 preflop, 1800 flop, 3500 turn) suggests he holds either a Queen with decent kicker (AQ/KQ/QT/Q9 range) or possibly a set, but more likely a marginal/mid-strength Queen; (3) The board run-out (7d then 6h) didn't significantly help most ranges - it's relatively dry; (4) I've established a strong aggressive image throughout this hand and the session, so my betting line is credible. A bet of 3,500 (approximately 25% pot) serves as a value bet: it extracts value from weaker Queens in his range while keeping 2,800 in reserve to make fold/call decisions if he raises. Against his likely holding distribution, this bet has positive expected value - I win immediately if he folds medium-strength hands, and I get called by worse Queens or split/lose to better Queens but maintain fold equity and positional discipline. Checking risks giving a free card and missing value from hands like QJ/QT that would likely call a reasonable bet.

River: Qc 3c 9s 7d 6h. I hold J♠9♣ — second pair (nines) with a decent jack kicker and I’m in position. Claude (called a 3‑bet preflop and barreled flop and turn) can have a continuing range of Qx, mid pockets/sets, or missed club draws/thin bluffs. The pot is 17,700 and calling 3,500 requires ≈16.5% equity, which my hand comfortably exceeds against a realistic mix of his value/bluff combos. Raising would over‑polarize my range, risk folding out worse hands I beat, and could get me committed against hands that already beat me. Folding is too cheap given the pot odds and board texture. I’ll call the 3,500 more to reach showdown (total committed this hand becomes 10,000).

With A2o on the button, I'm in position against two opponents. Flash (SB) has a very short stack of 3700, and Minni (BB) has 12150. 

A2o is a marginal hand, but my position is valuable. Raising here isn't ideal with this weak hand - if I raise, Flash's short stack might force an all-in situation where I'd need to fold, and I don't want to build a large pot with A2o out of position post-flop against Minni.

The best play is to call cheaply and use my button position post-flop. I can:
1. See a cheap flop with position
2. Attack with continuation bets if both opponents miss
3. Fold easily if the board is dangerous
4. Potentially improve to top pair (any Ace) or bottom pair

This is a standard position play - utilize the button advantage and play the flop in position.

I am holding T9s in the Small Blind. Claude has limped from the Button, and the Big Blind (Minni) is yet to act. T9s is a strong speculative hand that performs well in multiway pots. With a stack of 3,700 (37BB), I want to see a flop cheaply rather than bloating the pot out of position against two deep-stacked, active opponents. Calling the 50 to complete the bet is the most standard and flexible play here.

Two limpers (SB and BTN) have just called and the pot is 300. In the big blind with T♦Q♠ this is a clear spot to take the initiative and avoid a multiway limp-pot where my hand plays poorly. I want to isolate heads-up or charge speculative hands, so I’ll raise to a size that gives fold equity but keeps stacks manageable. Flash (SB) is short (~3650) and may shove; TQ has reasonable equity vs that wide shove range. If Claude (BTN) 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a big 3-bet, continue vs a small one). Postflop plan: continuation bet on many dry/A-high flops, proceed cautiously on highly connected/flushy boards. Raising to 1200 total accomplishes these goals.

A2o is too weak to call a 1200 raise. While I initially called hoping to play position post-flop cheaply, Minni's raise changes the equation. My hand has minimal equity (~30-35%) and I'm only getting 2.27:1 odds when I need closer to 2:1 for break-even. A2o's weakness is compounded by the fact that even hitting an ace leaves me vulnerable to better kickers. With a deep stack and many hands remaining, I can afford to fold weak hands and wait for premium opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with T9s. I originally called the limp, but Minni (Big Blind) has now put in a very large squeeze to 1,200, which is nearly a third of my remaining stack (3,650). While T9s is a good hand, calling off 1,100 more out of position against a player who has shown significant aggression in previous hands (like Hand #37) is mathematically poor. I would be playing for my stack with a speculative hand and likely behind a range of premium hands or big broadways. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I have been dealt pocket Kings (Ks Kc) on the button, which is the second strongest starting hand in Hold'em. With a stack of 36.5 big blinds, my objective is to build the pot and look for an opportunity to get all-in. A standard 3x open raise is effective here to extract value and potentially induce a three-bet from the blinds, especially given Minni's aggressive history in previous hands.

I’m in the SB with A♠6♦ (ace-high, weak kicker) facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250). Flash (button) has a relatively short stack (3,350) and Claude (BB) is deep and still to act. Folding an Ace to a 3bb open from SB is too tight — I want to realize equity and see a flop. 3-betting is unattractive: a small 3-bet (min to 500) is weak and will still leave me OOP vs both players and could induce a shove from the short-stacked button or a cold 4-bet from the big stack; I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal A6o. Calling 250 (total 300) is the best line — see a flop cheaply, keep options, and fold to heavy action if I miss or proceed if I hit top pair or a good draw.

7-4o is one of the worst starting hands in poker. I'm facing a button raise from Flash (3350 stack, short) and a small blind call from Minni (12150 stack). While the pot odds are 3.5:1 (200 to call into 700), my equity against two opponents is likely only 20-25%. With a premium stack (23800), folding weak hands to multi-way action is the correct long-term strategy. This hand has minimal showdown value and poor post-flop playability. I'll preserve my chip lead and wait for better spots.

Check. I’m OOP with A♥6♦ on Q♥ K♦ 2♠ — a dry flop where my hand has only ace-high and a very slim backdoor heart/future pair equity (three A outs). Pot is 700 and Flash is relatively short (~33bb). Leading here as the preflop caller bloats the pot and won’t fold many of the hands that beat me (Kx/Qx/pairs); checking controls the pot, allows me to realize equity, and lets the short-stacked opponent commit bluffs if they choose. Plan: check — if Button checks back I get a free turn; if he makes a small probe (roughly ≤1/3–1/2 pot) I’ll call to see a cheap turn; if he bets large or shoves (big portion of his 3,350) I’ll fold, since my ace-high won’t have enough equity against a value-heavy c-bet range.

I have flopped top set with pocket Kings on a very favorable board (K-Q-2 rainbow). This is an exceptionally strong hand, and my goal is to extract maximum value from Minni's range, which could include hands like KQ, KJ, KT, or various Queens. Since Minni checked, I will lead with a value bet of approximately 65% of the pot to build the pot toward a potential shove on later streets.

Fold. I checked the flop with A♣6♦ (A♥6♦) to control the pot, but Flash’s bet of 450 into a 700 pot (~64% pot) is too large for my bare ace-high. To call 450 into a 1150 pot I need ~39% equity. I only have ~6 clean outs (3 aces + 3 sixes) to pair by the river, which is roughly a 24% chance, and many of those pairs still lose to Kx/Qx or sets. There is no realistic backdoor flush/straight draw to justify calling or bluff-raising OOP, and a raise would be risky OOP against a relatively short, aggressive opener. Folding preserves my stack and waits for a better spot.

On the button with 9♠8♠ in a 3-handed pot this is a prime spot to open-raise and take initiative. A standard 3x open to 300 gives me fold equity against the small blind, keeps the pot controllable versus Flash (≈39bb) and lets me play postflop in position where 98s has good straight/flush equity. Calling 100 loses initiative and invites multiway pots out of position; a much larger raise risks getting committed to a marginal spot against Claude's deeper stack or inducing a shove from Flash. I’ll raise to 300 to isolate/take the lead and maximize long-term EV.

Q-2o is a weak hand in the small blind. While Minni has shown significant aggression from the button throughout the session, Q-2 has minimal playability. Even against a loose raiser, this hand is unprofitable to defend in the SB. The pot odds (450:250 = 1.8:1) don't justify calling with such a marginal holding. Folding preserves my substantial chip stack and keeps me focused on high-equity spots. Flash still needs to act behind me, and there's limited upside to playing this hand out of position.

I am holding K9s in the Big Blind, which is a very strong defending hand against a 3x raise from an active button player like Minni. My stack is roughly 40 BBs. While I could 3-bet to put pressure on Minni, calling is a solid and standard play that allows me to see a flop and utilize the post-flop playability of my suited King. Folding would be much too tight given the hand strength and position.

On a board of Ah-6h-6c, the Ace favors the pre-flop raiser (Minni). My King-high has very little showdown value and doesn't warrant a lead-out bet. I will check to the aggressor and see how they respond. If they bet small, I might consider a float if I pick up a club on the turn, but for now, checking is the standard and most disciplined play.

I was the preflop aggressor heads-up, so a continuation bet is the standard line to take the initiative and fold out many of Flash's missed hands. The flop (A‑6‑6 rainbow) does hit his likely defend/call range (lots of Ax and 6x), so I don't want to overcommit — a small c-bet preserves fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (200). If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and generally exercise pot control unless I pair up (9/8) or see a very favorable runout. If he check‑raises or shoves I’ll fold this airball to a strong line (he can credibly have an Ace or a six).

The flop of Ah-6h-6c is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser, Minni, who is likely to have many Ace-X combinations in her range. My hand, Kc 9c, has virtually no equity on this texture, possessing only a thin backdoor club draw and no made hand. While Minni's bet of 200 is small (less than 1/3 of the pot), calling out of position with King-high and no real draws is a losing play against an aggressive and sticky opponent. I will fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

8-7 offsuit is a marginal hand that falls below my threshold for playing from the button, especially against two opponents where I'd need to navigate postflop complexity. While position is favorable, the hand strength doesn't warrant calling 100 chips with two active opponents still to act. Flash (SB) is short-stacked at 3,700, which increases the likelihood of aggressive action, and Minni (BB) has shown consistent aggression throughout the session. Better to preserve chips and wait for stronger opportunities, especially given I have a healthy 23,750 stack with 109 hands remaining.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c2c. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). Minni has shown significant aggression throughout the match, frequently raising pre-flop and contesting pots. While 62s has some potential, playing out of position with a 37 BB stack against an aggressive opponent with a much larger stack is suboptimal. Folding here is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-Five offsuit.
