While King-high hands are often candidates for opening in a three-handed game, both Minni and Claude have shown significant aggression and a willingness to defend their blinds or 3-bet. With my stack being the shortest at 37 big blinds, I prefer to wait for a stronger hand rather than risk being outplayed out of position or facing a 3-bet from the larger stacks.

I’m in the small blind with Q♦8♠ heads-up vs Claude (BB). The pot is 150 and it’s only 50 more to see a flop. Q8o is a marginal, often-dominated hand and I’ll be out of position, so I don’t want to open-raise and inflate the pot against a deep, competent opponent. Calling 50 (100 total committed) is a cheap way to see a flop, keep pot control, and evaluate. Postflop plan: proceed cautiously OOP — check and fold to heavy aggression unless I improve, c-bet small on very dry boards if checked to, and look to extract value only when I make a clear pair or better.

K9o is a weak hand that benefits from keeping the pot small and leveraging positional advantage. By checking, I avoid building a pot with a marginal hand and retain flexibility to play the flop in position. Minni's aggressive tendencies make postflop positional advantage valuable. This is a standard BB defense play that keeps the hand simple.

I’m OOP with Qd8s on a relatively dry Kc-9h-2h flop and it’s checked to me (pot = 200). My hand has very little showdown value so I want to use some fold equity rather than just giving a free turn card. I’ll make a small continuation bet (half-pot) to pressure unpaired hands and win the pot now when Claude folds. Size: 100 keeps the pot manageable if called. If Claude raises I’ll fold my weak Q-high OOP; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue with a made pair or clear improvement.

I have two pair (Kings and Nines) on the flop, which is a very strong hand. Minni raised to 100 from the small blind. I should raise here to build the pot and extract value. A raise to 300 (betting 200 more to call) is appropriately sized - not over-committing but building the pot with a premium holding. This capitalizes on Minni's aggressive tendency and takes a strong positional and equity advantage.

I c-bet 100 as planned on the dry Kc-9h-2h flop to apply pressure. Claude’s raise to 300 is a clear sign of strength (likely Kx, 9x, or other strong holdings) and I hold Q8o with no pair, no straight draw, and no hearts — essentially zero real equity and lots of domination. Calling 200 to win 800 requires ~25% equity, which I don’t have versus this raising range, and raising myself would be too speculative OOP. I’ll fold and preserve my 12,350 stack for better spots.

8♦5♠ is a weak offsuit hand, but I'm on the button and can see a flop cheaply. Folding would be too passive for just 100 into a 150 pot; a raise to 200 risks a 3‑bet shove from Flash (only ~36BB) or a tough multiway pot against Claude. I'll call 100 to use position post‑flop, fold to heavy preflop aggression, and look for cheap realizable equity (pairs, straights) or opportunities to steal later streets.

I'm in the small blind with K2o against Minni (button) who called 100, and Flash (big blind) with only 3,600. While I only need to call 50 more chips, K2 is one of the weakest hands in poker. It doesn't develop well post-flop and will be out of position against Flash when the big blind acts. Even though the immediate cost is minimal, K2 is a chronic losing hand that should be folded from the small blind. My stack is strong at 23,900, so I can afford to be selective. Folding weak hands is more important than chasing small pots with marginal holdings.

I am in the big blind with 4d 2h. Minni just called from the button, and Claude folded the small blind. Since there is no further cost to see the flop, I will check to see if I can hit something on the board with this weak hand rather than folding or attempting a risky bluff raise out of position.

I have bottom pair on an Ace-high board with a diamond flush draw. Given I am out of position against a button limper, checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. My hand is weak and unlikely to survive significant pressure, but it has some showdown value if the hand remains small.

Flop: A♦ 6♥ 2♦ — I hold 8♦5♠. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond runner-runner (~4% to make a flush by river). Flash checked the flop after checking preflop, so his range is wide (missed broadways, small pairs, some Ax). Heads‑up on an A‑high board my preflop caller range misses here frequently, so a continuation bet can fold out the majority of his missed hands and win the pot now. I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand or bluff too big against a relatively short stack who can re‑raise or shove, so I’ll make a medium-sized c‑bet (~60% pot) to generate fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue only if I pick up meaningful equity or get a favorable card); if he raises big or shoves I will fold most of the time since my hand has very little showdown value.

I have bottom pair (deuces) on an Ace-high board with a weak kicker. Minni's lead of 150 into a 250 pot in a limped blind-on-blind/button scenario is a standard continuation attempt. Given the pot odds and the fact that it is a limped pot where ranges are wide, I will call one street to see if I improve to two pair or trips on the turn, or if Minni checks back the next street. I will evaluate the turn carefully and likely fold to a second barrel if I do not improve.

I have bottom pair (deuces) and a weak diamond draw on a board where the flush has now completed. After calling a bet on the flop, the turn card (5d) is a scare card that potentially improves Minni's range. I will check to see how Minni acts; betting here serves no purpose as I won't get better hands to fold and worse hands are unlikely to call. I'm looking to reach a cheap showdown or fold if facing a significant bet.

I currently have a marginal made hand: paired fives (5♠ + 5♦ on board) plus an 8♦ giving me a four‑card diamond draw. That’s roughly 11 clean-ish outs (9 diamonds + 2 fives) so ~23–25% to improve to flush/trips by the river, but right now I’m behind many hands in Flash’s calling range (A‑x, middling pairs, some 6x/sets, etc.). Flash called my flop c‑bet, then checked the turn — likely pot‑control with a made hand or a missed draw. The pot is already meaningful (550) and Flash is relatively short (~34BB), so betting here risks getting shoved on by better hands and forcing tough folds or expensive calls. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize my equity and gives me the option to call a small turn shove or smaller river bets. Plan: check to keep the pot manageable. If Flash bets a small amount (≈≤200) I’ll call with my pair+draw; I’ll fold to large bets/shoves that require too much equity. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river — bet for value only if I improve (flush/trips) or if the card is favorable to bluff‑catch; otherwise check back to a showdown.

I have bottom pair (deuces) on a board with an Ace and a potential flush. The turn was checked through by Minni after she bet the flop, which suggests she might not have a strong Ace or a flush. However, my hand is extremely weak and has very little showdown value. Betting would likely only get called by better hands and won't force many better hands to fold in a small, limped pot. I will check and hope to reach a cheap showdown or fold if facing any significant bet.

Board: 6♥ A♦ 2♦ 5♦ Jc. I hold 8♦5♠ — a single pair (fives) with no flush (missed on the river). Flash called my flop c‑bet, then checked the turn and checked the river. Given the line, his range is likely dominated by Ax, small pairs (6x/2x), and some missed broadways; many of those hands beat my pair. The pot is only 550 and Flash is relatively short (≈34BB), so any river bet risks getting called or shoved by better hands while only folding a subset of worse hands. I get little thin‑value and limited fold equity here, so the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV play is to check to showdown and preserve my stack. If Flash had bet a small amount I’d consider a call, but absent a river bet I’ll check.

6-4 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I have position as the button, this hand doesn't warrant involvement against two opponents with reasonable stacks (Flash has 3,400 and Minni has 12,550). The risk-reward isn't favorable - if I call the 100 and the pot goes to the flop with two opponents, I'm likely in a difficult position with a weak hand. Even if I try to steal with a raise, I'm playing a marginal hand out of position relative to the potential action, and given my healthy stack (23,900), I can afford to wait for better spots. Folding preserves chips for premium situations.

I am in the small blind with 9h 3c, a very weak hand. Claude has folded, leaving only me and Minni in the big blind. While it only costs 50 more to complete the bet, 93o performs poorly post-flop and lacks sufficient equity to justify a call or a raise from this position, especially given my relatively short stack of 34BB compared to the others. I will fold to conserve chips for a more advantageous starting hand.

AJ offsuit is a premium hand in a 3-handed game, particularly from the button. With a stack of 3400 (34BB), I want to open for a standard raise to take control of the pot and potentially thin the field or take the blinds. I will raise to 300.

Holding 2♦7♦ in the small blind versus a Button open to 300 (250 to call into a 450 pot). 2-7 offsuit is the worst hand — no playability, terrible equity and I’ll be out of position postflop. Calling here gives poor pot odds and invites aggression from the opener and the big blind; a 3-bet bluff is too risky and unnecessary with no blockers or fold equity. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

With 3h-Qs, I'm holding a very weak hand in the big blind. While Flash is short-stacked at 3,100 chips (roughly 10 big blinds) and may be raising wide from the button, Q-3 offsuit is still a marginal hand. 

I need to consider:
- My hand is below average even against a wide range
- Flash's 3,100 stack suggests they're in desperation/push-fold territory, but that doesn't make my hand strong enough to profitably engage
- I have a significant chip advantage (23,800 vs 3,100) and don't need to take marginal spots
- Q-3 will struggle even post-flop against most of Flash's reasonable holdings
- My position as BB is disadvantageous on later streets despite my chip lead

While I'm getting reasonable odds (4.75:1), Q-3 is simply not strong enough to justify a call. I should preserve my strong chip position and wait for better opportunities. Tight play early in a 150-hand session makes sense when I'm already ahead.

I’m on the button with 7♣5♠ (offsuit, marginal). In a 3‑handed pot the button should open very wide — position is a big advantage. Raising generates fold equity and lets me play postflop in position; simply calling 100 is too passive and invites multiway pots, while a min‑raise to 200 is too small and gives the BB good odds. I’ll open to a standard 3x (300) which is big enough to steal the blinds often but not so big that I’m committed with a weak hand. If I face a large 3‑bet (or shove) I’ll fold; if called I’ll proceed cautiously — c‑bet good flops, check/heal or fold dry boards, and avoid bloating the pot with this holding.

Raising to 850 total with AJ from small blind against Minni's button raise. This is a premium hand with strong equity against her wide opening range. The aggressive 3-bet applies pressure, builds value, and takes the initiative. Minni's aggressive profile suggests she's opening a wide range, making this a profitable spot to leverage a strong hand.

I am in the big blind with 6h 7d. Minni (button) raised to 300, and Claude (small blind) 3-bet to 850. I have a stack of only 3450 chips, and both opponents have significantly larger stacks. Calling 750 (nearly 22% of my stack) with a weak unsuited hand like 67 offsuit out of position against two aggressive opponents is a losing play. Claude's 3-bet from the small blind indicates a strong range, and Minni may also choose to 4-bet or call. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my remaining chips.

Claude 3‑bet to 850 after my button open to 300. 7♥5♠ is marginal, but I have position and the 3‑bet size is only moderate. Calling costs me 550 more (total 850) into a ~1250 pot, so I’m getting about 30.6% pot‑odds — and the call is a small portion of my 12,350 stack. A 4‑bet bluff to 1400 is thin and folding gives up position and initiative too cheaply. I’ll call to see a flop and use position to proceed: continue with any pair, two‑pair, good draws or very favorable boards; check/fold dry misses to heavy action; fold to a shove or very large pressure postflop.

Betting 1000 on the flop with AJ on a 6-K-A board. This is a value bet that builds the pot with my strong hand (top pair, best kicker). The bet size is reasonable relative to the 1800 pot and applies pressure while maintaining good pot odds. I established aggression pre-flop with the 3-bet, and this continuation bet extends that narrative while extracting value from Minni's likely range of worse Aces, Kings, and middling pairs. The bet sizing avoids overcommitting while keeping all options open for turn and river play.

I opened wide preflop and called Claude’s 3‑bet to see the flop, but this A‑K‑6♠ flop completely misses 7♥5♠. I have no pair and only backdoor (turn+river) straight/flush outs — effectively negligible immediate equity. Claude 3‑bet preflop and is betting 1,000 into a ~2,800 pot, so a call requires roughly 26% equity (1000/(2800+1000)), which 7‑5 offsuit doesn't have versus his likely A/K‑heavy range. A bluff raise is thin — he can call or shove with many hands that beat me. My plan was to check/fold dry boards, so I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Q-6 offsuit is a weak hand that falls below my opening standards even from the button. With a chip lead and a tight table image, I should fold marginal hands and wait for premium holdings. This preserves my image for maximum value when I do have strong hands. The risk/reward does not justify playing this weak holding out of position for the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with K8s after the Button has folded. K8s is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to either take down the blinds or play a pot with a range advantage against the Big Blind.

I’m in the BB with 2♣3♠ facing a 300 open from the SB (Flash) in a heads-up pot. The pot is 400 and I can call 200 (total commit to 300). 2-3o is weak but heads-up vs a SB open it has some playability and the pot odds are favorable (200 to win ~600, >3:1). Flash is short-stacked (~3150) and likely to shove over a 3-bet, so I don’t want to bloat the pot or force myself to call a large all-in with junk. I’ll call to see a flop cheaply, keep the pot manageable, and fold to a hostile shove or proceed cautiously OOP postflop.

I have top pair with an eight kicker on a King-high board that also offers a diamond flush draw (Kd8d on Kh Td 6d). This is a very strong hand for a head-up pot against the Big Blind. I will lead out with a continuation bet of about half the pot (300) to get value from weaker Kings, Ten-x hands, and diamond draws, while also protecting my equity.

I called preflop to see the flop, but on K♥ T♦ 6♦ with 2♣3♠ I have no pair, no diamond, and only six clean outs to make a pair. Flash bets 300 into a 900 pot — calling 300 only gives me ~3:1 pot odds (need >25% equity). My chance to hit a pair by river is ~24.5%, just below break-even, and even if I pair I’m often second-best to K/T/6 or better hands in his raising range. Flash is short-stacked and likely to shove or apply pressure on later streets, which would force large calls with a marginal hand OOP. Raising as a bluff risks committing chips unnecessarily. Given marginal equity, bad position, and risk of facing a shove, the correct +EV line is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I have been dealt pocket tens (Th, Tc) on the button. This is a premium pocket pair, especially in a three-handed game. My stack is relatively short (37.5 big blinds) compared to my opponents. Pocket tens are strong enough to raise for value and to seize the initiative. I will make a standard open-raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds or isolate one opponent.

I call to 300 total (putting in 250 more) with K♠8♦. Button opened to 300 (a standard 3bb open) and my hand has decent equity and a high-card blocker (the King) so it’s worth defending from the small blind. A min-3bet to 500 with K8o is unattractive: it’s too small to generate meaningful fold equity and will leave me playing a bigger pot out of position, while a larger 3bet would be a pure bluff and needlessly commit chips OOP. Claude in the big blind often defends, so this can easily go multiway and that further reduces the value of a 3bet. The pot odds to call are reasonable, and I can realize equity or proceed cautiously postflop — play aggressively heads-up vs the opener with K‑high or top pair, but be prepared to check/call or fold to heavy action in multiway spots. Preserving stack and seeing a flop with a playable King is the optimal line here.

I'm in the big blind with Q4o - a very weak hand. Flash raised to 300 from the button and Minni called from the small blind. While I only need to call 200 more, this hand is fundamentally weak and plays poorly out of position against two opponents who have already shown strength. Q4o has poor equity against Flash's raising range and will be difficult to play profitably post-flop, especially against Minni's call. With a solid 24,650 chip stack and only on hand 48 of 150, it's prudent to fold weak holdings and wait for better opportunities in position or with stronger hands. Maintaining discipline in early game is crucial for long-term success.

Flop is 4♥ A♣ Q♥ and I hold K♠8♦ — only King-high, no heart, and behind any A or Q (and many of BTN’s continuing hands). Flash opened from the button (wide range) and will c‑bet this board a lot, but I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Donk-betting here has limited fold equity and turns the pot into one I can’t comfortably play OOP; the correct line is to check and control the pot. Plan: check and call a small-to-medium c-bet to float and see a turn (or fold to a large raise/shove that’s likely two-pair/set), then only escalate if I improve (e.g. pair my K) or get a clear read.

I hold pocket tens (Th, Tc) on a board of Ah-Qh-4h. My hand is currently a medium pair, but I also have a heart flush draw. This board is quite dangerous as it hits the ranges of both the small blind and the big blind, especially given that Minni called my pre-flop raise. There are two overcards (Ace and Queen) which are very much in a typical calling range. Minni has checked to me. While checking back is a safe option to see a free turn, betting allows me to protect my hand against random cards and potentially realize equity if a heart comes. However, given the presence of two overcards, a check-back is the most prudent play here to control the pot size and keep the range wide. I can evaluate on the turn based on the next card.

I’m OOP with K♠8♦ on 4♥ A♣ Q♥ 6♠ after calling preflop and checking the flop. My hand has essentially no showdown value unless I pair my King; I have no heart to block the two-heart flop and no meaningful draws. Flash (BTN) checked the flop and turn, which narrows his continuing range toward many missed hands and medium pairs but still includes A/Q hands that beat me. Betting the turn would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity (the min bet is tiny and a larger sizing just bloats the pot out of position), and with Flash’s ~3.5k stack he can apply real shove pressure if I commit chips now. Pot control is the +EV line: check and see the river. Plan: if I pair my K on the river I can value bet; if he bets the river I’ll evaluate by sizing (call small probes, fold to big shoves unless I improve); if he checks back we can consider a river bluff only if the card credibly improves a bluffing story. Preserve chips and avoid creating a bloated OOP pot with a marginal hand.

On the turn, the 6s is a relatively blank card. I have pocket tens with the Ten of Hearts, giving me a middle pair and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack), along with a flush draw. After checking the flop, checking back the turn is a solid strategy to reach showdown cheaply or realize my equity on the river. Betting now might fold out hands I beat, but it's unlikely to fold out an Ace or a Queen. Since Minni checked again, I will check behind to keep the pot small and potentially catch a heart or a ten on the river.

Board is 4♥ A♣ Q♥ 6♠ 2♦ and I hold K♠8♦ — I never improved and have only K-high. BTN opened pre and checked both flop and turn, narrowing his range to Ax, Qx, small-to-medium pairs, or missed broadways. Betting as a river bluff OOP has very thin fold equity here and will often be called by worse or simply fold only the pure air hands; creating a larger pot with a marginal hand is unnecessarily risky. Pot control and stack preservation are the +EV lines: take the free showdown. Therefore I check and go to showdown rather than bluffing.

I have pocket tens on a board of 4h-Ac-Qh-6s-2d. After raising pre-flop and both players checking the flop and turn, the river is a blank 2d. Minni has checked for the third time. My pocket tens have significant showdown value against her range, which likely consists of mid-pairs (55, 77-99), small suited connectors that missed, or weak kings. There is little value in betting as most hands that would call me (like an Ace or Queen she might have been trapping with) have me beaten, and most hands I beat will fold. I will check back to see the showdown and take the pot if my tens are good.

Q2s is a weak hand but suited queen has some playability and, crucially, I'm on the button in a 3-handed pot — great position and a spot to open a wide stealing range. Table dynamics show standard button opens around 300; raising to 300 gives fold equity vs both blinds while keeping the pot manageable if I'm called. Flash (BB) is a shorter stack and may fold or shove; Claude (SB) is deep and capable of a big 3-bet, in which case I'll respect it and fold this marginal holding. If called, I'll play postflop in position, c-betting selective textures and pot-controlling when I miss. Raise to 300 to leverage position and fold equity rather than just limping or calling.

A7o (Ace-Seven offsuit) is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Despite having ace-high, the seven kicker is weak and vulnerable to domination. I'm in the small blind facing Minni's raise from the button - she's been aggressive throughout the session. Even with a decent 24,600 chip stack, this isn't a spot to get involved without a stronger hand. Calling 250 more puts me in a difficult out-of-position situation post-flop, and the hand doesn't warrant a 3-bet. There will be better opportunities to accumulate chips. Folding preserves chips for premium situations where I can leverage my stack advantage more effectively.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind from the button. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a 450 pot), Jack-Six offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Given my relatively short stack of 40.5 BB, I should avoid defending with marginal hands that are easily dominated. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Pocket fives from the button is a solid starting hand with excellent position. Key factors: (1) I'm in the strongest position pre-flop, (2) Flash is short-stacked at 4,000 and may need to make a play, (3) Minni is a frequent raiser but I can apply pressure with position. Small pocket pairs play best in heads-up or 3-way pots with strong position. A raise accomplishes multiple objectives: isolates the short stack, narrows the field, and gives me positional advantage post-flop. The pot is minimal at 150 and I have a healthy 24,600 stack. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate aggression for this situation—not overcommitting but taking the lead with a playable hand from premium position.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-6 offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 300. While Claude has been playing very aggressively and opening wide, my hand is too weak to defend out of position, especially with a 40bb stack. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without the initiative or a strong hand. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Claude opened to 300 from the button (a standard 3x raise). I’m in the big blind out of position with Q♦4♠ — an off‑suit, poorly-connected hand that will be dominated by a lot of Claude’s opening range and has very limited post‑flop playability. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (≈24% equity needed), but even if I meet the pot odds the hand is hard to play multi‑street OOP and I’ll face frequent continuation bets and tough decisions. A light 3‑bet here would be too thin and likely to be called or 4‑bet by stronger hands. I’ll conserve chips and fold to avoid playing a marginal, dominated hand out of position.

I am on the button with 7s3c, which is a very weak starting hand. The pot is currently 150 and it costs me 100 to call, but there is little reason to play this hand from any position, even with the positional advantage. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Heads-up from the small blind with pocket eights is a strong, value hand — I want to build the pot and avoid giving the big blind the cheap odds to see flops with speculative hands. A standard 3xBB open (raise to 300 total) is the right sizing here: larger than the min-raise so it folds out some junk and reduces free equity, but small enough to keep postflop maneuverability with deep stacks. If Claude calls I’ll have a healthy SPR to play for value or set-mining; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies (likely fold to a large 3-bet, call smaller 3-bets). Raising also fits my table image (I’ve been opening to ~300) and keeps pressure on his wide calling range. I therefore raise to 300 (total).

Folding Q4o from the big blind. This is a weak hand with poor playability. While I'm getting 2:1 odds to call 200 more into a 400 chip pot, Q4 unsuited lacks the strength to warrant continuing out of position against an aggressive opponent like Minni. I have a substantial stack (24,650) and nearly 100 hands remaining, so I can afford to be selective and wait for stronger holdings. The risk-reward doesn't justify the call here.

Button with K♣8♣ — a playable, suited King with position against only the blinds. Standard exploitative play is to open here to take the blinds and apply pressure, isolate the short-stacked BB, and keep ranges wide in position. My stack (11,350) is deep enough that a 3x open is small relative to the stacks and preserves post‑flop maneuvering. Raise to 300 gives good fold equity vs both opponents; if Claude 3-bets big I will often fold this hand, and if Flash (short) shoves I will evaluate calling based on pot odds and exact shove size. If called, plan to c-bet favorable, dry flops and play aggressively in position; control pot size on dangerous boards.

I'm holding 9cJc from the small blind facing Minni's button raise to 300. With 24,600 chips, I have a healthy stack. My hand is broadway-adjacent with decent showdown value. Minni has demonstrated aggressive tendencies throughout this session, raising frequently from favorable positions. Given the pot odds (250 to call into 450), calling is justified here. If Flash folds as a short stack, I'll be heads-up in position post-flop against an aggressive opponent, which is playable. If Flash calls, I'll be out of position but can play cautiously post-flop. Jack-nine offsuit is a reasonable hand to see a flop with in this spot given the pot odds and opponent tendencies.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Four of Diamonds. Minni opened from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. Although the pot odds are roughly 4.5 to 1 to call the extra 200 chips, T4o is a very weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially out of position. Given my stack size of 3900 chips (39 BB), it is better to fold this marginal hand and preserve chips for a better opportunity.

I'm holding 9♣J♣ on a flop of 4♣A♦5♦ in a heads-up pot against Minni. This flop is unfavorable for my hand - I have no made value, no strong draws, just jack-high. Given that Minni raised preflop from the button and I'm out of position on the flop, I need to proceed cautiously. Betting here would be a bluff with weak equity, and I'm unlikely to be ahead if Minni has an ace, overpair, or other strong holding. Checking allows me to: (1) keep my modest showdown value in the pot cheaply, (2) see what Minni does, and (3) get to the turn with information about her hand strength. If she checks behind, I get a free card. If she bets, I can make a more informed decision about whether to fold, call, or raise based on the bet size and dynamics. This is the prudent line with a marginal hand against an aggressive opponent.

I was the preflop raiser and have position with K♣8♣ on a dry A♦5♦4♣ flop. I have no pair but a backdoor club story and the initiative as the raiser — this is a textbook c-bet spot versus a single checked opponent. Claude’s check after calling preflop is consistent with a wide, often-missed calling range (small pairs, broadways, suited connectors, some A‑x), so a small continuation bet will fold a lot of his equity while keeping the pot controllable against hands that do call. I’ll make a compact bet (~1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity and preserve stack against callers. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and barrel only on turns that don’t complete obvious draws or that improve my perceived range; I will give up to large flop/turn raises with this non‑made hand.

I'm holding 9♣J♣ (jack-high) on a flop of 4♣A♦5♦ facing Minni's 250 bet. My hand is very weak here. I have minimal equity - just jack-high with a backdoor club flush draw that requires two cards to develop. The ace on the board is particularly concerning as Minni's preflop button raise followed by a flop bet strongly suggests she holds an ace, overpair (44, 55), or other strong hand. Being out of position against an aggressive opponent, I cannot profitably call with such marginal equity. At 24,350 chips, I have a healthy stack and should remain selective with marginal holdings. Folding preserves chips for better spots and aligns with solid bankroll management in deep stack poker. This is not a spot where bluffing or marginal calls make sense.

With T7o on the button, I have positional advantage over both blinds. While T7o is a marginal hand, the cost to call is minimal (100 to win 150 pot = 2.5:1 odds). I have a healthy stack (24,350) and can play well post-flop with position against Flash (3,850) and Minni (11,650). The hand has reasonable playability with two overcards and wheel potential. Given the low entry cost, favorable position, and implied odds from deeper stacks, calling is more profitable long-term than folding. This is a classic position-based call where I can leverage button advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of Sixes (6c 6d). Claude has limped from the button for 100. The action is on me. My hand has significant showdown value but plays best by seeing a flop cheaply or as a set-mine. However, given the small pot and the fact that I'm out of position, I want to keep the pot manageable or see if I can narrow the field. Calling the extra 50 is an easy decision to see a flop. Raising is also an option, but with a stack of 3850 (38.5 BB), I don't want to bloat the pot too much against Claude's massive stack if I don't hit my set. I will complete the small blind.

I’m in the big blind with King of Hearts / Ten of Diamonds (Kh Td). Two opponents have merely limped (SB Flash is short-stacked, BTN Claude is deep). K-T offsuit is a medium-strength, OOP hand that plays fine multiway but isn’t strong enough to profitably isolate by raising here — a raise will often get called and leave me out of position with a hand that’s frequently dominated. I’ll check and see a cheap flop; if I connect (top pair, good draw) I can continue, otherwise I’ll pot-control or fold to significant aggression. If there’s a small squeeze from the short stack I may call to see the flop, but I won’t overcommit preflop with KTo.

In a limped pot with a board of 2h-Kc-2s, my pocket sixes have moderate showdown value but are vulnerable to overcards and any King. Since I am out of position against Claude and Minni, checking is the best play to keep the pot small and see how the action develops. Leading out into two opponents on this texture is unlikely to fold out a King and might bloat the pot unnecessarily.

I have top pair (K♥) with a medium kicker on a paired flop (2♥ K♣ 2♠). Two opponents limped in, both checked to me — this is a multiway pot where I should get value/protect but avoid bloating the pot against potential sets of deuces. A modest bet keeps worse kings, Kx, and small pairs/drawing hands in, while giving me a reasonable plan if I face resistance. I’ll bet about half the pot (150) to extract value and deny free cards. If I get a large check-raise from Claude I’ll treat it as strong (likely a set) and fold; I’ll call a small raise and re-evaluate on the turn if I get a single caller; if checked around I’ll reassess on the turn and continue for value or pot-control depending on the card.

T7o completely whiffed on the 2h-Kc-2s flop with no draws available. Against Minni's raise from the BB, I have essentially zero equity - no pair, no straight possibility, no flush draw. At 3:1 odds, the math looks tempting, but my hand is too weak for continued investment. Being out of position on remaining streets compounds the problem. Even with a large stack, calling with essentially air against aggression is chip bleeding. This is a straightforward fold to preserve chips for better opportunities.

In this limped pot, the board is very dry (2h-Kc-2s). My pocket sixes are currently a bluff-catcher. Minni's lead of 150 into a 300 pot from the Big Blind is a standard probe bet that could easily be made with a King, a Deuce, or a variety of air/high-card hands like Ax or Jx. Given the pot odds (calling 150 to win 450), folding would be too tight, as I am ahead of many hands in her range. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (Kx). I will call to see a turn and re-evaluate, keeping the pot manageable with my medium-strength hand.

On this paired board with a King, my pocket sixes remain a marginal made hand. Minni's flop bet could be a King, a Deuce, or a wide range of bluffs/probes. The 4h turn is relatively safe, though it does open some backdoor draws. I will check to keep the pot small and see how Minni reacts. Leading out doesn't provide much utility as I only get called by better hands and fold out the hands I already beat.

Heads-up vs the short-stacked SB who limp-called my flop bet. Board is 2♥ K♣ 2♠ 4♥; I have top pair K♥ with a medium ten kicker. Flash's line (limp-call flop, check turn) looks like a relatively wide calling range of Kx, small pocket pairs and the occasional 2 or slow-played hand — not a clear sign of strength. I want to keep the initiative and extract thin value from worse kings and medium pairs while denying a free river card that could give him trips or a surprising two-pair. A half-pot sized continuation (≈300 into a ~600 pot) achieves that: it’s small enough to get calls from worse Kx and pockets, but big enough to charge draws and overcards. If he check-raises big or shoves, I’ll treat that as a polarized line (likely trips/very strong) and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river.

I am holding pocket sixes on a paired board of 2h-Kc-2s-4h. This is a very dry board where my hand is primarily a bluff-catcher. Minni has bet both the flop and the turn in this limped pot. Given her aggressive history in previous hands, she could be betting a King, a Deuce, or simply probing with air or Ax. The turn 4h is a brick. With pot odds of 4:1 (300 to call into a 1200 total pot), I have enough equity to call one more bet to see a river. Raising would turn my hand into a bluff and only get called by better hands, while folding is too exploitable given her high frequency of betting.

On the river with a board of 2h-Kc-2s-4h-5h, my pocket sixes are now a marginal hand. The 5h river completes some potential draws (like A3 for a straight or a backdoor flush if Minni had two hearts), although these are unlikely given the pre-flop action. Minni has led the flop and the turn, and as the first to act on the river, I should check to see if she wants to continue her aggression or check behind. Betting here doesn't achieve much, as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands and will likely only be called or raised by better ones (Kx or a 2). I'll check and prepare to evaluate a potential bet from her.

I have top pair (K♥) with a medium ten kicker on a fairly wet-looking river: 2♥ K♣ 2♠ 4♥ 5♥. Villain limp‑called preflop and then check‑called a small flop and turn bet, which usually represents a fairly wide, medium‑strength calling range (worse Kx, small pocket pairs, some heart combos, and the occasional deuce/slow‑played strong hand). The river 5♥ does complete some two‑heart flush combos and gives trips to a few holdings (44/55/2x), but those are relatively uncommon in this line compared with the hands that will call a value bet. I want to extract thin value from worse Kx and underpairs while avoiding bloating the pot vs the rare monsters, so I’ll bet about half the pot. Bet 600 into ~1200 should get called by worse and fold out pure air; if he check‑raises/shoves I’ll treat it as a polarized line and fold.

On the river with a board of 2h-Kc-2s-4h-5h, my pocket sixes have become a very weak bluff-catcher. Minni has led for three consecutive streets in a limped pot (1/2 pot, 1/2 pot, 1/2 pot). While she is an aggressive player, a triple barrel on this texture typically represents a King, a Deuce, or a rivered straight with A3. It is rare for a player to bluff all three streets in a limped pot when the opponent has shown resistance by calling twice. Although the pot odds are 3:1, my hand is likely beaten by her value range, and I do not expect her to be bluffing often enough here to make the call profitable. I will preserve my remaining stack and fold.

I am on the button with Jd3h. This is a very weak hand. While I could attempt a raise to steal the blinds, my stack is relatively short (33.5 big blinds) compared to my opponents. Minni and Claude have been active and aren't folding easily to small raises. Given my position and the weakness of my hand, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I'm in the small blind heads-up with Q♦9♥. Against a lone big blind, this is a hand I can open — it's playable, has broadway potential and can pick up the pot preflop. I want initiative and to deny Claude good odds to see a flop for free, so I’ll make a standard small open to 2x the big blind (raise to 200 total). This sizing keeps the pot controllable, preserves fold equity, and allows me to C-bet favorable flops or fold to heavy 3-bets (I will concede to a large, polarized 3-bet and re-evaluate if Claude 3-bets a small size). Action: raise to 200 to take the lead heads-up.

Pocket deuces in the BB facing a SB raise. Minni has shown consistent aggression throughout. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 100 to win 300), which is excellent. A small pocket pair plays well post-flop in position, and I can make disciplined decisions based on board texture. This is a straightforward call with good odds.

Flop: 9♣ K♠ Q♠ — I hold Q♦9♥, which is two pair (Q9). This is a very strong heads-up hand but vulnerable to Kx and KQ combos and to spade/straight draws. My objective is to extract thin value from single-pair hands (Kx, Qx, 9x) and charge/price out spade and straight draws while keeping the pot controlled. Claude called a min-raise preflop, so his range includes lots of Kx, Qx and broadway/connectors. I’ll bet for value and protection rather than check and give free cards. A bet of 250 (~60% pot) is big enough to charge draws and get called by worse pairs, but not so large as to overcommit vs the few KQ/sets that dominate me. If Claude raises large I’ll re-evaluate (a sizable check-raise is likely a cooler); vs a small/medium raise I’m inclined to call with two pair. Action: bet 250 to extract value and deny equity to draws.

I'm in the Big Blind with pocket deuces (22) facing Minni's flop raise to 250 on a 9c-Ks-Qs board. Analyzing this spot: (1) Pot odds are favorable at 3.6:1 (calling 250 to win ~900), requiring only ~22% equity to break even; (2) While my hand is currently an underpair, I have 4 clean outs to hit a deuce on the turn for a very strong hand (trips); (3) Minni has shown consistent aggression throughout the session, but this is a coordinated, dangerous board; (4) My stack is healthy (24,050) relative to the bet, allowing me to play exploitatively; (5) By calling, I maintain position and can make better decisions on the turn based on new information. The board texture (K-Q-9) is concerning and suggests I'm likely behind currently, but the pot odds justify the call to potentially improve, and folding immediately would be unnecessarily tight given my position and the discount I'm getting.

Turn paired the board with K♣, which makes my two-pair (Q9) much more vulnerable. Claude called my flop C-bet, and his calling range contains a lot of Kx and a few full-house-making combos (KK in hand, KQ, K9, QQ, 99) that now beat me. Betting here would charge some draws and extract from worse Qx/9x, but it also bloats the pot and invites a value raise from all the Kx hands that dominate me. I want to control the pot size and avoid getting barreled off a big portion of my stack by a now-plausible trips/full-house range. Checking keeps worse pairs and some bluffs in, lets me realize equity, and allows me to call a reasonable bet or fold to a large polarized raise. Plan: check-turn, call small-to-medium bets for showdown value, fold to big overbets that strongly represent trips/full house; if Claude checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and look to extract thin value or showdown depending on the card.

Minni's check on the turn is a critical tell suggesting weakness. After raising pre-flop and on the flop, her check indicates she likely doesn't have a King and is probably holding a hand like AQ, AJ, or QJ that's uncomfortable on this paired board. With a healthy stack and position, I should bet to take down the pot. A bet of 550 gives good fold equity while not overcommitting. If called, I can reassess on the river, but most likely Minni will fold here.

I hold Q♦9♥ — two pair (Q9) on the flop, but the turn K♣ paired the board and makes my hand vulnerable to any Kx (trips/full houses). Claude led for 550 into a 1,450 pot (small-to-medium sizing). Folding here would be too weak — my hand still has substantial showdown value and the bet is small relative to the pot. Raising would be too risky: it polarizes the action and can induce a shove or reraise from trip/full-house combos that currently beat me, while folding out worse hands I want value from. Pot odds are attractive: calling 550 to keep a pot around 2,550 requires only ~22% equity, which my two pair has against a reasonable calling/bluffing range; plus my Q removes some KQ combos that make full houses. Plan: call the 550 to reach the river and re-evaluate — fold to large, polarized overbets that clearly represent full houses/trips; call or extract thin value vs single-pair hands if the river is a blank, and proceed cautiously if a K/Q/9 pairs the board.

Board: 9♣ K♠ Q♠ K♣ A♠. I hold Q♦9♥ — my best 5 is K K Q Q A: two pair (Kings & Queens) with an Ace kicker. The river A is a very bad card for my hand: any A‑x now makes two pair (A+K) that beats me, and any K in Claude’s range already had trips on the paired turn. Claude’s line (calling my flop bet, then leading the turn) is exactly the type that includes many Kx and some A‑x — hands that now beat me. Betting for thin value here is unlikely to get called by worse very often, and it only risks getting raised or called by hands that dominate me. I’ll check to control the pot and go to showdown; if Claude fires a large river bet I’ll fold, and I’ll only call small river bluffs if the sizing and read justify it.

Minni has checked the turn and river, indicating weakness. My two pair (Kings and deuces) is a showable hand that likely wins here. I've already extracted value with my turn raise to 550. Betting the river risks getting called by better hands like AK (which makes two pair Aces and Kings, beating me), while hands I beat fold anyway. The best EV play is to check and go to showdown where my two pair wins against most of her range.

I'm on the button with A♦3♦ in a 3-handed pot (pot 150, to call 100). A3s is a clear button-open: good blocker (ace), suited with flush/straight potential, and plays well in position. Flash (BB) is short (~32.5bb) and will often shove wide — I'm willing to face that and call a shove because A3s has reasonable equity vs a short-push range. Claude (SB) is deep and capable of 3-betting; I don't want to get bloated out of position against a large 3-bet, so I'll fold to a strong 3-bet and only continue versus small 3-bets depending on the sizing/read. Standard, table-consistent sizing to isolate and apply pressure: raise to 300. Postflop plan: c-bet thin on favorable boards, pot-control when checked to, and extract value when I pair or make the nut flush.

Folding 7-Jack offsuit in the small blind facing a raise from an aggressive opponent. This is a weak, unconnected hand that doesn't warrant calling a raise out of position. My solid stack allows me to be selective and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 8s. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, making it 200 more for me to call. While 98o is a decent connective hand, my stack is relatively short at 3250 chips (32.5 big blinds). Calling out of position against a big stack who has been active and aggressive often leads to difficult post-flop situations. Given my current stack size, I prefer to wait for a stronger starting hand or a better spot to commit chips rather than defending with a marginal offsuit hand out of position.

5-10 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't have strong post-flop playability, even from the button. While position is valuable, the hand quality doesn't justify calling 100 chips. Flash is short-stacked at 3,200 and Minni has a healthy stack at 13,450. Given that I'm 56 hands into a 150-hand session with a solid stack of 23,200, I should be selective and wait for premium holdings. This hand doesn't meet that threshold. A fold preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with J5o. The pot has 150 total, and it is 50 more for me to call. Minni is in the Big Blind and has been playing aggressively. J5o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against an active Big Blind. Although it's only 50 to call, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my short stack of 32 BB for better spots.

I am on the button with Ace-King of Diamonds, a premium starting hand. My stack is relatively short at 32 big blinds (3200 chips), so I want to build a pot immediately. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to narrow the field or take down the blinds.

Fold. T♥2♠ is a very weak hand from the small blind facing a standard button open to 3xBB. Although the raw pot odds (250 to call into 450) look tempting (~36% required equity), T2o is frequently dominated by the button’s range and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. Claude (BB) also often defends, which would make the pot multiway and even tougher to realize equity. A light 3‑bet bluff is risky here because Flash (2900) can shove or call, and I don’t want to commit with such a marginal holding. Preserve the 13.5k stack for better spots — fold.

I'm in the big blind with Q5o, a weak hand that doesn't connect well. Flash has raised to 300 from the button with only 2,900 chips (a 10 BB stack), which is a short-stack situation. While Flash's range may be wide due to their chip position, I have no equity advantage with this hand. Q5o plays poorly post-flop even against a short stack - I'll often have a weak queen or weak pair if I hit. The risk/reward doesn't justify calling with such a marginal hand. I should maintain discipline and fold, preserving my 23,100 chip stack for better opportunities.

Button with T♥7♦ — marginal but playable in position. Pot 150, to call 100; SB Claude is deep, BB Flash is ~32.5BB. This is a good spot to attempt a steal and avoid giving a cheap flop to the blinds. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB (300) to generate fold equity and keep the initiative; if I face a large 3‑bet I can fold this hand, but if called I can leverage position to c‑bet favorable flops or control the pot on dangerous textures.

I'm holding AhTd (Ace-Ten of hearts) in the small blind. This is a decent hand with good equity and Broadway potential. Minni has raised to 300 from the button and has been playing aggressively throughout the session, frequently raising from position and applying pressure. The pot is 450 and I only need to call 250 more. While I'm out of position, my large stack (23,050) allows me to play defensively post-flop if needed. Calling keeps me in the hand to realize my equity against Minni's likely range of raises from the button, which includes weaker holdings. I can fold to excessive aggression on the flop if the situation warrants it. A raise here would be speculative given my positional disadvantage, so calling to see a flop is the appropriate play.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h5h. The Button (Minni) raised to 300 and the Small Blind (Claude) called. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 700. This gives me pot odds of 3.5:1. 8h5h is a decent suited connector-type hand that plays well in multi-way pots, and I have a good price to see a flop despite being out of position.

Betting two pair on a draw-heavy flop. I have Aces and Tens which is a premium holding. The Ts Ad 9h board has multiple straight and flush draw possibilities, so I need to build the pot and get value now before additional outs complete. A 450 bet (50% of the 900 pot) is a standard continuation bet that builds the pot while remaining disciplined with stack management. This bet pressures Flash's short stack and sets up a decision for Minni while protecting my equity against draws.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h5h. I called a pre-flop raise from Minni (Button) and a call from Claude (Small Blind). The flop is Ts-Ad-9h. I have no pair, no flush draw, and only a very weak backdoor straight draw. Claude has led out for 450 into a pot of 900. Minni is still to act behind me. Given the board texture (Ace-high with broadway cards) and the fact that Claude is leading into two opponents, my hand is extremely weak and does not have the necessary equity or pot odds to continue. I will fold.

I have top pair (T♥) but a very weak 7 kicker on a T♠ A♦ 9♥ board — an Ace on board means many value hands beat me. Claude’s bet is small (450 into a 900 pot), which is consistent with a wide c-bet/probe rather than a polarized value jam. With position and a relatively strong but vulnerable holding, I prefer pot control and to keep his worse tens/9x and bluffs in the pot. Calling 450 into a 1350 pot requires ~25% equity, which I likely have against a wide c-bet range. Plan: call, use position to evaluate the turn — fold to significant raise/shove if he shows heavy resistance and the turn doesn’t improve me; if the turn is a blank I’ll proceed cautiously and extract value when appropriate.

I'm holding Ah-Td with a board of Ts-Ad-9h-9d, giving me top two pair (Aces and Tens). This is a strong hand that should be ahead of most of Minni's range from the button. The turn brought a 9, which could help potential straight draws but doesn't significantly diminish my holding. Given that Minni called my flop raise of 450, he likely has broadway cards or a mid-pair. Betting 750 here (about 42% of the pot) accomplishes several things: (1) extracts value from weaker aces and broadway hands that might call; (2) puts pressure on potential KQ/JQ straight draws; (3) continues my aggressive narrative established on the flop; (4) keeps my hand well-disguised - I appear to have a strong draw rather than made two pair. With a large stack (22,350) and Minni still holding 12,800, I'm well-positioned to handle future action. Checking would be passive and allow Minni to potentially bluff or improve on the river for free.

Claude called preflop from the SB, fired a small c‑bet on the Ts Ad 9h flop that I flat-called in position. Turn pairs the board (9d) and he bets small again (750 into a 2550 pot). That sizing gives me ~22–25% equity to continue, which is well within range for a call. I have top pair (T♥) but a weak kicker and there are many Ax and 9x combinations that beat me; however there are also plenty of bluffs and worse tens in his likely range. Raising here would inflate the pot when I’m often behind and would fold out too many of his worse hands; folding is too passive given the pot odds and the chance to realize equity in position. Plan: call the 750 to keep his bluffs/worse tens in the pot and see the river. On the river I’ll re-evaluate — check back or make a small value bet if it’s a blank and he checks; call small bets from him but fold to large overbets that polarize to A/9+ or sets/full houses.

River decision with Ah-Td on a final board of Ts-Ad-9h-9d-Jh. I hold top two pair (Aces and Tens), a very strong holding. The Jack on the river is a concerning card that could complete straight possibilities (KQ would make a straight), but it likely didn't improve most of Minni's holdings significantly.

My analysis of Minni's range based on action:
- Pre-flop raise from button (wide range)
- Called my flop raise of 450 on Ts-Ad-9h (likely broadway cards, mid-pairs, or drawing hands)
- Called my turn bet of 750 after 9d came (likely Ax for a pair of aces, 9x for trips/pair, KQ for straight possibilities, or broadway cards)

Given that Minni has called all my aggressive betting, he has shown sufficient strength to potentially call a river value bet. He's unlikely to fold a holding strong enough to have called two raises. My two pair is very likely the best hand at this moment unless he specifically has KQ or trips with a 9.

River strategy: Value bet. Checking here would be overly passive and fail to extract value from hands like Ax (non-K), Q-J, 10-J, or other broadway combinations that would call a reasonable bet. Even if he has KQ and wins, I'm simply beaten by a better hand. A direct value bet of around 1,200 chips (36% of the pot) is appropriate - it's substantial enough to build a winning pot while remaining sizing-disciplined. This bet extracts value from most of his calling hands while not being so large it only gets called by hands that beat me.

Minni has 12,050 remaining, so he has sufficient stack to call a 1,200 bet if he chooses.

I have top pair (T♥) with a very weak kicker on T♠ A♦ 9♥ 9♦ J♥. Claude’s line — calling preflop, small c‑bet on the flop (450), a small turn continuation (750) and another smallish river stab (1200) — reads as a wide, non‑polarized continuing range that includes Ax, 9x, some thin Tx value hands and a handful of bluffs/missed broadways. Folding here is too passive given the pot odds; calling 1200 into the ~5700 pot after my call requires ~21% equity, which I expect to have vs his mixed range (I’m behind Ax/9x but ahead of worse tens and most bluffs). Raising would bloa­ten the pot and commit me when many of Claude’s continuing hands beat me, and it would also fold out hands I can beat. Plan: call to realize equity and get to showdown; fold to a significant raise/shove that polarizes his line.

Raising pocket deuces to 300 from the button. This is a standard 3x raise that applies pressure on both blinds. Flash's short stack (3000) makes them vulnerable to fold, while my positional advantage allows me to win the pot pre-flop or play with equity post-flop if called. The raise is appropriate given my strong button position and the vulnerability of the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 3c, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised to 300 from the button. Given the strength of my hand and being out of position, there is no reason to call or raise. I will fold to minimize losses.

Facing a button raise to 300 from the big blind with 4♠9♣ (to call 200 into a 450 pot). Pot odds are ~31% (200 / 650), but 9-4 offsuit has very poor equity and playability versus a standard button opening range and is frequently dominated. Raising as a bluff OOP is risky and unlikely to be +EV; calling would commit to a tough out-of-position postflop spot with a marginal hand. With a deep stack I’ll preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I am on the button with J2s and a stack of 3,000 chips (30 big blinds). While this is a 3-handed game where opening ranges are wider, J2s is at the very bottom of any potential stealing range. Given that both Minni and Claude have been playing quite actively and aggressively in recent hands, and they both cover my stack significantly, I don't want to risk chips on such a weak hand. Folding is the safest play to preserve my stack for a better opening opportunity.

I have J4o in the small blind heads-up to the big blind. This is a marginal hand and I’ll be out of position postflop, so I don’t want to inflate the pot by opening a raise into a BB (Claude) who has shown a tendency to defend/call widely. Calling is cheap (50 more) to realize equity and see a flop; I can fold to strong aggression on dry boards and continue only with hits or strong draws. Commit total 100 chips (my SB + 50) to see the flop.

Checking with 4c-Qd in the big blind heads-up against Minni. This weak hand should not be forced with aggression. By checking, I gain post-flop position while seeing the flop cheaply. I can then make an informed decision based on the board texture and Minni's action. This approach maximizes my positional advantage while minimizing risk with a marginal holding.

I have J♦4♥ on 3♣ A♣ 5♥ — I completely missed. I only have a very weak backdoor draw (gutshot to a 2 for the wheel, and a remote heart backdoor) and I'm out of position vs Claude. He has shown a tendency to defend/call widely, so a small bluff will get called too often and a larger bluff risks too much. I called preflop cheaply expecting to fold dry boards or continue only with real equity; this is a dry ace-board that likely hits a large chunk of his checking range. Plan: control the pot and see what he does — check and fold to significant pressure, only continue with a pair, the 2, or clear reads of weakness.

I'm holding 4c-Qd on a flop of 3c-Ac-5h. This is a weak holding with only Queen-high and a gutshot straight draw (needs a 2 or 6). Minni checked, indicating she likely doesn't have a strong hand. With a marginal holding on a coordinated flop, checking is the prudent play. This allows me to: (1) see another card cheaply, (2) keep the pot manageable with a weak hand, (3) maintain a balanced checking range. My position and deep stack relative to Minni allow me to control the hand through post-flop play rather than forcing marginal equity situations. If I hit my straight or catch a Queen on a later street, I can extract value. For now, checking preserves chips and lets the hand develop naturally.

I have J♦4♥ on 3♣ A♣ 5♥ 5♣ — I completely missed. Claude checked both flop and turn; given his wide calling/defending tendencies he’s likely checking with Ax, small pairs, or floats rather than folding to a small turn stab. I’m out of position with almost no equity (only remote runner-runner outs) and the pot is small, so the correct play is pot control and preserve stack. Bluffing the turn here would have poor fold equity and be costly if called, so I’ll check and fold to any significant bet, only continuing on the river with a real pair, a 2, or a clear read of weakness.

I'm holding 4c-Qd on a board of 3c-Ac-5h-5c. I have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needs a 2 or 6). Minni just checked the turn, indicating weakness. 

My hand is very marginal here - Queen-high is unlikely to win without improvement. The board shows a pair of 5s, an Ace, and a 3, making it unlikely Minni has a strong hand either given her check-through action. 

I have no made hand, just a weak draw with two cards to come. Checking allows me to see the river cheaply and cost-effectively. If I somehow hit a straight or catch a Queen on the river, I can evaluate then. Given my weak equity and Minni's checked turn, there's no value in betting here. Checking is the disciplined, bankroll-preserving play with a marginal holding.

Board: 3♣ A♣ 5♥ 5♣ T♣. I have J♦4♥ — missed completely. The board has four clubs, so any club in Claude’s hand is an automatic flush; he also could be checking back Ax, a small pocket pair, or a turned/rivered ten. The pot is only 200 and nobody has shown aggression (both checked flop and turn), so my bluffing/folding equity is very weak and a bet is likely to be called by better hands and fold only by pure air I could beat — unlikely given Claude’s wide calling tendencies. I preserve stack and take the free showdown: check and muck if faced with a river bet (but there is none).

Checking on the river with a pair of 5s against an opponent who has shown weakness through the entire hand. Minni's check-through pattern indicates a marginal/weak holding. By checking, I reach showdown safely with what is likely the best hand, minimizing downside risk while maximizing value from my modest but competitive holding. A river bet invites difficulty against an opponent who either folds (losing potential value) or calls/raises with a better hand.

I’m on the button with J♠7♠ — a playable, suited hand with good post‑flop potential and useful blockers. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call. Flash (BB) is very short (~29bb) and Claude (SB) is deep. Opening to a standard 3×BB (300 total) maximizes fold equity against the short blind, lets me take the initiative and keep positional advantage postflop. Calling surrenders initiative and a min‑raise (200) gives less fold equity, so the +EV line here is to raise to 300. If Flash shoves I’ll re‑evaluate vs his shove range, but preflop the correct action is to open to 300.

Calling with K7o from small blind. The cost is minimal (250 more), I get position postflop, and Minni's aggressive range gives me exploitable opportunities. The pot odds justify seeing the flop with a playable hand that can make strong holdings. My large stack allows for flexibility and positional advantage will be key in determining postflop strategy.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks (Jh, Jc) in the Big Blind. Minni opened to 300 from the Button, and Claude called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700 and I need to call 200 more. However, with pocket Jacks, I should be looking to build the pot and potentially thin the field. A 3-bet is the standard play here. Given the open is 300 and there is a caller, a squeeze play to around 1200-1400 is appropriate. This prices out the caller and isolates the initial raiser or takes the pot down immediately. I will raise to 1300.

Fold. Flash (BB) just 3-bet to 1,300 after my open to 300 and Claude’s call, making the pot 1,900 and the price to continue 1,000. I would need ~34.5% equity to break even (1,000 / (1,900+1,000)). J♠7♠ is a marginal suited hand that loses a lot of its value multiway — Claude has already called so this will be at least a three‑way pot. Flash’s short‑stack 3‑bet is effectively a near‑all‑in and will contain many hands that dominate or are coin‑flip/favoring against J7s (Ax, broadways, pairs). My realized equity multiway is well under the required 34.5% and implied odds are limited because Flash is short. 4‑betting/isolation would commit too many chips with a marginal holding. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold here.

Flash is extremely short-stacked (only 1,700 chips) and has re-raised to 1,300 from the big blind, leaving himself with just 400 chips. This is a desperation shove. K7o has reasonable equity (approximately 28-35%) against Flash's wide shoving range, which at these desperation stack depths likely includes many hands (pairs, broadway cards, weak aces). With pot odds of 2.9:1 (need 25.7% equity to break even), the math is favorable. My large stack (26,050) can easily absorb a potential loss here, and this is the type of marginal hand that becomes profitable against extremely short-stacked desperation plays. Additionally, I'm already committed 250 chips to the pot, so the decision has good risk/reward. Call and go to showdown with reasonable equity.

Checking K7o on a 9h6s6c flop after Flash checked. My hand lacks showdown value with a paired board and no draws. By checking, I maintain pot control, keep the hand cheap, and allow Flash to either check through or overplay with his remaining 400 chips. This is better than betting a marginal hand and inflating the pot unnecessarily.

I have an overpair (JJ) on a paired, relatively dry board of 9h-6s-6c. My stack is only 1700 and the pot is 2900, meaning I have a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of less than 1. Since I am already committed to this hand and my hand is likely the best, I want to get the rest of my chips in now rather than giving a free card to overcards or straight draws. Claude checked to me, and since I am effectively all-in with any bet I make, I will shove my remaining stack.

K7o has approximately 28-35% equity against Flash's all-in desperation range. Pot odds require only 27% equity to break even (4600:1700 = 2.7:1). This is a marginal but mathematically positive spot. My large stack (25,050) can absorb the risk. Calling exploits Flash's aggressive all-in on the paired board where my king-high should occasionally beat weaker holdings and broadway cards in his range.

8-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I have favorable position on the button and a healthy chip stack (23,350), this hand lacks both high-card value and connectivity. Against both the small blind (Flash, 6,250 chips) and big blind (Minni, 10,250 chips), 8-3o has poor equity and will struggle if facing aggression. With only 100 chips to call, it's tempting to play any hand from the button, but this is well below the threshold for profitable plays from late position. I should reserve my aggression and chips for premium hands that can win larger pots. Folding weak hands allows me to maintain my chip advantage and capitalize on better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Q5 offsuit. The Button (Claude) has already folded. It's just me and Minni in the Big Blind. Q5o is a marginal hand, but against only one opponent in the blinds, it has some playability. However, looking at the previous hands, Minni has been quite active and aggressive. Since I only have to put in 50 more to call, I could complete, but Q5o is often dominated or difficult to play out of position if Minni raises. Given the stack sizes and the desire to play tighter when out of position against aggressive opponents, I will simply fold this hand and wait for a better spot, though calling is also an option in a blind-vs-blind scenario.
I am on the button with K9o. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. I'll raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds.

Holding 3♦4♣ in the small blind vs a button open to 300 is a clear fold. This is a weak, unsuited low connector with very poor equity against a BTN open and any caller/3‑bettor from the big blind. I’d have to call 250 more (300 total) and then act first post‑flop out of position — the big blind can still 3‑bet or isolate. Pot odds (250 into a 450 pot → ~36% of the final pot) require much stronger equity than this hand has. Preserve chips for better, positionally advantaged spots and fold here.

Calling with Ad9c from the Big Blind. This is a playable hand with decent showdown value. I have position advantage post-flop, good pot odds (2.25:1), and flexibility to respond to the flop. Calling is superior to raising here as it allows me to make better decisions with information and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal hand against Flash's aggressive range.

First to act on the flop with Ad9c (ace-high, gutshot straight draw) on TcQd3c against short-stacked Flash. This is a relatively dry board and my hand is marginal. Checking allows me to see Flash's action without building the pot with a weak hand, maintain flexibility, and potentially get a free card. The board offers limited ways to improve, so keeping the pot small is prudent while I determine Flash's intentions.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). The flop of Tc-Qd-3c is somewhat coordinated and likely hits Claude's defending range from the Big Blind. However, as the raiser, I should represent strength. Claude checked to me, which is standard. A continuation bet of roughly half the pot (350) allows me to take down the pot now if he missed, while still leaving me room to navigate if he calls and the turn improves my hand or provides a better bluffing opportunity.

Calling with Ad9c on a dry flop (TcQd3c). I have reasonable pot odds of 3.85:1 with multiple ways to improve: a gutshot to the king (4 outs) and potential ace outs. My hand has decent showdown value and equity in this spot. Folding sacrifices invested blind money and pot equity. Raising is overly aggressive with this marginal holding against Flash's aggressive range. Calling allows me to gather information on the turn, maintain flexibility, and control pot growth. Flash is short-stacked at 5,600, so managing the pot size favors me. If I hit my draw or improve on the turn, I'll have leverage. If I miss, I can fold to further aggression in a manageable pot.

Checking on the turn with Ad9c on a dry TcQd3c4d board. My hand is Ace-high with limited outs (~6 meaningful outs for top or second pair). Flash is critically short-stacked at 5,600, and his aggressive pre-flop and flop action suggests he'll likely continue betting or bluffing. Checking keeps the pot controlled with a marginal hand, allows Flash to either bluff into me (where I can call with good odds given the pot size) or check back. This preserves my stack advantage and avoids putting myself in a flip situation. If Flash bets, I'll have better information for a river decision.

I am in position with King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) on a board of Tc-Qd-3c-4d. Claude called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, which indicates he likely has a piece of this board, such as a Ten, a Queen, or a draw. The 4d on the turn doesn't change much. Since Claude checked, I have the option to take a free card. Given my lack of made hand value and the likelihood that Claude is sticky or looking to check-raise, checking behind is the most prudent play to see the river and potentially improve or evaluate a final bet.

I'm on the river with Ad9c on a board of TcQd3c4dTs. My hand is Ace-high with a pair of tens from the board—a marginal holding. Flash's turn check is revealing: he showed weakness rather than continuing his flop aggression, suggesting he either whiffed his draw, has a medium-strength hand, or is playing cautiously given his short stack (5,600). 

Betting here creates poor risk/reward: if Flash folds, I win a small pot; if he calls or raises, I'm almost certainly beaten (he likely has a Queen, a higher pair, or a made hand I didn't see). My ace-high actually has reasonable showdown value against many hands in Flash's range—unpaired broadways, missed draws, and small pairs.

Given Flash's passivity on the turn and my marginal holding, checking is optimal. It allows the hand to reach showdown with my decent equity, avoids building the pot with a weak hand, and respects the range Flash has demonstrated through his checked-down turn. In a short-stacked confrontation, letting marginal hands go to showdown when I have any equity is standard strategy.

The river Ten pairs the board (Tc-Qd-3c-4d-Ts). Claude has checked to me after calling my flop bet and checking the turn. While my King-high has very little showdown value, Claude has shown a tendency to be sticky in previous hands (e.g., Hand #37), making a bluff on this board risky. He could easily be holding a Queen or even a Ten and playing it cautiously, or simply waiting to call with a marginal pair. I will check behind to see the showdown, as betting is unlikely to fold out better hands and checking preserves my stack.

On the button with Q6 suited I want to take the initiative and try to steal the blinds. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) is the right size — big enough to discourage easy calls and small limps but not so large that I bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Claude (SB) is deep and can call or 3-bet, Flash (BB) has a middling stack; plan is: c-bet favorable flops heads-up, be cautious multiway (check/fold to heavy action), and fold to a substantial preflop 3-bet since Q6s is poor to play out of position against strong aggression.

Q3o from the small blind is a weak hand that doesn't warrant continued investment. While I have a large stack (24000) and positional advantage over the big blind, the hand itself is too marginal. Minni's 3x raise from the button is a standard aggressive play, but his range here is still likely stronger than Q3o on average. With 150 hands total and only on hand 64, I should be selective with marginal holdings and preserve chips for premium situations where my stack can exert maximum leverage. Folding preserves capital for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. Minni opened to 300 from the Button, and Claude folded. This is a very strong hand to defend in a 3-handed game. While 3-betting is an option for value and to take control, calling allows me to keep Minni's wider opening range in and play post-flop with a hand that often dominates her bluffs or weaker high cards. The pot odds are also favorable (200 to call into a pot of 450).

I have top pair, top kicker (Tens with an Ace) on a very coordinated and wet board (7-9-10 with two spades). While my hand is currently strong, the board offers many straight and flush possibilities for the opponent. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check to her to see how she interacts with this texture. If she bets, I will most likely call to see a turn, as my hand is too strong to fold but a raise might over-commit me against straights or sets that she could have in her range.

I was the preflop aggressor and this is a good flop to continuation-bet heads-up. I hold Qc6c: a live overcard to the T and a backdoor club draw, so I have reasonable fold equity and some turn-improvement outs. The 7c-9s-Ts board is fairly dry/disconnected for many of BB’s preflop calling hands (he’s likely wide), so a ~1/2-pot c-bet will fold a lot of his missed holdings while keeping the price reasonable against draws and medium pairs. I’ll bet 300 into 650 to apply pressure; plan is to fold to a large check-raise (he’s representing a very strong made hand), call small-to-medium resistance and re-evaluate on the turn — proceed only if I improve or get a favorable action/price.

I have top pair, top kicker (Tens with an Ace) on a very coordinated board (7-9-10). Minni's 300 bet is roughly half the pot. While my hand is currently strong, this board is very 'wet' with numerous straight and flush draws possible. Raising here might force out her bluffs and isolate me against better hands like sets, straights, or two-pair. Calling allows me to keep her wide button-opening range in the pot, control the size of the pot, and re-evaluate on the turn. I have the Ace of Spades, which gives me a 'backdoor' nut flush draw and a blocker to the nut flush if more spades come, adding to the strength of my call.

The turn 6s is a very dynamic card. It completes several potential straights (any 8) and a flush (any two spades). I currently hold top pair with the Ace of Spades, which is the nut flush blocker. This blocker is valuable as it reduces the combinations of flushes Minni can have and gives me equity to the nut flush if a fourth spade falls on the river. Given the coordination of the board and my position, checking is the most prudent play to see how Minni responds to the board becoming so wet. If she bets, I will likely call given my pair and the nut flush draw, but I want to avoid building the pot myself against a potentially completed hand.

I improved to a marginal pair on the turn (6s pairs my 6c) but it’s still a very weak, bottom-pair type holding on a dangerous board. The turn brought a third spade (9s, Ts, 6s), so flushes are possible and opponent could easily have T/9/top pair or be slow-playing a strong hand. The pot is already sizable (≈1250) and Flash has enough stack to put me to a tough decision with a raise — I don’t want to bloated the pot with a second‑pair hand. Checking controls the pot, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in the range, and lets me reevaluate the river. Plan: check and fold to a sizable bet; call a small/medium bet to see a cheap river if the sizing is reasonable.

The river card completes a five-card straight on the board (6-7-8-9-10). My hand does not improve this, so I am currently playing the board's straight. Any opponent with a Jack has a higher straight, and any two spades make a flush. Given the high probability of a split pot or being beaten by a Jack or a flush, checking is the most prudent action. I hold the Ace of Spades, which blocks the nut flush, but Minni could still hold a Jack or a non-nut flush.

Check. The five community cards (7c 9s Ts 6s 8c) produce a 6–10 straight on the board. My Qc6c only makes a bottom pair (pair of 6s with a Q kicker) which is irrelevant versus the board—everyone can use the board to make the straight and only hands that beat the board are flushes (there are three spades on board so a two‑spade hand), or unlikely stronger holdings. Flash’s line (call pre, call flop, check turn, check river) is consistent with him simply playing the board or holding a strong but passive hand. There are essentially no worse hands that will pay me off, so this would be a bluff with very poor equity. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid bloat; I will fold to any meaningful river bet.

Excellent spot with a premium hand. I have AK (King of Spades, Ace of Clubs) on the button, one of the best starting hands in poker. Position is optimal - I'm last to act pre-flop with the strongest hand. My stack is healthy at 24,000, the largest at the table. 

Both opponents have committed to blinds (Flash 50, Minni 100), and I need to win the pot and build value with this premium hand. A standard 3x BB raise to 300 is appropriate here - it:
