1. Builds the pot with a strong hand
2. Narrows the field and makes it more difficult for weak hands to call
3. Maintains my aggressive table image
4. Keeps me in position postflop

AK has solid equity against most calling ranges and plays well postflop where position matters significantly.

I am in the Small Blind with T9o. Claude has raised from the Button to 300. While T9o is a decent hand, it is significantly disadvantaged out of position, especially against an aggressive player like Claude who has shown a willingness to put pressure on post-flop (as seen in Hand #37). With a stack of 5575 (about 55BB), I prefer to wait for stronger hands or better positions rather than calling a 3x raise with a marginal offsuit connector. Folding is the most disciplined play here to avoid difficult post-flop scenarios.

Claude opened to 300 from the button, SB folded. I’m in the big blind with Q♦5♦ — a marginal but suited hand with decent backdoor equity and a blocker to some Qx hands. Calling 200 more to see a flop is cheap relative to my 10,275 stack (200 to win a ~650 pot after my call, break-even equity ≈31%), so I’ll defend the blind rather than 3-bet out of position. I’ll fold to a large 4-bet; if called to the flop I’ll continue with top pair, strong diamond draws, or good turn equity, otherwise release to heavy pressure.

I defended the BB preflop with Q♦5♦ because the call was cheap, but the flop Kc Jh 2h misses my hand. I only have Q-high with a tiny backdoor diamond draw and a queen blocker — no pair and very little equity. Out of position vs the preflop raiser I don't want to bloat the pot with air, and my pre-stated plan was to continue only with top pair, strong diamond draws, or meaningful turn equity (none apply). I’ll check to control the pot, let Claude have to prove it, and fold to meaningful pressure. If he checks back I can realize showdown or re-evaluate on the turn if I pick up a pair or a diamond.

Betting 450 on the flop with top pair, ace kicker. This is a standard continuation bet that represents about 2/3 of the pot. Minni checked, suggesting weakness. I want to build value with my strong hand, extract chips, and maintain my aggressive, positional advantage. This bet size is appropriate - not too large to scare him away entirely, but substantial enough to indicate strength and take down the pot or get called by weaker holdings like Jack-high, medium pairs, or overcards.

I checked the flop with Q♦5♦ to control the pot and let the preflop raiser prove it. Claude bets 450 into a 1100 pot. The board Kc Jh 2h completely misses my hand — I only have Q‑high, a queen blocker and a weak backdoor diamond plan. Pot odds to call: 450 to win 1550 → ~29% equity required. Realistic equity for Q5♦ by river is very low: ~12.5% to hit a queen by river and only ~5% for runner‑runner diamonds (naively ~17% maximum), which is well below the ~29% needed and even lower against Claude’s likely c‑bet range (Kx, Jx, broadways, pairs). Raising as a bluff to 900 is out of position, large, and has little fold equity here. With negligible equity and no good bluffing spot, I will fold to this pressure.

On the button in a three-handed game, I have a suited hand (9-5 diamonds). While marginal, opening with a raise is standard to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot in position. I will raise to 300.

Facing a BTN open to 300 with Jc4c in the small blind. This is a marginal hand but calling is the best option: pot is 450 and calling 250 (300 total committed) gives good pot odds to see a flop, I have backdoor club potential and a chance to hit a jack. The hand is too weak and out of position to 3-bet profitably, and folding surrenders good implied-odds opportunities against a wide button range. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to heavy 3-bet/continued aggression unless I improve to a pair/club draw or have a clear bluffing line.

8-3o is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Despite having reasonable pot odds (3.5:1 to call 200), several factors make folding the clear choice: (1) I'm out of position as the Big Blind; (2) I'm facing two opponents - Flash's button raise and Minni's small blind call; (3) My hand has very limited equity against most reasonable ranges; (4) With ~24k chips in a 50/100 game, I can afford to be disciplined and wait for better spots. This hand will struggle on almost any flop and I'll be forced into difficult decisions post-flop while out of position. Saving the 200 chips here is valuable over the long run.

I have top pair (Jc) but a very weak kicker (4c) and I’m out of position vs the button’s open. The flop (Jd Ts 7h) is reasonably dry but still hits a lot of the button’s preflop raising range (better Jx, JT, two-pair/sets, and broadway combos). Betting OOP to protect is an option, but with this bad kicker I don’t want to build a big pot or face a raise; checking controls the pot, allows me to call reasonable continuation bets, and lets me fold to heavy aggression. Plan: check now. If Flash bets a small/standard c-bet (<= ~1/2 pot) I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn; if they fire a large polarizing barrel I’ll strongly consider folding.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on the button. The flop is Jd-Ts-7h, which is quite coordinated and likely hits a lot of Minni's calling range from the small blind. However, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) and a backdoor flush draw with my 9d-5d. Minni has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of 350, which is exactly half the pot, to try and take the hand down immediately or build a pot for when I potentially hit my equity on the turn.

I call 350. I have top pair but a very weak kicker (J♣4♣) on a relatively dry J♦T♠7♥ flop. Flash’s 350 into the 700 pre-flop pot is a standard half‑pot c-bet; calling 350 to potentially win a 1,400 pot (~25% break‑even equity) is a good price. Calling controls the pot OOP, keeps villain’s bluffs and worse Jx/Tx hands in, and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal kicker where I’d often be dominated. If the turn bricks and Flash barrels big I’ll fold; if I improve or the turn is a favorable card I’ll proceed. I will fold to significant raises here (min‑raise to 700 or larger) unless I improve.

I have top pair with a very weak kicker (J♣4♣) on J♦ T♠ 7♥ 3♥. The turn is a fairly blank card for my hand — it doesn’t improve me and it still leaves lots of better Jx, Tx, two‑pair or sets in the opener’s continuing range. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot and put me in a tough spot vs raises from hands that dominate my kicker. I prefer to control the pot and realize my equity: check back the turn to keep worse Jx/Tx and bluffs in, plan to call a reasonable river bet but fold to large barrels/raises unless I improve.

On the turn, the 3h is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Minni checked again after calling my flop bet. Since she called on a coordinated board (J-T-7), she likely has a piece of it or a draw. I have a gutshot straight draw (8) but very little showdown value. Rather than continuing to bluff into a range that has already shown interest in the pot, I will check back to take a free card and attempt to realize my equity. If I hit my straight on the river, it will be well-disguised.

I have top pair (J♣4♣) but a very weak kicker on a dangerous three‑heart board (J♦ T♠ 7♥ 3♥ 8♥). Flash opened, c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn — that line is wide and can include many weak Jx hands, missed broadways, blockers, but also slowplayed value and completed heart draws. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot and fold out worse Jx hands that I beat while getting called or raised by the hands that dominate me (better Jx, two‑pair, or any heart flush). Pot control and showdown value are better: check and let a worse hand bluff or a weak J call. If Flash leads into me, I’ll reassess (call small/medium bluffs but fold to a large value raise). For now: check to get to showdown without risking a big raise.

The river 8h has completed a straight for me (7-8-9-10-J). Minni has checked to me after calling my flop bet and checking the turn. This suggests she likely has a marginal made hand like top pair or two pair, or perhaps a set. While Q-9 would make a higher straight (Q-J-10-9-8), I have a very strong hand and must bet for value. A bet of 1000 into a 1400 pot is a strong value bet that can be called by her sets or two-pair hands.

Board J♦ T♠ 7♥ 3♥ 8♥; I hold J♣4♣ — top pair with a very weak kicker. Flash’s line (open preflop, small c‑bet on the flop, check on the turn, small ~1k bet into a 2.4k pot on the river) is consistent with a lot of thin value hands (worse Jx, Tx, missed broadways) and some bluffs; a made heart flush is possible only when he started with two hearts (a relatively small portion of his range). Pot odds to call are ~29% (1k into a 3.4k total pot after my call), and my hand has enough equity vs a wide value+bluff river range to justify a call. Raising is unattractive — my kicker is weak, I have little fold equity vs a potential made flush, and a raise would just bloat the pot when I’m often behind to the nut flush or two‑pair. Plan: call and take showdown; fold to a significant raise/shove.

On the button with 7♣5♠ facing only the blinds (pot 150, 100 to call). This is a weak but playable hand in position — good candidates for a standard button open to collect the blinds and generate fold equity. A min-raise to 2bb (200 total) is low-risk, preserves stack depth, and keeps postflop decisions simple; if I face a large 3-bet I can fold and save chips. If called I have positional advantage and some straight/2-pair possibilities. Action: open-raise to 200 to steal the blinds or take the initiative postflop.

Folding 2-3 from the small blind. This is the worst starting hand in poker with no strategic merit. I'm out of position, need to call 150 more, and there's no reason to invest chips in a hand that has zero equity. Minni's raise from the button is likely wide, but that doesn't change the fundamental fact that 2-3 cannot win without improvement and I'll be OOP for all streets. Preserving my 24,200 chip stack for premium hands is the correct strategic decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey suited. Minni has raised to 200 from the Button. Although I only need to call 100 more to see a flop (offering 3.5:1 pot odds), T3s is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given Minni's activity, I expect to face pressure post-flop. I'll choose to fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

8-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker and unplayable from any position. Despite being on the button with good positioning, this hand has no equity against the blinds' reasonable ranges and will lose money long-term. Folding is the only correct play here.

I have Ad 7d in the Small Blind. After the Button folds, it is down to me and the Big Blind (Minni). Ace-high suited hands are very strong in blind-vs-blind scenarios. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop advantage.

Facing a 300 open from the small blind while I'm in the big blind with K♥9♠. I have a marginal but playable hand vs what is likely a wide stealing range from Flash. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call — that gives me ~33% break-even equity. K9o does well enough vs a wide SB open to meet that threshold, and with ~85/70 BB effective stacks there are postflop implied odds to realize when I hit a king or make other improvements.

I don't want to 3-bet light here because the SB will have positional advantage postflop (they act last) and a tiny min-3-bet (to 500) won't fold enough of their range or give me comfortable playability out of position. Folding is too tight against a frequent open from this player. So I will call 200 more (300 total) to see a flop, plan to keep the pot controlled OOP, value-bet or raise when I make a strong hand (top pair or better), and fold to heavy, confident aggression on dry boards where I miss.

On a board of 9c-8c-Qd, I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw and a backdoor straight draw. Minni, as the Big Blind, has many combinations of 9x, 8x, and various draws that hit this texture. However, since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (half-pot) is standard here to test where I stand and to potentially fold out hands like small pocket pairs or weak high-card hands that missed.

I have top pair (9♠) with a King kicker on 9♣-8♣-Q♦. Flash was the preflop aggressor and has been opening a wide range; his small 300 c-bet into the ~600 pot is a thin sizing that will include a lot of bluffs, floats and club draws in addition to value hands (Qx, some 9x, etc.). Per my preflop plan I raise for value and protection when I make a strong hand. Raising here charges club draws, forces worse pairs/floats to pay to see turns, and extracts value from hands that call (weaker nines, 8x, Kx, some thin Q calls). I will raise to 900 total — a meaningful size above the min-raise to 600 to get fold equity vs pure bluffs but still keep worse hands and draws in. If Flash 3-bets large I will re-evaluate and likely fold this marginal top pair; if he calls I will proceed cautiously OOP and extract further value on safe turns or check-control when appropriate.

On a board of 9c-8c-Qd, Minni has raised my continuation bet. This texture significantly favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many combinations of two-pair (98s), sets (99, 88), and strong top-pair or straight draws (JT, Qx, clubs). My Ad 7d has no direct piece of this board and very limited backdoor potential. Continuing against a raise with just Ace-high on such a coordinated board is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Four suited. While this hand has some potential for flushes or straights, it is generally at the bottom of the opening range even in a 3-handed game. Given the recent high activity and aggressive play from Minni (Small Blind) and the large stack of Claude (Big Blind), I will choose to fold this marginal hand to avoid potentially expensive pots out of position later or facing a 3-bet pre-flop.

Heads-up from the small blind with 9♠2♠ — suited but a very weak hand. Pot is 150 and I need to put in 50 more. With deep stacks (~91bb) and an opponent who can be aggressive from the big blind, I don't want to build a bigger pot out of position with this junk. Calling the extra 50 (100 total committed) lets me see a cheap flop and realize equity (flush/backdoor) while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a significant 3-bet preflop; postflop continue only with a pair, strong draw, or good equity; otherwise check/fold to heavy pressure.

Raising to 300 with Td Kc vs Minni's limped SB in heads-up. This is a standard broadway hand that warrants aggression against a limp. The raise builds the pot, punishes the loose play, and takes control preflop. I have position postflop which is an additional advantage. Minni is aggressive but this sizing is reasonable for the situation.

Call to 300 total. 9♠2♠ is a weak hand, but suited and the price is cheap to see a flop vs a standard 3x open. Raising OOP here would be unnecessary and folding relinquishes too much equity; calling keeps the pot manageable with ~90bb effective stacks. Plan: see the flop and proceed only with a pair, strong draw (flush/straight), or clear equity; fold to significant 3-bets or heavy postflop pressure on dry boards. Preserve stack and wait for better spots to extract value or apply pressure.

Preflop I called with 9♠2♠ to see a cheap flop and avoid building a big OOP pot with junk. The flop Q♥5♥8♣ completely misses my hand: no pair, no heart draw, only a very remote backdoor spade plan. Claude raised preflop so his range is weighted toward Qx, 8x, pairs and heart draws — hands that will continue vs. aggression. With ~88bb effective and nothing to gain by barreling out of position, the correct line is to check and preserve my stack. I will fold to any meaningful bet and only continue if Claude checks back (or posts an extremely tiny probe bet that’s so cheap it’s worth a bluff-catch), then re-evaluate on the turn.

I'm playing Td Kc on a flop of Qh 5h 8c after raising preflop. Minni checked, showing weakness. This is a strong semi-bluff spot:

1. **Hand Strength**: I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs - any J or 9 completes a straight) plus broadway cards. Even if I miss, I have decent equity.

2. **Positional Advantage**: I'm in position and currently have the initiative. Minni's check is a weakness indicator.

3. **Aggression**: A continuation bet here is standard and punishes Minni's weak check. It also builds the pot while I have a strong drawing hand.

4. **Bet Sizing**: 400 is roughly 2/3 of the pot (600), which is a reasonable c-bet sizing that maintains pot control while showing strength.

This semi-bluff has multiple ways to win - either Minni folds immediately or I have excellent equity if called, with the straight draw providing 8 outs plus potential overcard value.

I called preflop to see a cheap flop, but this board (Q♥5♥8♣) completely misses 9♠2♠. Claude opened preflop and is c-betting here, so his range is heavy in Qx, 8x, and pocket pairs and will often continue vs aggression. He’s bet 400 into the pot (I need to call 400) — that requires ~28.6% equity (400/(1000+400)). My raw chance to pair by the river is only ~24% with a tiny (~5%) runner-runner flush possibility; importantly a lot of those pair-outs will still be second-best against his range. I’m out of position with deep stacks, so there’s no reason to commit chips chasing remote equity or to attempt an OOP bluff raise on this dry board. Folding preserves my stack for better spots. If he had checked or bet a trivially small probe I’d re-evaluate, but given the size and his range this is a clear fold.

Button with 9♣T♠ — playable, position is my biggest advantage. Pot is 150 and it’s unopened; I can apply pressure to the blinds and take the initiative. Open-raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is a standard size here: large enough to fold out worse hands and avoid multiway cheap flops, but not oversized given stacks (I have 8,875; SB Claude is deep, BB Flash ~6,525). Plan: if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable textures and realize equity with my connected hand; fold to a large, committed 3-bet (very large sizes >~1,200) but consider calling a smaller 3-bet to see a flop. Raise to 300.

This is a straightforward fold. I'm holding Td5s, which is a weak, unconnected hand with poor equity. From the small blind position, I'm out of position against Minni's button raise. While my stack is large enough to call, playing Td5s out of position would lead to difficult post-flop situations. The hand lacks playability - it's neither strong enough to play for value nor has enough connectivity for implied odds. Against a button raiser with position on me, folding weak hands from the small blind is standard practice. I'll preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the big blind with 6h 5c. Minni, who has been quite active and often opens for 3x, has raised to 300 from the button. Claude has folded the small blind. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call. While 65 is a speculative hand, getting 2.25:1 odds in a heads-up pot against an aggressive player isn't terrible. However, playing out of position with a weak connectors like 65o (it's not even suited) against a larger stack is generally a losing proposition long-term, especially given my current stack is around 65BB. I prefer to wait for better spots or suited hands to defend against these button opens.

With 9d 6h on the button, this is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity - no high cards, no connectivity, and struggles significantly against the likely ranges of both the small blind (Flash) and big blind (Minni). While I have a deep stack (24,450), playing weak hands from early-middle positions is poor long-term strategy. The pot is only 150 and I'm looking at a 2.5:1 call, but my hand is dominated and plays very poorly post-flop. With so many hands remaining (79 of 150), I should be selective and wait for better opportunities. This hand doesn't justify investing chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs2d. Claude has already folded from the Button. Minni is in the Big Blind. This is a very weak hand, and although it's only 50 chips to complete the bet, Q-deuce offsuit plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Minni has been quite active and aggressive throughout the game. Given my relatively short stack compared to the others and the poor quality of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid being put in a difficult spot post-flop.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o). This is an extremely weak hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, T2o is generally not part of an opening range against active and aggressive opponents like Minni and Claude. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

3♦7♣ is a very weak, largely unplayable hand — especially out of position against a single big blind. I’m first to act preflop after posting the small blind; calling 50 to see a flop leaves me OOP with poor equity and lots of dominated combinations. Raising to 200 as a bluff is too loose here against a deep, sticky big blind and will commit extra chips with almost no fold equity or postflop playability. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I’m on the button with 8♠3♠ — a very weak, low suited hand. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to play. Calling 100 into 150 gives ~40% break-even equity versus the field; 8-3s’ equity multiway is well below that, and raising from the button with this holding would be a pure bluff likely to be called or 3-bet by Claude (deep and willing to defend) or Flash (capable of aggression). With 90bb effective I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot to exploit position. Folding preserves stack and avoids tricky multiway postflop decisions with a hand that plays poorly.

I'm in the Small Blind with T4o against Flash in the Big Blind after Minni folded. The cost to call is only 50 chips into a 150-chip pot, offering 3:1 odds. While T4o is a weak holding, heads-up poker from Small Blind typically plays with a wider range, and the cheap price combined with my significant chip advantage (24,450 vs 6,375) makes this a profitable call. I'll have position post-flop and can outplay Flash on many board textures. Folding here gives away too much value given the pot odds.

In the Big Blind with 9s5s, a suited gapper. The action has folded to Claude in the Small Blind who completed (limped), and I am checking to see a free flop. This hand has potential for flushes or straights, and checking here is the standard play in this position against a limp.

Checking with T♥4♦ on 9♦ J♣ T♣. While I have a pair of tens, my kicker is weak and I'm out of position. Checking allows me to gather information about Flash's hand and keep the pot manageable while maintaining positional control. My chip lead gives me the flexibility to play cautiously here and let Flash dictate action, which I can respond to on later streets.

I have bottom pair (nines) on a very coordinated board (J-T-9). In a limped pot, there's no reason to inflate the pot with a marginal hand that is easily outdrawn or already beaten by many combinations in Claude's range. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free and keep the pot small.

With T♥4♦ on a board of 9♦ J♣ T♣ 8♣, I have a pair of tens but with a critically weak four kicker. While I'm currently ahead of most hands, this is a vulnerable holding that can easily be dominated. Flash has shown weakness by checking both the flop and implied action, but the 8♣ on the turn has created multiple ways for Flash to have improved: two-pair hands (J8, J9, 89), flush draws, or even a made flush. Given: (1) my weak kicker leaves me vulnerable to being out-kicked by better tens or losing to higher pairs/two-pair, (2) my significant chip advantage (24,400 vs 6,375) allows me to play cautiously, and (3) checking gathers information about Flash's hand strength on the river without inflating the pot. I'll check to keep the pot manageable, see the river card and Flash's action, and reassess strength before committing additional chips.

I have bottom pair (nines) on a extremely coordinated board. The board shows 9-T-J-8 with three clubs. This means any Queen or seven makes a straight, and any club makes a flush. Claude has checked twice, but there is no reason to bet here as I have some showdown value but will likely only be called by better hands. I will check behind to keep the pot small and try to get to a cheap showdown.

Checking the river with T♥4♦ on a 9♦ J♣ T♣ 8♣ Q♥ board. I have a pair of tens with a critically weak four kicker. Flash has checked twice (turn and river), indicating weakness throughout. The Q♥ on the river adds risk that Flash could have improved to a queen. Given my vulnerable hand and Flash's weak checking pattern, betting risks being called by better hands (any queen, jack, better ten, or even a lower pair that still beats my weak kicker). Checking allows me to either win a small pot at showdown or avoid losing more chips. My 24,400 to 6,375 chip lead gives me the flexibility to play cautiously here and move to the next hand with chips preserved.

The board shows 9-J-T-8-Q with three clubs. This means a straight is already on the board (8-9-10-J-Q). However, any King or Seven makes a higher or lower straight, and any club makes a flush. My hand is a pair of nines, which doesn't improve the board straight. Since Claude has checked and the pot is small, there is no value in betting; I will likely either chop or be beaten by a superior hand. Checking is the most sensible action.

J4o is a weak hand that doesn't warrant investment. With a healthy stack of 24,500 and no pressing chip pressure, I can be selective. Against two opponents with reasonable stack sizes (Flash at 6,425 and Minni at 8,925), J4o has poor equity when called and weak showdown value. This is a clear fold situation from the button pre-flop. I should preserve my stack for better opportunities where I hold stronger holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 2d. This is a very weak hand, even if it is suited and connected. Claude has already folded from the Button. The action is now on me to either complete the blind by calling 50 more, raising, or folding. Minni is in the Big Blind and has been fairly active. Given the extreme weakness of 32s and the fact that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if I play, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with QTo. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. Opening here allows me to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. QTo is strong enough to play post-flop, especially with positional advantage.

Facing a standard 3bb open to 300 from the Button while I’m in the small blind with 7♠2♦. This hand has virtually no playability and will be dominated a large majority of the time; calling 250 into a 450 pot (pot will be 700 after my call) requires ~26% equity to break even, and out of position versus two opponents my practical equity and postflop prospects are poor. A 3-bet bluff to 500 is possible in some spots, but the Button is opening frequently and the big blind (Claude) is deep and often willing to continue, so a 3-bet risks facing callers or a 4-bet and a tough decision. Preserve the ~90bb stack for better spots — fold.

I'm in the big blind with Qc5c against Flash's raise to 300 from the button. This is a weak hand (Queen-Five offsuit) that doesn't warrant a call. While Flash is relatively short-stacked (6125 chips) and I have a significant stack advantage (24400), Q5o is at the bottom of my calling range even in heads-up situations. Flash's standard button raise doesn't give me enough reason to get involved with such marginal hole cards. Folding preserves my chips for stronger opportunities where I have better equity. At hand 75/150, I should be selective and disciplined with marginal hands.

I’m on the button with T♥8♥ — a playable, suited one-gapper with good post‑flop playability and backdoor flush/straight potential. There’s only the blinds in front of me (pot 150, 100 to call), so I want to seize initiative, steal the blinds, and build a pot in position. Standard sizing on this table has been ~3x the BB, so I’ll raise to 300 — enough to apply pressure and deny the blinds good odds to see a free flop while keeping the pot manageable relative to our stack. Plan: C-bet favorable flops (especially hearts, low/connected textures) and proceed cautiously if met with a sizable 3‑bet (fold to a large polarized 3‑bet; consider calling small/medium squeezes only with reads).

Folding 4♥9♥ from the small blind. This is a marginal hand with limited playability, and I'm out of position against Minni's button raise. While Minni has been aggressive throughout the session, 4♥9♥ lacks sufficient equity to justify a call here. The hand doesn't have strong straighting or flush potential relative to the investment required. I'm only getting 450:250 odds (1.8:1) with a hand that struggles to win even in the best scenarios. Folding preserves my chips for better opportunities where I have positional and hand strength advantages. This fits with my tight, positional-aware strategy demonstrated in the previous 75 hands.

I am in the big blind with 7h 5s, which is a weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the button. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1, this hand plays poorly out of position and has very little equity against a button opening range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

K3c on the button is a marginal holding that lacks strong playability. While I'm getting favorable pot odds (2.5:1) and have position, K3 is not a hand I want to get involved with post-flop. Against an aggressive big blind like Minni, this hand will be very difficult to play out of position if called. The weak three kicker means even if I flop a king, I'm vulnerable to being outkicked. As the chip leader, I can afford to be selective and wait for stronger hands. Folding here is the disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. This is a very weak hand, and while the action has folded to me, playing this hand out of position against Minni, who has been active and aggressive, is not ideal. Folding is the safest play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 3 of spades and 4 of hearts. This is a very weak hand with almost no potential in a three-handed game. Given the stacks and the activity of the opponents, there is no reason to attempt a steal or play this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm in the small blind with A♦K♥ heads-up vs the big blind (Claude). This is a premium, high-equity hand that I want to play for value and to isolate the BB. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to call, but limping/calling surrenders initiative and allows cheap multi-street play. I raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) — big enough to get value and fold equity against a wide defending range, yet small enough to keep the pot manageable with deep stacks and preserve post-flop maneuverability. If Claude 3-bets I'll evaluate based on size and his tendencies (call and play post-flop with blockers versus a light 3-bet; consider a 4-bet vs polarized aggression). Standard exploitative, high-ev raise here.

Tc5c is a very weak hand that doesn't warrant defending the big blind. While I have 24250 chips and can afford to call 200 more, this hand has poor prospects both in terms of equity and playability. Minni has shown consistent aggression throughout the session, and I would be out of position post-flop with a hand that rarely improves to a winner. Even with favorable pot odds (2:1), Tc5c simply doesn't have sufficient equity against Minni's range here. Folding preserves chips for stronger spots where I can be more profitable.

Button in a 3-handed pot — Qs7c is marginal but playable because of position and the opportunity to steal the blinds. Limping (calling 100) is too passive and invites multiway action; a small open to 300 (roughly 3x the BB) is a standard, constructive play: it applies pressure, gives fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable if we get resistance. Claude (SB) has shown aggression and can 3-bet big; Flash (BB) is shorter and will defend or shove sometimes. Plan: raise to 300 to seize initiative; fold to a sizable 3-bet or shove (we’re behind too often), and if called use position to c-bet favorable flops, realize equity with pairs/draws, and avoid bloating the pot with air.

Minni has been extremely aggressive from the button throughout this session, raising a wide range of hands and often winning pots preflop or with continuation bets. With Ac8h, I have a playable hand with an ace - not premium, but strong enough to continue against button aggression.

My stack is very healthy at 24200, giving me positional flexibility. While I'm technically out of position postflop as the small blind, A8 has good equity against Minni's likely wide raising range. Calling 250 to win 450 is reasonable odds, and I can play well postflop with ace-high.

I consider a 3-bet, but with Flash still in the Big Blind and my hand not being premium enough, calling allows me to see the board and extract value when I hit. I'll take a flop and use my stack advantage and observational reads to navigate postflop play.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h 6s. Minni (Button) raised to 300, and Claude (Small Blind) called. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 more to call. I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds (200 to win a total pot of 900). While 76 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position, the price is attractive enough to see a flop in a multi-way pot, as I only need roughly 22% equity to break even on the call.

Out of position with A8 on a paired ten board facing two opponents. My hand is currently just ace-high with improvement potential. Checking allows me to gather information about opponent strength without committing additional chips. I can evaluate their actions (bet/check) before deciding my next move. A free card could help significantly - any ace or eight improves my hand considerably. This is a standard play that keeps my chips safe while maintaining positional flexibility on future streets.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a paired board of 10-10-6. Since I'm out of position in a multi-way pot against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) and the small blind (Claude), checking is the most prudent action. I want to see how the other players react to this board before committing more chips. My hand has some showdown value but is very vulnerable to overcards or any Ten.

Three-way flop with a paired board (T T 6) and I missed entirely with Q♠7♣. Both opponents checked, so I don’t have fold equity versus two callers worth risking a big continuation bet — their calling ranges include many tens, pairs, Ax/Kx/Qx and slowplays. With only one club I have no real flush draw and very little equity to continue as a bluff here. I’ll exercise pot control and use position to see a turn card for free. Plan: check and evaluate — call a small probe bet if it’s cheap to see a river, but fold to a sizable raise or heavy pressure since I’m essentially on a bluff catcher with little showdown value.

Ace-high on a paired ten board after everyone checked the flop. My ace is likely the strongest hand here given the action. With a dominant stack and likely the best hand, I bet 500 chips (about 55% of pot) to win the pot now or extract value from weaker aces/hands. This sizing is not aggressive enough to unnecessarily scare both opponents, but shows strength and leverages my chip advantage.

On a board of 6c-Ts-Tc-9h, I have bottom pair (sixes) with a weak kicker. After the flop was checked around, Claude led out for 500 into a pot of 900. With Minni still to act behind me and the turn 9h being an overcard that improves many hands in their ranges (any 9x, or even straights like 87), my pair of sixes is extremely vulnerable and likely behind. I am getting roughly 2.8:1 odds to call, but given the multi-way nature and the board texture, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid losing more chips on the river.

Heads-up vs Claude after he leads 500 into the 900 pre-bet pot (pot is 1400 now). I hold Q♠7♣ with no pair on T♠ T♣ 6♣ 9♥. To call 500 here I’d need roughly 26% equity (500/(500+1400) ≈ 26%). My hand has almost no meaningful equity — only ~6 clean outs to pair on the river and many of those will still lose to hands Claude is representing. His line (call preflop, check a paired flop, then lead the turn) is weighted toward tens, made pairs, or thin value bets rather than frequent bluffs on this dry texture. Raising as a bluff into a deeper stack is too risky. I preserve chips for better spots and fold.

I'm holding 5-6 offsuit on the button, which is a marginal hand at best. While position is valuable, 5-6o lacks the strength to profitably call or raise here. The hand plays poorly both out of position (if re-raised) and post-flop (low equity, weak kicker). With my large stack (24,850) and the blinds already in the pot with decent stacks (Flash: 6,075, Minni: 8,925), I should preserve my chips for better opportunities. Folding marginal hands from the button is standard disciplined poker when facing active opponents.

I am in the Small Blind with K6o. Claude has already folded from the Button. The pot is 150 (100 from Minni's BB and 50 from my SB). It costs me only 50 more to complete the blind and see a flop against Minni. While K6o is not a premium hand, getting 3-to-1 odds to call in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind is generally acceptable, especially given the stack sizes. However, I have noticed Minni has been quite active and aggressive. Since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, I will simply call to see a cheap flop and evaluate from there.

Check. Facing just the small blind who completed to 50, the pot is small (200) and I hold T7♥ — a speculative, marginal hand that plays well postflop but isn’t worth building the pot with preflop. A min-raise to 200 would only put 100 more chips in with limited fold equity against a caller and risks bloating the pot out of position; I’ll see a flop cheaply, realize equity with my straight/flush potential, and proceed based on the flop texture (continue with a pair or strong draw, fold to heavy aggression without improvement). Preserving my 89k-ish stack and keeping pots manageable in these blind-vs-blind situations is the optimal line.

I have top pair (Kings) on a board with a pair of Jacks. Minni checked the big blind pre-flop and has checked the flop. I will lead out with a bet for value and to protect my hand against potential straight or flush draws (like QT or two clubs).

Flop: J♠ J♣ K♣. I hold T♥7♥ — no pair, no immediate club or heart draw (only very remote backdoor possibilities). Flash (SB) bets/raises to 150 into a 350 pot: calling 150 would make the pot 500, so I need ~30% equity to break even on a pure call. Versus a realistic SB continuation/raise range (Jx sets/trips, Kx, broadways, A-high, club hands, some bluffs) my actual equity is well below that (roughly single‑digit to low‑teens %). A raise here would be a pure bluff out of position with little fold equity against a single opponent who can easily call or reraise. There's no reason to invest more with a hand that has almost no showdown or draw equity. Fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with K5 suited. In a three-handed game, this is a strong hand to open with. I have been consistently raising to 300 from the button and other positions to exert pressure and take control of the pot. I will continue with this standard sizing to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds.

I’m in the small blind with 9♠3♠ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450). This is a very weak, unconnected suited hand and I’ll be out of position against the opener. To call 250 I need ~35.7% equity to break even (250 to win 700) — 9♠3♠ has far less vs a standard button opening range, and any call is likely to become a tough OOP post‑flop decision (often multiway if the big blind stays in). A min‑raise to 500 gives little fold equity and a larger 3‑bet would be overly spewy with this holding. With deep stacks but no hand or positional advantage, the +EV play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Calling 200 to see the flop with J9o in a heads-up situation against Flash's button raise. Pot odds are favorable at 2.25:1, and my hand has sufficient equity (~40-45%) against his wide button range. This is a standard heads-up big blind defense spot where calling allows me to realize my hand's equity on the flop while maintaining balance in my range.

On the flop (Ad 6s 2d), I have J9 with no made hand, no flush draw, and minimal equity against Flash's wide button-raising range. In a heads-up situation where Flash is the aggressor, checking is the standard play. This allows Flash to make his natural continuation bet while I maintain positional awareness and control the pot. I can then make better decisions with additional information on future streets. Betting would require credibility I don't have in this spot.

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding King-high on an Ace-high board. This board texture significantly favors my range as the button raiser. Claude checked to me, and a standard continuation bet should be enough to take down the pot most of the time, forcing folds from his non-Ace hands like small pairs or suited connectors that missed. I will bet 250 into the 650 pot.

Folding J9 on the A62d flop in heads-up play against Flash's c-bet. While pot odds of 3.6:1 are reasonable, my hand equity is marginal (~25-30%) and I'm out of position facing an aggressive opponent. The Ace on the board is bad for my range, and I'd be forced to act first on the turn if I call. Position disadvantage combined with hand weakness makes this a fold. Better to preserve my substantial stack for stronger situations.

I’m on the button with A♥ K♣ — a premium hand and ideal to open-raise for initiative and fold equity. Standard button open (3x BB) to 300 will isolate the blinds and let me play postflop in position against Claude (deep stack) and Flash (mid-sized). If called I’ll continuation-bet favorable A-high or dry boards; if I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing/stack (likely a 4-bet or a call vs common 3-bet sizes, avoid spewy shoves unless sizing/reads justify it). Raising to 300 balances pressure and postflop options.

5-2 is the worst hand in poker. While I only need to call 250 more chips to see the flop, this is an easy fold from the small blind out of position. I have a large, healthy stack (24,500) and can afford to be selective with my starting hands. There's no reason to get involved with garbage holdings, especially when facing an aggressor like Minni and with Flash still to act in the big blind. I'll preserve my chips for better spots.

In the Big Blind, I am facing a 3x open (300) from Minni on the Button. I have Q4o, which is a weak hand even for defending the Big Blind against a button raise. Minni has been very active and frequently opens for 300. However, Q4o doesn't play well post-flop and the pot odds don't justify a call given the weakness of the hand and my positional disadvantage for the rest of the hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the button with Q9o, facing a 100 chip call into a 150 chip pot. This is a standard button position with decent odds (2:1 implicit). Q9 offsuit is a weak hand in isolation, but button position is powerful and allows me to play profitably post-flop with strong positional advantage. Both opponents have moderate stacks (Flash ~64 BB, Minni ~89 BB), so there's reasonable room to maneuver. A call allows me to:

1. Take a flop in position against two opponents
2. Use my positional advantage to control the pot post-flop
3. Fold if I whiff and face aggression
4. Extract value if I hit or can represent strength

A raise doesn't make sense with Q9o - it's not strong enough to build a bigger pot with, and folding is too tight from the button in this spot. The small 100 chip call is the mathematically and strategically sound play.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 5s. Claude has limped from the Button. The pot is 250 and it costs me 50 to call. 65o is a speculative hand, but getting 5:1 odds on a call in a three-way pot (assuming the Big Blind checks) is generally acceptable to see a flop. Raising is an option, but given my stack size relative to Claude and the nature of the hand, completing the blind is a low-risk way to see if I can hit a strong board.

I’m in the big blind with A♣J♦ and two players have limped (Button Claude and SB Flash). AJo is too strong to just check and play a 3‑way pot out of position — I want initiative and to isolate one opponent. I’ll raise to 700 total: big enough to price out many speculative hands and get heads‑up or a single caller, but small relative to stacks so I keep maneuverability postflop (my stack ~89bb, Claude 244bb, Flash ~64bb). Plan: if called I’ll c-bet A/high and dry flops for value, control the pot on coordinated boards, and respect large 3‑bets from deep where AJo can be dominated.

Calling 600 into 900 chip pot (1.5:1 odds) with Q9o from the button. Strong positional advantage (act last post-flop), deep stacks, and favorable implied odds against aggressive opponent justify the call. Position and opponent tendencies make this profitable despite marginal hand strength.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 5s. After Claude limped from the button and I completed, Minni raised to 700 from the Big Blind. Claude has already called the 700. The pot is currently 1500, and it costs me 600 to call. While I am getting decent pot odds (2.5:1), 65o is an extremely weak hand to take three-way against a significant raise and a call. Furthermore, I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given my relatively short stack of 6,375 compared to the pot size, the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) would be very low post-flop, making it difficult to play a speculative hand effectively. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger opening.

I have top two pair (A♣J♦) on A♠ J♥ 4♥ — a very strong hand on this flop but vulnerable to sets/trips (pocket Js) and to some heart combos. Claude is in position and called my preflop raise with a wide range that contains worse Aces, Jx, pocket pairs and heart draws. I want value and to charge draws, so I’ll make a sizable c-bet to extract from worse Aces/Jacks and deny free cards. Pot is 1500, so a ~2/3-pot bet of 1000 is appropriate. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue for value on many cards; if I face a large check-raise I’ll give credit to sets/trips and re-assess (fold or proceed depending on sizing).

Folding Q9o (queen high) on 4h As Jh against Minni's flop raise. Hand is too weak - only approximately 20-28% equity against Minni's likely range, right at or slightly below the 29% breakeven threshold for pot odds. Q9o has no made strength, no straight draws, only backdoor flush prospects. Minni will act first postflop as we're now heads-up. With healthy stack and many hands remaining (83/150), folding marginal hands facing aggression is the correct long-term strategy. Better opportunities will come.

I am on the button with 7c4h, which is a very weak hand. The pot is small (150) and it only costs 100 more to call, but this hand has very poor playability and realization of equity post-flop, especially in a three-way pot. Minni and Claude have been playing aggressively. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Hand is 9♣5♦ in the small blind, heads-up against Claude in the big blind. This is a very weak, unsuited hand with poor flop playability and I’ll be out of position for the whole hand. Calling 50 to see a flop OOP is negative EV given how often this hand will be dominated or have trouble realizing equity; a raise would be a thin, high-risk bluff against Claude’s deep stack. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

On the button with K2o I don't want to limp into a multiway pot where this hand performs poorly. I prefer to take the initiative and attempt a standard steal: raise to 3x the BB (300). That size gives good fold equity against Flash (shorter stack) and still keeps the pot manageable if Claude (deep and calling-station) defends. If either opponent 3-bets I will concede K2o and fold; if called I will play postflop in position and fold to heavy resistance unless I improve. So I raise to apply pressure and leverage position.

Folding 4-2 offsuit (the worst starting hand in poker) in the small blind facing a button raise. Several factors make this an easy fold: (1) 4-2o has minimal equity against any reasonable raising range, (2) I'm out of position and would play the hand in disadvantage post-flop, (3) The big blind still needs to act, meaning I could face further action, (4) With a healthy 23.8k stack, there's no need to chase marginal spots with trash hands. This is standard poker theory - even at 50/100, folding 4-2o is correct nearly 100% of the time when facing aggression out of position.

I have a pocket pair of sixes in the big blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, which is a standard opening size for her. Given the pot odds and the potential for set-mining or winning a small pot post-flop, calling is a solid defensive play here. My stack size is around 63 big blinds, which is deep enough to see a flop and play accordingly. I'll call the remaining 200 to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of sixes. The flop is 7h Qc 9d. This board is quite coordinated and contains two overcards to my pair (Queen and Nine) as well as a Seven. Minni, who raised pre-flop, will have many hands in her range that connect with this board or are simply better than mine (any 7, 88, 9x, Tx, Jx, Qx). Since I am out of position against Minni and my hand is essentially a weak bluff-catcher now, checking is the standard and most prudent play to see how she reacts. I'll check and evaluate based on her bet size and future street cards.

I opened preflop to 300 as a button steal. Flop Qc 9d 7h is very dry and I missed with Kc2h — no real draws. Flash checked, showing weakness or pot-control. With position and initiative I should try a small continuation bet to fold out missed hands and low pairs. A ~1/3 pot c-bet (250 into 650) gives good fold equity — break-even fold% ≈ 250/(650+250) = 27.8% — which is realistic against a caller's wide range. If he calls I will rarely barrel the turn without improvement and will give up to strong aggression unless I pair up (K or 2) or get a favorable runout. Bet small to extract value when he has nothing and to keep pot manageable when called.

I have pocket sixes on a flop of 7-Q-9. This board is quite unfavorable as there are three overcards to my pair and several straight possibilities (J-10, 8-10). Minni has made a small continuation bet of 250 into 650. While the price is relatively cheap, I am out of position and have very few ways to improve my hand (only two sixes remaining in the deck). Given that the board texture heavily favors a button opening range and I have no backdoor draws, I will choose to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

2-3 offsuit is the worst hand in poker and has minimal playability from any position. Despite having positional advantage on the button, this hand lacks both strength and playability. With a small stack disadvantage compared to Minni (23,800 vs 10,025) and Flash's short stack at 6,025, I need to be selective with my hands. Even with late position, playing 2-3 leads to difficult postflop situations where I'm likely dominated if I encounter opposition. Folding preserves my stack for premium hands where position and card strength align better.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-high offsuit (9d 6h). Claude has folded on the button. While it only costs 50 more to call and see a flop against Minni, 9-6 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Minni has shown aggression in previous hands, frequently raising or betting when checked to. Folding is the safest way to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with J7o. This is a weak starting hand in a three-handed game. While the button typically offers an opportunity to steal the blinds, J7o is generally below the opening range threshold, especially with aggressive opponents like Claude (Big Blind) and Minni (Small Blind). Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve my stack.

Q♦4♥ is a weak, easily-dominated hand. The pot is 150 and I only have to call 50 (25% break‑even) to see a flop — good price to realize equity cheaply. Raising to 200 as SB with this holding would be thin: I’d be out of position and likely face calls or 3‑bets from Claude, who has shown willingness to defend. I’ll call the 50 to see the flop and proceed cautiously — fold to significant aggression unless I pair the Q/4 or pick up meaningful backdoor draws.

I'm in the big blind with 4s7s, a weak hand. Minni (small blind) has called 50, and Flash (button) folded, leaving heads-up play. 

With a weak holding out of position, checking is the standard play. The pot is only 100 (50 SB + 50 BB), so I'm not risking much by checking and seeing the flop. If I raise here with 47s, I'm just bloating the pot with a marginal hand from a bad position. Checking allows me to see the flop cheaply and play conservatively while I pick up information about Minni's hand strength. The stack sizes suggest this will be a longer session, so maintaining discipline with weak hands is critical for long-term profitability.

Check. I currently have a marginal made hand: a small pair (4s using my 4h with the 4d on board) plus Qd gives me four diamonds (one in hand + the three on the flop) — I need one more diamond to make a flush (about ~35% to complete by river). The board is dangerous (three diamonds + a 10) so many hands (two diamonds in hand = flopped flush, sets, 10x) can already beat me. With deep stacks and only a small pot (200), I prefer pot control and to realize equity cheaply rather than lead out and face a raise from a flopped flush or set. Plan: check and call small-to-medium continuation bets to see the turn; fold to large, committed raises that show a made nut/near-nut hand; if checked through, re-evaluate on the turn (continue if I pick up more equity or can cheaply see river).

With 4s7s on a 4d 3d Td flop, I have bottom pair with a weak kicker. Minni checked from the small blind, suggesting weakness. The three-diamond board is draw-heavy, making my pair vulnerable. Betting doesn't accomplish anything productive - I'm either called by draws that can improve or by hands that beat me. Checking allows me to see the turn cheaply and potentially check down to showdown. This is a disciplined play that avoids spilling chips with a marginal holding out of position.

I hold Q♦4♥ on 4♦ 3♦ T♦ 3♥. That gives me a pair of fours plus a four‑card diamond (one diamond in my hand + three on board). I have 9 diamond outs to make a flush (9/46 ≈ 19.6% on the river) and 4 additional outs (two remaining 4s and two remaining 3s) to make trips/full house, so total meaningful improvement outs ≈ 13/46 ≈ 28.3%. Pot is small (200) and I’m out of position vs Claude (BB). He checked the flop (could be weak or a slow‑play of a flush/set), and the turn paired the board (gives trips to anyone holding a 3), so the board is very dangerous for a marginal hand. Betting here to “protect” or steal is thin — I’ll get called by diamonds, tens and pairs and can be crushed by sets/full houses or a flopped flush. With deep stacks, the prudent play is pot control and to realize equity cheaply. Plan: check and call small bets to see the river; fold to large, committed raises; if checked through, re-evaluate on a diamond/4/3 (value/raise) vs a blank (check or small value bet depending on Claude’s action). For now: check to keep the pot manageable and preserve fold equity.

Checking with pair of 4s out of position. Minni has shown weakness by checking both flop and turn. Betting accomplishes nothing productive - I'm either called by better hands or folded to. Checking allows me to see the river cheaply and make an informed decision based on Minni's river action. This is disciplined play with a marginal holding.

I hold Q♦4♥ and the river (4♦ 3♦ T♦ 3♥ 6♦) gives me a Q‑high diamond flush (Q♦ + four diamonds on board = five diamonds). Claude checked flop, turn and the river — passive line that makes a nut flush or full house/quads less likely, though still possible. Pot is small (200) and my stack is deep, so I should bet for thin value rather than check and allow worse diamond hands or curious one‑pair hands to show for free. I’ll make a small value bet (100, half‑pot / the minimum) to get called by worse diamond flushes and weaker hands while keeping my downside limited if I’m behind. If Claude raises big, that would polarize him to stronger flushes/full houses and I will fold rather than commit a large portion of my stack.

River call with pair of 4s against small bet from weak opponent. Minni checked flop and turn, indicating weakness, then made a small river raise to 100 into a 300 pot. This bet sizing and action pattern suggest either a bluff or weak value bet. I'm receiving 4:1 odds, and my pair of 4s beats most of Minni's likely range after two checks. This is a profitable call against her probable bluff or marginal holding.

Button with K♠8♣ in a 3-handed pot — this is a reasonable open. There’s 150 in the pot and only 100 to call, so limp/call is too passive and gives opponents cheap odds to see a flop with wide ranges. Claude (SB) is very deep and can defend wide; Flash (BB) is shorter and capable of aggression but will fold a fair amount to a standard open. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to take the initiative, build the pot with position, and generate fold equity. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable flops (Kx, Ax with good backdoors, dry boards) and pot-control or fold versus heavy resistance on coordinated boards. If I face a large 3-bet/iso shove from either opponent I’ll fold K8o unless the price is extremely good; proceed cautiously if we get called and use positional advantage to extract value or steer small pots.

Folding 4-10 offsuit from small blind. This is a weak, unconnected hand that plays poorly out of position. Against Minni's raise, I have insufficient equity (approximately 25-30%) to justify calling 250 more. Pot odds of ~1.8:1 don't offset the fundamental weakness of the hand when combined with positional disadvantage. Preserving chips for higher-quality opportunities is the correct long-term strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Minni has raised from the Button, which is a wide range. I will 3-bet for value and to take initiative in the hand, as AJ plays better as the aggressor. A raise to 1,000 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the button and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a favorable post-flop situation.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♠8♣ and Flash 3‑bet to 1000. I must call 700 to continue into a pot of 1350 — pot odds ≈ 34%. K8o offsuit is a marginal hand in a 3‑bet pot: often dominated by the 3‑bettor’s value range (AK/AQ/KQ, mid/high pairs) and it doesn’t have strong blockers or playability to comfortably continue out of position. A small 4‑bet (min to 1700) is ineffective and a larger 4‑bet/shove would be overcommitting with a weak hand. Given the price, my estimated equity vs a realistic 3‑bet range is below break‑even and I’d be playing a bloated pot OOP with a poor kicker. I’ll preserve my stack for better spots and fold here.

T9o is a marginal hand that doesn't warrant playing, even from the button. While I have position and Flash is short-stacked at 6,325, T9o has poor play characteristics. The hand can miss frequently on the flop and create tough decisions in multiway pots. With a healthy 23,550 stack and 59 hands remaining, there's no benefit to playing weak holdings just for position. Maintaining hand selection discipline is crucial for long-term profitability. I'll fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 4c. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While Claude has folded on the button, leaving only the Big Blind (Minni) to contend with, playing such a weak hand out of position is not profitable. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey offsuit. While it is a three-handed game, this hand is significantly below the threshold for a standard opening range from the button. My stack is roughly 63 big blinds, and I prefer to wait for a better hand to commit chips rather than trying to steal with very weak holdings, especially since Claude and Minni have shown willingness to defend and play aggressively.

I'm in the small blind with 6♠8♣ — an unsuited, one-gap connector that's speculative but fundamentally weak. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 more to see a flop (100 total committed). Claude (BB) is deep-stacked and has shown a willingness to defend and play postflop, so a min-raise to 200 would often be called or 3-bet, leaving me out of position with a marginal hand and little fold equity. Calling gives me 3:1 immediate pot odds to see a flop and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: complete to see the flop; continue only with strong pair/two pair/trips or good draws, otherwise control the pot and fold to significant aggression OOP.

Checking the big blind with 3To offsuit against a small blind call. This is a weak hand where I want to see a flop cheaply while maintaining positional advantage post-flop. Raising would build a pot out of position with marginal cards, which is unprofitable.

I have two pair (8s and 6s) on 6c 9h 8h — a very strong hand heads-up but vulnerable to sets (66/88/99) and made straights (5+7 or 7+10) and heart flushes developing. I want to extract value from worse one-pair hands and charge any straight/flush draws while denying correct odds to continue. Betting for value and protection is best here, so I size up to ~3/4 pot to get called by pairs/draws but make sets/straights pay to continue. If Claude raises big his range will be thin (mostly sets/straights) and I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to extreme overbets, call smaller raises and proceed cautiously on the turn).

T3o has zero equity on 6-9-8 flop with no pair, no draw. Minni's raise indicates strength, and I'm out of position. Even at favorable pot odds (350:150), I need ~30% equity to break even and have essentially 0% here. Clear fold—no point throwing chips at a lost hand.

3-handed on the button with 7♣8♠ (connected, not ideal but playable). Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call — good spot to be aggressive with positional advantage. A standard open to 300 matches table norms and often isolates or wins the blinds outright while keeping the pot manageable. Flash (BB) has shown aggression preflop before, so I’m ready to fold to a large 3-bet, but I’ll call a small squeeze if the price and SPR are attractive. If called, I’ll use position: c-bet light on dry boards, control size on coordinated/paired boards, and extract value when I improve.

I'm in the small blind with 6c-2h, universally the worst starting hand in poker. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and I'm facing a 250 chip call. With a healthy 23,400 chip stack, I can easily wait for better spots. 6-2o has no playable value here - it loses to virtually every other hand and would put me in difficult post-flop spots. The correct decision is to fold and preserve my stack for premium hands.

I am in the Big Blind holding 2d 3h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Although I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, playing 2-3 offsuit out of position against an aggressive button is a losing proposition in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

4-10 offsuit is a weak hand unsuitable for playing from any position. It lacks connectivity, is unsuited, and has a poor kicker. Even with positional advantage on the button, calling 100 chips without any hand equity against two blind opponents is poor poker strategy. I'll fold and preserve my healthy 23,400 chip stack for stronger opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 2h 8s, which is a very weak hand. Claude has already folded from the button. While it's only 50 chips more to call, this hand is extremely difficult to play out of position, and Minni (the Big Blind) has shown significant aggression throughout the game. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Q9o in a three-handed game. Q9o is well within the standard opening range for the button. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard play to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand with position.

Facing a 3bb button open with Q♣9♦ (Qh9d) from the small blind, this is a marginal but playable hand — especially for the price. I’ve already got 50 in; calling 250 (300 total) keeps the pot manageable, allows me to see a flop for a reasonable price, and avoids getting into a bloated OOP 3-bet pot with a hand that has limited play as a 3-bet shove/iso. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously postflop (look to continue on Q/9 or strong draws, fold to heavy action when out of position), and fold to a significant squeeze/4-bet preflop.

Folding 9-3 offsuit from the big blind. Despite 4.5:1 pot odds, this is one of the weakest hands in poker. Playing out of position against two opponents with marginal holdings is not profitable long-term. Disciplined hand selection is crucial for consistent cash game success.

I have Q♥9♦ on 6♣ T♠ 8♥ — no pair but a strong semi-bluff: J or 7 complete a straight (8 clean outs) giving ~31% to hit by the river, plus pair-backups (Q/9) that can pair (but often second-best). I’m out of position to the preflop opener, so I want to control the pot and realize my equity rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: check and either call a standard continuation bet to chase my straight (or fold to a large raise), or take the free card if villain checks. If I hit a J/7 or improve to a pair on the turn I’ll re-evaluate for value/backraise depending on sizing and read.

I have an open-ended straight draw (8-9-10-J or 6-7-8-9-10) and one overcard (Queen) on a coordinated board of 6-10-8. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play. This allows me to take down the pot immediately if she has nothing, or build a pot for when I potentially hit my straight on the turn or river. I will bet 350, which is exactly half the pot.

Call 350. The pot is 1050 and I only need ~25% equity to make a call profitable. With Q♥9♦ on 6♣ T♠ 8♥ I have ~10 realistic outs (3 queens + 3 nines + 4 sevens) — roughly a 38–39% chance to improve by the river. Some of those pair-outs can be second‑best vs a Ten or an ace/king, but the 7s are clean straight outs and the Q is a strong overcard that frequently wins. Flash’s button C-bet sizing (350 into 1050) is standard and indicates a wide barreling range; calling keeps the pot manageable out of position, realizes my equity and preserves flexibility. I don’t want to bluff‑raise OOP with a marginal hand (a small raise will be easy to call and a larger raise commits too much). Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy polarizing action if I miss, and extract value or apply pressure only if I improve (pair/straight) or get a clear read.

I’ll check. I called the flop to realize equity and the turn paired the board (6♣ T♠ 8♥ → 8♣) so I still haven’t improved. With Q♥9♦ my clean straight outs on the river are the Js and 7s (8 outs → ~17.4%), and including pair-ups (3 Qs + 3 9s) I have up to 14 potential outs (~30.4%) but many of those will still lose to a T or better. I’m out of position and have limited fold equity, so betting here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and often get called. Checking controls the pot and lets me see the river for free; if Flash bets the river I’ll fold to large, polarizing bets and call reasonable sizing or gamble only when I actually improve (pair/straight).

The turn 8c pairs the board. Minni checked again after calling my flop bet. While I have an open-ended straight draw, the board is now paired, which increases the likelihood of her holding trips if she has an 8. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit my straight on the river without risking a check-raise that would force me off my hand.

I missed — holding Q♥9♦ on 6♣ T♠ 8♥ 8♣ 2♣. I have no pair, no straight and no flush; my only outs were on earlier streets. I called the flop to realize equity and checked the turn to control the pot — that was the right line. The board is now paired and with three clubs on it, Flash’s range (Ts, 8x, sets, club draws, some overcards) contains many hands that beat Q9. Betting OOP as a bluff here is low EV: I have poor blockers to the club/8/T parts of his range and he’s likely to call thin. I’ll check and take the free showdown; fold to any meaningful river bet (only consider calling an extremely tiny, fishy bet where pot odds are absurd).

On this board of 6-10-8-8-2, my Q9o has failed to improve to a straight. While I have Queen-high, it is unlikely to win at showdown against Minni's calling range, which likely includes pairs or Ace-high. However, looking at the previous hands, Minni has shown a strong tendency to be a 'calling station,' frequently calling large bets on the river (e.g., Hand #2, #37). Since she called the flop bet, she likely has some piece of the board or a pair. Attempting a bluff here is risky because she is unlikely to fold even a weak pair. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free, even if my chances of winning are slim, rather than losing more chips to a call.

I'm on the button with Tc8c — a playable, suited connector-type hand with good multi-street potential. Three-handed, standard exploit is to open for fold equity and to avoid limp-induced multiway pots. Table tendencies and past hands show 3x BB opens are common, so I size to 300 (3x the BB): big enough to get folds from the blinds and to protect my hand, but not so big that a call from Flash (61bb) or a light 3-bet from deep Claude commits me unnecessarily. Plan: if called, proceed to C-bet favorable textures and use positional advantage; if I face a large 3-bet, fold or step back depending on sizing and opponent. Execute a standard button open to 300.

Raising to 800 with AQ from the small blind. AQ is a premium hand that should be played aggressively, especially against a button raise from an active player like Minni. The 3-bet helps narrow the field, puts pressure on the opponents, and builds the pot when I have a strong hand. Stack advantage supports an aggressive posture here.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 4d, a very weak hand. Minni raised from the Button and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind. Given the strength of the action in front of me and my poor hand quality, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with Tc8c — a playable, suited connector with good multi-street potential in position. Claude 3-bet to 800; given his deep stack and shown willingness to 3-bet/pressure, his range is likely wide enough that Tc8c has reasonable equity and excellent playability IP. Calling 500 more (total commitment 800) gets ~29% immediate pot odds and preserves position, which is valuable for realization of flush/straight/paired equity and for extracting implied odds from his deeper stack. A 4-bet here would be a thin, polarized bluff and unnecessarily bloats the pot out of position post‑raise; folding is too tight given the price and my positional advantage. Plan: call, see flop, play aggressively when I hit or get strong draws, and otherwise control pot size and fold to significant resistance on bad textures.

Continuation betting on a dry flop after preflop aggression. AQ has solid equity and likely the best hand against Minni's calling range. A 800-chip bet (roughly pot-sized) applies pressure, builds value, and takes control of the hand. Minni's short stack makes this a good opportunity to accumulate chips.

Board is 6d-2d-3c, I’m on the button with Tc8c. Claude (who 3-bet preflop) makes a small continuation bet (800 into the ~2500 pot) on a very dry, low texture — a spot where his range will contain a lot of missed hands and many high-card/Ax holdings. I have no pair but I have position, backdoor club flush and backdoor straight possibilities, and good playability post‑flop. Calling 800 here gives me ~24% immediate pot odds and lets me use position to float, realize equity, and potentially bluff the turn if he shows weakness. A raise would be a thin, high-variance bluff against a deep, aggressive 3-bettor and would bloat the pot without strong equity; folding is too tight given the price and my post‑flop options. Plan: call, see the turn, proceed aggressively only if I pick up a club, pair, or a clear improvement; otherwise control pot size or fold to sizable turn aggression.

Continuing my aggressive line with AQ on a relatively dry board. Minni has shown weakness by calling both my preflop 3-bet and flop continuation bet without raising. A 1600 chip turn bet (roughly 48% of the current pot) maintains pressure, extracts value from inferior hands, charges potential draws, and continues building the pot when I'm likely ahead. This bet size is proportional to the pot and my stack advantage, keeping good balance between value extraction and pot control.

Turn: 5♦ makes the board 6♦-2♦-3♣-5♦. I hold T♣8♣. Claude (deep, preflop 3-bettor) has shown consistent aggression (3-bet pre, c-bet on flop, bet again on turn). The turn card killed my backdoor club possibility — I no longer have any meaningful draw. My only clean outs are pairing my T or 8 (2 tens + 2 eights = 4 outs). Equity to hit the river ≈ 4/46 ≈ 8.7%. Pot odds to call 1,600 into a 4,900 pot require ~1600/(4900+1600) ≈ 24.6% equity. That’s far above my real equity, so a call is a -EV bluff-catcher. Raising as a bluff is very high variance OOP against a deep, aggressive opponent who has shown willingness to play big pots. Committing ~18%+ of my stack on a speculative float with <9% to improve is unjustified. Fold and preserve stack for better, more actionable spots.

JQ offsuit is a solid broadway hand with good upside (flush and straight possibilities). I'm in button position, the strongest spot at the table, giving me positional advantage post-flop. Both opponents have relatively short stacks (Flash: 6,075, Minni: 8,775), which typically makes them tighter and more defensive. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate - it applies reasonable pressure, allows me to win the blinds with positive EV, and if called, I still have position for the flop play. This hand plays well in position against weaker holdings, and the button is the ideal spot to build the pot with decent cards and fold equity.

In Hand #95, I am in the Small Blind with 6c 9s. Claude, who is the chip leader by a massive margin, has opened for 300 from the button. 96s is a weak hand out of position, and against a large stack like Claude's, it's rarely worth defending, especially with Minni still to act in the Big Blind. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Claude (button) opened to 300 — I’m in the big blind with As9h. This is a defendable hand vs a standard 3x button open: I get ~3.25:1 pot odds to call (pot 450, need 200 more) and have good showdown potential with the Ace. 3-betting is an option, but A9o off-suit is marginal to iso‑3bet OOP and I prefer to keep the pot manageable and see a flop to realize equity. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — continue for value with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry, bad flops.

Flop is 8s-5h-3c, I have As9h — just Ace-high with no pair and no real draw on this dry rainbow board. Out of position versus the button opener, I want pot control and to realize my showdown equity rather than build a big pot OOP with a marginal hand. I’ll check to induce a c-bet and see how Claude behaves: I’ll call small continuation bets (thin value/bluffs) to get to showdown, but fold to large, committed barrels since A9o has poor equity when called/raised. If checked through I’ll reassess on the turn (usually check down unless an Ace or a very favorable card appears).

This is a classic continuation bet spot. I raised pre-flop to 300 and got called by Minni in the BB. Flash folded. The flop (8♠5♥3♣) is low and uncoordinated, which favors my pre-flop aggressor range more than Minni's defensive range. Minni's check is a weakness indicator. With JQ offsuit, I have solid equity—broadway cards with potential to hit top pair or better, plus straight/flush possibilities. Betting here accomplishes several things: (1) maintains my aggressive initiative, (2) gets weaker hands to fold and improves my win rate, (3) extracts value when I'm ahead, and (4) keeps my opponent under pressure. The 400 bet (roughly 60% of pot) is a standard, respectful continuation bet that keeps good sizing for subsequent streets. Minni's short stack (8,575) makes them play tighter and more defensively, so this bet should win the pot frequently.

Flop is 8s-5h-3c; I hold As9h — just Ace-high with only a backdoor spade. Claude (button, deep) made a small c‑bet of 400 into a 1050 pot (~38%). This is a standard low-frequency bet size that often represents a wide c‑betting range. I prefer pot control OOP with a marginal hand: calling is correct. Calling buys me the turn for a reasonable price (400 to win 1450 → ~28% pot‑equity required) and keeps many bluffs and worse hands in that I beat. I will fold to heavy continued aggression on a dry turn unless I improve, and I’ll extract value if I pair the Ace. So: call the 400.

Turn decision with As9h on 8s-5h-3c-4d: I have only Ace-high and no realistic draws (turn 4d kills any backdoor spade/straight prospects). Pot control OOP is the priority — Claude’s small flop c-bet (400) into a single-raised pot is consistent with a wide, often-bettable range (missed broadways, small pairs, some sd value). Betting as a bluff here has poor fold equity versus a deep, willing-to-barrel opponent and unnecessarily bloats a pot when my showdown equity is marginal. Best line is to check and either see a cheap showdown if he checks behind or call a small turn barrel and fold to heavy continued aggression. If the river pairs an Ace I’ll extract value; otherwise I’ll be ready to fold to large river bets. Check to keep the pot manageable and realize equity.

Minni checked twice (flop and turn), indicating weakness. With JQ high and 15 outs to improve, I have solid equity and should continue my aggression from the button. A 700 bet (48% of pot) applies pressure to Minni's short stack while keeping consistent sizing. The blank 4♦ doesn't improve their range significantly, making this an ideal spot to bet for value and fold equity.
