Claude (button) has shown a lead on both flop and turn and now bets 700 into a 2150 pot. I hold As9h — only Ace-high with no real draws. I have 6 clean river outs (3 Aces + 3 Nines) → ~6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river. Calling 700 into a 2150 pot means I must have ~700/(2150+700) ≈ 24.6% equity to break even, so a call is -EV. Raising as a bluff OOP with no fold equity and no real equity is over-ambitious versus a deep, often-continuing opponent. Claude’s line is consistent with value (pairs, maybe an 8, or hands that can still barrel the river) and not thin enough to justify a speculative call. Preserve my 8175 stack for better spots — fold.

82s is a very weak hand, even on the button in a 3-handed game. Claude (BB) is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively, making it likely he will defend or 3-bet. I'll fold this and wait for a stronger hand to get involved.

Open-raise to seize initiative heads-up. K9♥ is a playable, suited king that blocks some strong Kx combos and has good postflop playability; vs a wide BB range it performs well as an opener. A 300 open is my standard size at this table (gives solid fold equity and avoids giving cheap odds), calling 50 is too passive and folding is too weak. Plan: continue on favorable flops, exercise pot control or fold to a large 3-bet if Claude shows heavy aggression.

Folding J3o from the big blind despite getting 2:1 odds. This is one of the worst hands in poker - the Jack is weak when it hits (easily outkicked), and the 3 is essentially worthless. Even facing favorable pot odds, J3o has poor equity against Minni's raising range from the small blind (likely 30-35% at best). Minni has been quite active in recent hands, and I have a healthy stack to be selective with my hands. Discipline in folding weak holdings is crucial for long-term profitability. I'll wait for a better opportunity.

Button with 6h8d in a 3-handed cash game — marginal but perfectly reasonable to open. I want to leverage position and steal the blinds, keep my range wide and consistent with prior table behavior (standard 3x). Raise to 300 total (3x BB) to apply pressure and either take the pot pre-flop or play post‑flop in position. If I face a sizable 3‑bet I will fold this hand; vs a single call I’ll c‑bet appropriate flops and control the pot when I miss.

Playing AK (As-Kh) from small blind against Minni's button raise. This is a premium hand that warrants aggression. Minni has shown throughout the session that he's an aggressive button raiser with a wide range, making him a prime target for a 3-bet. With my strong stack of 25,600 and position advantage when re-raising, I'm taking the initiative with AK. A raise to 850 accomplishes several things: (1) establishes that I have a strong hand, (2) likely narrows Minni's range if he calls, (3) potentially wins the pot immediately or gets it heads-up in favorable territory, (4) provides multiple ways to win post-flop. This is standard premium hand play in this aggressive game context.

I am holding 7d 4s in the big blind. Minni raised from the button and Claude 3-bet from the small blind. 7-4 offsuit is a very weak hand that is easily dominated and does not play well post-flop, especially in a 3-bet pot against two aggressive opponents. Even with the current pot odds, calling is not profitable. I will fold.

I opened the button with 6♥8♦ to 300 and Claude (SB) 3‑bet to 850; Flash (BB) folded. Pot is 1250 and I must call 550 more (break‑even equity ≈ 550 / (1250+550) ≈ 30.6%). 6‑8 offsuit has poor equity vs a typical SB 3‑bet range and I already planned to fold to a sizable 3‑bet in this spot. 4‑betting as a bluff is too risky against Claude’s stack and shown aggression, and calling will put me in tough post‑flop situations with a weak holding. Preserve stack and fold.

7-4 offsuit is the worst possible starting hand in poker with virtually no playable equity. While I have position on the button and am getting 3:1 odds to call (150 to 100), this hand should be folded from any position. At 26,050 chips, I have a healthy stack and don't need to play marginal hands. I should be selective with my range and wait for better opportunities. My previous play has shown I'm successfully picking up chips with strong hands and solid decisions, so no need to deviate from that strategy with trash hands.

I am in the Small Blind with J8 suited. Claude has already folded from the Button. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to complete the blind, or I can raise to put pressure on Minni. Given the relatively high stack-to-blind ratio and the fact that J8s plays reasonably well post-flop, raising is a standard play here to try and take the pot down immediately or build it with a decent hand. However, Minni has been quite active and defensive of her Big Blind. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to see where I stand.

Call to 300. 6♠8♦ offsuit is a marginal but defendable hand heads-up from the big blind against a lone SB raise to 300. Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot (about 33% pot odds) and with deep stacks I can see flops that offer straight/backdoor opportunities without bloating the pot out of position. I don’t want to three‑bet light out of position with this rag — I’ll fold to a sizeable 3‑bet. Post‑flop plan: control the pot if checked to, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards unless I have a pair or a strong draw; continue and build pot with pairs, two‑pair/sets or clear draws.

I have flopped top pair with an eight kicker on a board that contains several draws (diamonds and various straight possibilities). Leading out with a bet is the standard play here to get value from weaker hands like mid-pairs or draws and to protect my equity. I will bet approximately 60% of the pot.

Call. Pot is 950 and Flash has bet 350, so calling 350 into a 1300 pot is ~27% break‑even equity. I hold 6♠8♦ — bottom pair (pair of sixes) with a weak kicker and only remote backdoor outs, but vs a small SB c‑bet I expect a fairly wide range (Jx, 9x, some small pairs, diamonds and a number of bluffs). That range gives my pair enough showdown equity to justify the call and keeps weaker hands and bluffs in. Raising here would be thin value with a bad kicker and will often commit me unnecessarily; folding is too tight given the pot odds and my pair. Plan: call, control the pot and reevaluate on the turn — fold to heavy, polarized aggression unless I improve to trips or the villain shows clear weakness and I can extract value.

The turn brings an Ace, which is a significant scare card for my hand. Since I have a pair of Jacks with a weak kicker, the Ace is a likely card for Minni to have in her Big Blind defending range. To control the size of the pot and avoid getting blown off the hand by a bet if she did indeed hit the Ace, I will check and see how she responds.

Board is 6♦ J♦ 9♥ A♠, I hold 6♠8♦ — bottom pair with a weak kicker and only 5 clean outs to improve to two‑pair/trips (~11% on the river). Flash c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn, which is ambiguous: could be pot control with A or J, or weakness/air. Betting here for thin value is unlikely to get many worse hands to call (Ax, Jx and 9x will usually call; stronger hands will raise; missed hands will fold), and creating a bigger pot with this marginal holding is unnecessary with deep stacks. The safest, most +EV line is pot control — check behind and see the river for a free showdown card, then decide based on the river texture and bet size. Plan: check the river as well vs clear strength; call small river bets from bluffs/weak pairs but fold to large/ polarizing river bets. So I will check.

I have second pair with Jacks on a board where an Ace appeared on the turn and the river completed a possible diamond flush. My turn check was met with a check from Minni, suggesting her range is somewhat capped, but the river 7 of diamonds is a dangerous card that completes both a flush and certain straight draws (like T8). While I might have the best hand, betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands, and I risk being raised by a better hand. Checking to realize my showdown value is the safest and most standard play in this spot.

I have bottom pair (6♠) with a weak 8-kicker on a 6♦ J♦ 9♥ A♠ 7♦ board. Flash c-bet the flop then checked the turn and river — a line consistent with medium strength (Jx/9x) or a pot-control Ace, and also allows missed draws to check back. Betting here for thin value is unlikely to get many worse hands to call and will often just get called or raised by hands that beat me. Checking preserves stack and keeps the pot small with a marginal holding; it also allows me to call small river bluffs but fold to large polarizing river bets. Therefore I will check and take the showdown.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Given the aggression of Claude and Minni shown in previous hands, opening with such low equity is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up vs Claude (BB) after Flash folded. I’m in the small blind with K♦4♦, stack ~72bb; pot 150, 50 to call. K4d is marginal but has useful blockers and backdoor diamond potential heads-up. Limping/calling is too passive — I want fold equity and to keep a balanced opening range from the SB. I’ll open-raise to a standard 300 (consistent with my previous sizing) to apply pressure, deny good pot odds to the BB, and take the initiative. Plan: if Claude folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I proceed cautiously OOP and look for favorable boards (king or diamond), and if he 3-bets I’ll generally fold this dominated hand.

Folding 7hJs from the Big Blind. This is a weak off-suit connector with no real strength. While I'm facing 200 more to call in a heads-up spot and could argue pot odds justify a call, my hand is simply too weak for profitable long-term play. Minni has shown consistent aggression but I have a large stack (25,950) that allows me to be selective and wait for better spots. With 99 hands played out of 150, I should focus on playing premium and playable hands rather than marginal holdings. The risk/reward isn't favorable enough with 7-Jack off-suit even heads-up.

K♣3♥ is a very weak, largely dominated hand with poor postflop playability. Calling 100 creates a multiway pot with little equity; a small open (min-raise) is likely to be called by the BB and/or SB and a larger open risks a 3-bet from the deep-stacked SB. Neither opponent has shown an extreme tendency to fold to button steals consistently, so my fold equity is low. With ~74bb I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot on the button. Fold.

Raising with J9s in heads-up position after opponent folds. Have significant chip advantage (25,900 vs Flash's 6,525). J9s is a strong hand heads-up with flush and straight possibilities. Raising to 300 applies pressure, builds the pot, and maintains aggression in this favorable situation.

I am in the big blind with 3s 5h. Claude, who is the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the small blind. This is a heads-up situation from the blinds. While I am getting decent odds to call (200 to win a pot of 400), 53 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker and performs poorly post-flop, especially against a wide and aggressive range that Claude has shown throughout the game. Given that there are 50 hands remaining and I want to preserve my stack for better opportunities, folding this trash hand is the most disciplined play.

5-10o (unsuited bad connector with poor kicker) is a weak hand that doesn't justify a raise from the button. While I have the strongest stack at the table and am in position, this hand has poor playability and limited equity. Both opponents have short stacks, so I should be selective and wait for stronger holdings. Folding weak hands from the button when I'm not getting odds to call the big blind and have better opportunities ahead is the correct strategy.

I am in the Small Blind with T6o. Claude has folded on the button. It's just me and Minni in the Big Blind. To call, it's only 50 chips into a 150 pot. However, T6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against Minni, who has been quite active and aggressive throughout the game. While the price to see a flop is cheap, folding here is a disciplined play to avoid potential trouble in a blind-vs-blind battle with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with 8c 4c, a speculative suited hand. The blinds are Minni (SB) and Claude (BB). Minni has a standard stack, and Claude is the chip leader. In a 3-handed game, 84s is playable from the button for a standard raise to steal the blinds or play in position. However, since the goal is to maximize the stack over 150 hands and I have a relatively short stack compared to Claude, I should be selective. Since it's folded to me, I have the option to raise or fold. Given the tendencies of the other players (Claude is quite active and has a huge stack), I'll choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to avoid getting into a marginal situation against the chip leader.

Q♠Jc in the small blind is a strong heads-up hand — worth taking the initiative rather than limping OOP. With ~74BB effective I want a standard isolation sizing to apply fold equity and narrow Claude’s defending range, so I’ll raise to ~3x the big blind. Raising to 300 (total) achieves that: big enough to discourage easy calls and small 3-bets, but small enough to play multi-street postflop if called. If Claude 3-bets large and commits a meaningful portion of stacks I’ll fold QJ (or re-evaluate vs a small 3-bet), otherwise I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play for value/position control.

2-10 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker with poor playability postflop. While I have decent 2:1 pot odds, the hand quality is too weak to justify involvement. Strong opponents exploit marginal hands in these spots. My disciplined approach has worked well; I should continue waiting for better opportunities with my healthy 25,950 chip stack.

Button with K♦7♠ — a marginal hand, but in a 3‑handed game position is powerful. Pot is 150 and a standard open to ~2.5–3x the big blind (300) is the proper play to pressure the blinds and pick up the pot outright often. Raising gives me initiative and the positional advantage postflop; I’ll fold to a large 3‑bet (K7o doesn’t fare well vs strong aggression), but if called I can c‑bet favorable textures or check/control on dry boards and continue when I pick up a king or good equity. Calling 100 is too passive and invites multiway play with a weak hand. I will raise to 300.

I'm in the small blind with 8c 3s, a weak hand. Minni (on the button) has raised to 300 with a very short stack of 7275 chips. While Minni has been raising frequently from late position, this particular hand (8c 3s) is too weak to defend from the small blind. The hand lacks both strength and playability - it has no broadway cards and would be difficult to play post-flop against Minni's likely premium range. My large stack advantage means I can afford to be selective. Folding is the correct play here, allowing me to preserve my chip lead and wait for stronger opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 3x the big blind. While the Button's range in a three-handed game is typically wide, Q5o is a weak hand to defend with out of position, especially against a 3x open. Given the current stack sizes and the difficulty of playing this hand profitably post-flop, folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

Raising to 400 chips on the button with 9-10 suited. This leverages my positional advantage and significant stack size advantage over both opponents (who have 6,325 and 7,625 respectively). The raise puts pressure on the blinds, wins the pot immediately a high percentage of the time, and allows me to take control preflop with a decent hand. Position and stack advantage favor aggression here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four of diamonds. Claude, the button, has raised to 400. While the hand is suited, Ten-Four is significantly below the range of hands I should be defending with out of position against a button raise, particularly against a player with a massive stack who has shown a willingness to be aggressive. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Claude opened to 400 from the button and SB folded — I’m heads-up with 6♣4♣ in the big blind. This is a marginal but playable suited connector with decent implied odds and deep stacks (my stack ~76 BB), and the pot odds to call (550 in pot, 300 more to call → ~2.8:1) require only ~26% equity. I’m out of position so I won’t 3-bet as a bluff with such a weak hand; calling to see a flop preserves showdown equity and allows me to fold to heavy aggression on dry boards while continuing with club/straight draws or any pair. Plan: call 300 more (400 total), evaluate flop — fold to large turn/river pressure if I haven’t improved, otherwise extract value when I hit.

Flop: 8♠ 4♠ 7♣; my hand 6♣4♣ = second pair (4s) with a weak 6 kicker and a very slim backdoor club/five-runner straight potential. Claude opened from the button, so his range is wide and will contain many overcards, some 7s/8s/medium pairs and spade combos. OOP with a vulnerable low pair, I don't want to build a big pot by barreling — betting OOP here is thin and gets me raised or called by lots of better hands/draws. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me use information on Claude's action to decide. Plan: check. If Claude checks back I see a free turn and re-evaluate. If he C-bets small (~≤½ pot) I’ll call and use position to proceed (fold to very large pressure if I miss). If he fires big, I’ll often fold this medium pair unless I improve. If I pair my 6 or hit a 5 (straight) or runner-runner clubs, I’ll extract value/raise accordingly.

Continuation bet on the flop with 9-10 of spades. I have strong positional advantage (button) and was the preflop aggressor. Minni's check to me indicates weakness. My hand has solid equity:

1. **Drawing potential:** Two overcards (9-10), backdoor flush draw (2 spades already), and gutshot straight draw (need a 6). Multiple ways to improve.

2. **Positional advantage:** Button vs weakened opponent who just checked - standard continuation bet applies.

3. **Stack advantage:** My 25,500 stack dwarfs Minni's 7,325 - I can dictate action and apply pressure.

4. **Board texture:** 8-4-7 is a relatively dry board with no massive draws, so my two overs have reasonable showdown value even if called.

Betting 450 (roughly half pot) accomplishes multiple goals:
- Builds pot with my drawing hand
- Maintains aggression and position control
- Prices out weaker holdings
- Sets up future action

If called, I'll have good relative equity with my draws. If Minni folds, I win immediately.

I checked the flop with 6♣4♣ and Claude fired 450 into the 1,300 pot. I have a vulnerable second pair (pair of 4s) with a weak 6-kicker, no spade and only a slim runner-runner club/5 straight chance. However the price is good: calling 450 into a 1,300 pot (1750 total after my call) requires ~26% equity. Versus a wide button C-bet range I have enough showdown equity to continue — against top pairs/sets I’m behind, but his range also contains many overcards and bluffs. I’m out of position, so raising here is thin and will often get me raised off my hand or committed unnecessarily. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity, and allows me to fold to big turn aggression if I don’t improve. Plan after the call: fold to large turn shove/raise unless I improve to two pair/trips or pick up a clear redraw (a 5 gives me a straight); if the turn is a blank and he fires a medium bet I may call again to see showdown. Therefore I call the 450.

Check. I have 6♣4♣ — second pair (4s) with a very weak 6 kicker on 8♠-4♠-7♣-A♥. I already called the flop c-bet and the turn Ace drastically improves Claude’s continuing range (many Ax, A8/A7, pairs and sets) and removes a lot of the bluffs. Out of position with a marginal pair and limited redraws, my priority is pot control and avoiding bloating the pot OOP. Raising here is thin and will often get me raised off my hand or committed when behind. Plan: check and evaluate Claude’s action. If he checks back I’ll see a cheap river and re-evaluate. If he fires a small blocking/feeler bet (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot, ≲600) I’ll call to realize equity; if he bets medium-to-large (>~600) he’s most often value-heavy and I’ll fold to preserve my stack.

Betting 1,000 on the turn with my strong flush and straight draw. The Ace was a neutral card that likely didn't help Minni (who just checked). I'm the preflop aggressor with position and a massive stack advantage. This bet accomplishes multiple goals: (1) applies pressure to fold weaker holdings, (2) builds the pot with my 12+ outs, (3) maintains control of the hand. Minni's pattern suggests a weak-to-medium hand. If they fold, I win the pot immediately. If they call, I have roughly 25-27% equity to win with the river card to come, plus fold equity. Given my position, stack size, and draw equity, aggressive betting is the optimal play.

Fold. I have 6♣4♣ — second pair (4s) with a very weak 6-kicker on 8♠-4♠-7♣-A♥. Claude opened from the button, c-bet the flop and now barrels the Ace for 1,000. That line heavily weights A-x, two-pair, 8/7 pairs and sets in his continuing range, and the Ace significantly reduces my relative equity. Calling 1,000 to win a pot that will be ~3,750 after my call requires ≈26.7% equity, which I do not have against his turn range, and I have almost no redraw. Raising OOP with this marginal hand is thin and likely to get me raised off or committed by better hands. With a healthy stack, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the correct play — fold.

I am on the button with 2h 3s, which is essentially the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. In a 3-handed game, while positional play is important, this hand has zero post-flop playability and very little equity even against a wide range. Minni (SB) and Claude (BB) have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet. Folding is the most prudent action to preserve my stack.

Heads-up as the small blind/button with K♠T♥ and ~68BB effective, this is a standard open-raise spot. KTo plays very well heads-up in position — good showdown value and decent equity vs a wide BB defending range. The 200 min-raise is too small and invites loose calls; I want some fold equity and to define ranges, so I’ll open to 300 (a standard 3x BB). Plan: if Claude calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and use position to control the pot; if he 3-bets big I’ll fold to preserve stack unless sizing/past tendencies justify a call or 4-bet.

I'm holding QQ (pocket queens), the second-best hand in poker, in the big blind against Minni's min-raise to 300. This is a premium situation.

Key factors:
1. **Hand strength**: QQ is a top-tier hand that's ahead of Minni's aggressive raising range from small blind
2. **Stack dynamics**: Minni has only 6575 chips while I have 26700, so he's relatively short-stacked
3. **Table dynamics**: Minni has been playing aggressively throughout the session, frequently raising with a wide range. Flash folded, leaving me heads-up
4. **Value extraction**: I want to build the pot with my premium hand and get more money in

A raise to 1000 accomplishes several goals:
- It's about 3.3x Minni's bet, significant enough to apply pressure but not so large that it only gets called by premium hands
- Given Minni's aggressive tendencies and short stack, he'll likely call with a reasonable portion of his range, giving me value
- It establishes control of the hand and the pot

This is a straightforward value raise with the second-best starting hand in poker.

Call to a total of 1000 (700 more). Facing Claude’s 3-bet to 1000, K♠T♥ on the button is too playable and valuable to surrender — good showdown value, blockers to AK/KQ, and strong postflop playability in position. Claude’s sizing is big but his history shows aggression and wide 3-bets, so folding would be too nitty while a 4‑bet bluff with KTo is thin and can commit me unnecessarily if he 5‑bets or calls. Pot odds are reasonable: I must call 700 into a ~1300 pot (making it ~2000), so I need ~35% equity — achievable in position against a wide range. Plan postflop: use position to c-bet favorable boards (K/T textures), control the pot with marginal hands, extract value from worse when I connect, and fold to heavy turn/river pressure on dry boards where I miss.

Flop: Js 5c Jd. I hold K♠T♥ — I missed the flop, I only have one overcard (K) and no realistic backdoor draws. Claude 3‑bet preflop, so his range is weighted to strong hands and Jx/overpairs; this paired jack board hits that range hard. Betting as a bluff could fold out some missed broadways, but a 3‑bettor will call or raise often enough that c‑betting here is high variance and likely to put me in tough spots with marginal equity. I’ll check to control the pot, see how he reacts, and preserve chips — plan to float small bets in position or fold to heavy aggression on the turn/river.

QQ is a strong hand on this dry paired board. Minni's flop check indicates weakness. With him short-stacked at 5,175 chips remaining and the pot at 2,000, a standard value bet of 1000 applies appropriate pressure and extracts value from a wide range of hands while building the pot. This is a straightforward continuation bet with a premium holding against a vulnerable opponent.

Board is Js 5c Jd, I checked to control the pot after Claude’s wide 3‑bet preflop. Claude now bets 1000 into a ~3000 pot — calling 1000 makes the pot ~4000 so I’m getting ~25% pot equity. With K♠T♥ I have two clean overcards (6 outs to pair K or T on the river) and ~22% two‑card equity to pair by river — a touch below pure pot‑odds break‑even — but Claude has shown a tendency to 3‑bet and barrel wide, and I have position. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity, and preserves the option to bluff‑catch or bluff‑raise on later streets if the turn is favourable. A check‑raise bluff here is too thin and folding is too passive given his frequency. Plan: call the flop, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn/river pressure if I miss, but extract/raise for value if I pair up or if board texture gives me initiative.

Board: J♠ 5♣ J♦ A♣; I have K♠ T♥. Claude 3‑bet pre and barreled the flop, so his range is weighted to Jx, strong AX, overpairs and some bluffs. I have no pair and only two clean overcards (6 outs to pair K or T = ~12% to hit the river), so my equity is very weak vs his continuing range. The turn Ace actually helps his range more than mine (he can now have Ax, AJ trips, or still have hands that continue barreling). Pot control is the priority: I don't want to inflate a ~4k pot with a marginal hand and risk committing most of my ~4.9k stack with minimal equity. Checking keeps the pot manageable, preserves the option to fold to heavy river aggression, and still allows me to value/raise if I actually pair on the river or to float/catch small bets. I’ll check and re‑evaluate on the river — fold to sizeable bets, call small ones only when pot odds justify, and look to extract value only if I hit.

Checking the turn with QQ on a dangerous Js 5c Jd Ac board. The Ace is a threatening card that likely helps Minni's range. His turn check after calling the flop suggests he either has a weak holding or a hand that connected with the Ace. With Minni critically short-stacked at 4,875 chips, checking allows me to control the pot size with a strong but vulnerable hand, see the river cheaply, and make a better-informed decision based on river action. This prevents me from getting into an awkward spot where a bet/shove scenario would force a difficult decision with a hand that's likely ahead right now but vulnerable to showdown.

Board: Js 5c Jd Ac Td. I hold K♠T♥ — I’ve turned a second pair (tens) with a good kicker but I’m still behind a large portion of Claude’s continuing range. He 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the paired jack flop, then checked the Ace on the turn — a line that includes lots of Ax, Jx (trips/full house) and strong overpairs that beat me. Betting here for “thin” value is unlikely to get called by enough worse hands to be +EV and can get raised by the hands that already beat me. Pot is ~4k and my stack is ~4.9k, so I don’t want to inflate a big pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check to keep the pot controlled and see a showdown (and plan to call a small river stab — roughly up to ~1/3 pot — but fold to larger river aggression).

River value bet with QQ on Js 5c Jd Ac Td. Minni has checked throughout, indicating a weak or marginal holding, and is critically short-stacked with only 4,875 chips. My pair of Jacks with QQ kickers is the second-best possible hand and beats most of his checking range. A 1,800 bet extracts value from weaker pairs and Ace holdings while maintaining reasonable pot control. This bet follows proper GTO river strategy - value-betting strong hands against opponents who show weakness.

I have K♠T♥ on J♠ 5♣ J♦ A♣ T♦ — that’s two pair (Jacks and Tens). Claude 3‑bet pre, bet the paired‑jack flop, then checked the Ace on the turn and now makes a small river stab of 1,800 into a 5,800 pot (~31% pot). A bet this small is often a blocker/thin value bet or a bluff — a lot of the hands that absolutely crush me (AA/KK/QQ, Jx trips/full house, AT/AJ two‑pair/full) either would line‑play differently or are fewer combos than the hands I beat. Calling 1,800 to win a ~7,600 pot (~24% break‑even equity) is the easy play: my two pair is strong enough to call and see showdown, while a raise to the min (3,600) commits too much of my stack, will fold out some worse hands, and risks a costly jam by hands that already beat me. I’ll call and see showdown; fold would be too nitty and a raise is unnecessary risk for thin value.

I’m on the button with T♦7♥ and 30.8bb effective (stack 3,075). With only the blinds in, this is a standard spot to open a wide button range — T7o is playable in position and has decent postflop playability. Raising gains initiative, builds the pot when we hit, and has good fold equity versus the blinds. I’ll make a standard min/open to 200 to try to steal the blinds while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: if I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold (T7o is too weak vs a polarized 3-bet); if I face a small 3-bet or call, proceed postflop using position to pressure and extract value when I hit tens or good draws.

4-6 offsuit is a bottom-tier hand with poor playability. Folding from the small blind with weak holdings against an active raiser is standard strategy. With 305 BBs and 44 hands remaining, I can afford to wait for better opportunities. No reason to get involved with garbage hands when position and stack sizes aren't favoring a marginal call.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-6 offsuit. Minni, who has been quite active throughout the session, has raised to 200 from the Button. While the pot odds are 3.5-to-1 to call, 9-6 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given that Minni is playing a relatively short stack (under 30bb) and has shown a willingness to be aggressive post-flop, it is better to fold and wait for a more favorable spot rather than committing chips with a marginal hand.

Calling with A6o on the button. While A6 offsuit is not a premium hand, it has merit in this situation: (1) I have position as the button, which provides postflop advantage; (2) The cost to call is minimal at 100 chips relative to my 30550 stack; (3) Both opponents are relatively short-stacked (Flash 6175, Minni 3125), making this a good opportunity to win chips if I flop well; (4) An ace gives me top pair potential. This is a profitable marginal spot given position and stack depth dynamics. I'll take the cheap option to see a flop and leverage my button position.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 4h. The Button (Claude) has limped in. I have 6175 chips, which is about 62 big blinds, while Minni in the Big Blind is short-stacked with only 3125 chips (about 31 BB). Claude is the chip leader with over 30,000. 3s 4h is a very weak hand. Even though it only costs 50 more to call and see a flop, this hand realization is poor, and I'm out of position against a very aggressive/large stack. Given the stack dynamics and the weakness of my cards, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better spots.

Claude (button) just limped; I’m in the big blind with K♣2♥ and ~31bb effective. K2o is a weak, often dominated hand and I’m out of position postflop. There’s no to-call so I can either check and see a flop or try a min-raise to isolate. Against a deep, likely loose caller, a min-raise will usually get called (or 3-bet) and put me OOP in a bloated pot with a marginal holding. Better to exercise pot control and realize equity cheaply — check and evaluate the flop. Plan: fold to heavy aggression if I miss, otherwise proceed only with good pair/draws or clear equity.

I checked preflop and the flop gives me top pair (K) but with a terrible 2‑kicker and I’m OOP versus a limper with a very wide, often passive range. The board K♦ Q♠ 3♣ is fairly dry (no flush draws) so worse kings, middle pairs and Qx hands will often call a value bet. I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal holding, but I should still charge worse hands and deny free cards that can improve some of Claude’s floating hands. A half‑pot bet (~125 into 250) extracts value from worse Kx/Qx/pocket pairs, keeps his calling range wide, and preserves pot control. Plan: if he flat‑calls I continue cautiously on safe low turns and fold to heavy, polarized aggression (big check‑raise/overbet) which typically represents AK, sets or two‑pair that dominate my hand.

Calling the small raise with A6o. The 25-chip raise into a 375-chip pot from a critically short-stacked Minni (3000 chips) appears weak and suggests desperation play rather than premium strength. My position and deep stack (30450) provide flexibility on later streets. While A6 is marginal, the small raise size and stack dynamics justify calling for 3:1 implied odds. I can fold if the turn brings significant action or hit the ace.

I have K♣2♥ — top pair with a very bad kicker. I bet the flop (125 into ~250) to charge worse Kx/Qx/pocket pairs and Claude called, showing a wide, somewhat passive calling range. The turn J♠ is a hostile card: it helps QJ/Jx, AJ/AT (made broadway with A-T), pocket J’s and some two‑pair/set combinations — overall it improves Claude’s range more than mine. With only ~30bb effective and being out of position, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal, easily dominated hand. I’ll check to control the pot size and give Claude the opportunity to bluff or show weakness. If he bets a small, reasonable amount I can call to realize equity; if he shows heavy, polarized aggression (big shove/overbet or large raise) I will fold. Checking preserves flexibility and minimizes downside against the many hands that now beat my K2o.

Betting 300 on the turn after Minni checked. Her flop bet followed by turn check suggests weakness - likely a bricked continuation bet or missed draw. With ace-high, I'm ahead of most of her checking range. Position and stack depth (30k vs 3k) favor aggressive play. A 300-chip bet is appropriately sized to take down the pot most of the time while getting value from weaker holdings if called. This is profitable with position and opponent weakness indicators.

I have K♣2♥ — top pair but a terrible kicker, out of position against a limp/caller. Claude called my flop c‑bet and now fires 300 on the J♠ turn (~60% into the ~500 pot), so his range contains lots of Qx, medium pairs, some Kx, and the occasional set/two‑pair or Jx that just improved. Calling 300 gives me roughly 27% immediate pot‑odds and keeps worse Kx/Qx and weaker pairs in; raising would bloat the pot OOP with a poor kicker and folding is too nitty with top pair. I’ll call 300 to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the river — fold to very large/obvious overbets, call or raise for value against small/medium river bets depending on the runout.

Board: K♦ Q♠ 3♣ J♠ Q♦. I hold K♣2♥ — top pair with a very bad kicker. Claude’s line (limp/call preflop, call of my 125 flop bet, then a ~300 turn bet into ~500) is weighted toward hands that improved on the turn: Qx (now trips/full house), Jx (two‑pair), some Kx and medium/strong pairs. The river Q drastically reduces my relative equity — many hands now beat a lone king. Betting here would either fold out very few better hands or get called by the hands that already beat me, so it’s a negative‑EV enlargement of the pot out of position. I’ll check and take the showdown; if Claude fires a significant river bet I will fold rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand.

Betting 300 chips on the river for value. Minni's double check pattern (turn and river) indicates weakness and a giving-up hand. My ace-high is very likely ahead of her checking distribution which consists of missed draws, underpairs, and weak holdings. She's critically short (2700 chips) and can't defend wide. This bet extracts value from her weak range and likely takes down the pot. The sizing is appropriate to get called by marginal holdings while applying pressure with her desperate chip situation.

I hold K♣2♥ and the board is K♦ Q♠ 3♣ J♠ Q♦ — that gives me two pair (K & Q) with the J as the fifth card. Claude limp/called pre, called my flop c‑bet, then bet the turn and now puts out a small 300 into a 1400 pot. Calling 300 into 1400 (300/(1400+300) ≈ 17.6%) requires only ~18% equity to break even. My two‑pair beats the majority of Claude’s reasonable line (Kx, Jx, weaker pairs, floats, bluffs); only hands containing a queen (trips/full house) or the very rare KQ full house beat me. The sizing is small and looks like thin value or a blocking bet rather than a polarized shove, so folding would be too nitty and raising would needlessly bloat an OOP pot. I’ll call the 300 and take it to showdown.

I am on the button with 8c 7h, a mediocre connector. The pot is small (150) and it's 100 to call. Minni is in the Small Blind and Claude is in the Big Blind. Both opponents have been playing relatively aggressively. Given my position and the fact that 87o can play okay post-flop in a three-way or heads-up pot, I will call to see a flop, hoping to hit a straight, trips, or two pair, while keeping the investment low.

I have Q♦8♥ in the small blind facing a limp from the button and a big blind posted. This is a marginal but playable hand — calling 50 to see a flop is cheap, but raising to isolate the limp gives fold equity and the initiative. I’ll make a minimum isolation raise to 200 total: it’s big enough to pick up many limps, keeps the pot manageable with my ~40 BB stack, and lets me play proactively post‑flop. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet from Claude, call small defenses, and c‑bet favorable, dry flops while playing cautiously on highly coordinated boards since I’ll be out of position versus remaining players.

3-2 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. While I'm getting 4:1 pot odds to call, this hand has minimal equity against Minni's raising range from the small blind. With no pair, no draws, and poor kicker strength, folding is the correct play. This hand cannot profitably call a raise out of position even with decent pot odds. I'll conserve my stack for better spots where I have more playable holdings.

Minni min-raised from the small blind after I limped on the button with 8-7 offsuit. I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call (100 to win a pot that will be 500 total) and I will have the advantage of position for the remainder of the hand. While 8-7 offsuit is a marginal hand, the price is too attractive to fold in this scenario.

I opened preflop to isolate a limp and kept the initiative, but this flop (7c-6h-As) is one that heavily favors a caller’s range — lots of A-x, 7x/6x, and low pairs that Flash could have from a limp/call line. I have Q♦8♥ (no pair, only a backdoor heart and very little showdown value) and I’m out of position with ~39 BB effective. Pot control and information are priority: I’ll check to see how Flash reacts. If he checks back I get a free turn to improve; if he fires a small probe (≈ ≤1/3 pot) I can call to realize my backdoor equity and possibly fold to big turn pressure; if he bets big (≈ ≥1/2 pot or a raise) I’ll fold — this keeps the pot manageable and avoids getting committed with a marginal hand on an A‑high board.

On a flop of 7c-6h-As, I have middle pair (sevens) with an 8 kicker. Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. This check could indicate a variety of hands, including a missed high-card hand or a trap with an Ace. Given that I have some showdown value but am vulnerable to any Ace or better kicker, checking back is a safe way to control the pot size and see how the turn develops without overcommitting on a board where an Ace is present.

Check. I’m out of position with Q♦8♥ and no pair or meaningful draw — the turn (6♦) actually helps the caller’s limp/call range (A‑x, 7x, 6x, small pockets) more than it helps me. Flash checked the flop and pot‑controlled the turn; a bet here would be a thin bluff that will get called too often by Ax/7x/pairs. Pot control is the priority: keep the pot at 500, fold to sizable aggression, and only call a small probe (≈≤1/3 pot ≈150–175) to realize my roughly 6 outs (~23% to pair by river). If Flash checks back I’ll see the river for free and decide whether a small bluff or a fold/lead makes sense depending on the card.

On the turn, the board has paired the sixes, making the board 7c-6h-As-6d. I currently hold two pair (Sevens and Sixes) with an Eight kicker. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked both the flop and the turn. This could indicate she's missed or is playing a hand like a medium pocket pair or an Ace cautiously. However, since the Ace is on the board and the sixes are paired, my hand is vulnerable to trips or a better two pair. Checking back is the best move to control the pot size and reach showdown with my marginal value hand without risking a check-raise.

Board: 7c 6h As 6d 2s. I hold Qd8h — no pair, no meaningful showdown value. Flash limp‑called preflop and then checked flop and turn, which is exactly the line of Ax, 7x/6x and medium pairs that beat me and are unlikely to fold a river probe. To bluff profitably I'd need substantial fold equity (>~25–30% for a small bet), which this line and opponent profile do not credibly provide. Betting here is high variance and likely negative EV; the correct low‑variance play is to check and preserve my ~39BB stack. If Flash now leads into me, I'll fold to any meaningful river bet since I have no showdown value.

The river card is the Deuce of Spades, which doesn't change much. The final board is As-7c-6h-6d-2s. I have a pair of sevens, and with the sixes paired on board, my hand is two pair (Sevens and Sixes) with an Ace kicker. Minni has checked all three streets after raising pre-flop. This behavior typically suggests a missed high-card hand or a small pocket pair that she is hesitant to value bet. While I might have the best hand, there isn't much value in betting because it's unlikely a worse hand calls, and I could potentially be walking into a trap or a better pair like 88-KK. Checking back allows me to reach showdown and win the pot with my pair of sevens.

Open from the button with 2♦3♦ — a speculative, suited connector that plays very well in position. Limping hands like this surrenders initiative and gives both blinds cheap odds to see flops; a standard 3x open gives fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with 39bb effective. Raise to 300 (3x BB): this is a normal sizing that balances stealing the blinds and allowing postflop play if called. Plan: if either blind 3-bets big, fold this hand (no reason to get committed with 2-3 suited against a strong 3-bet); if we get single or double calls, use position to realize equity and pressure turns/river. Overall this maximizes EV by leveraging position and fold equity while preserving stack depth for postflop play.

5-4 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and I'm in the small blind facing a 250 call. Even accounting for the small blind I already posted, calling with 54o out of position against a button raiser is unprofitable. I don't have the hand strength to justify investing more chips, and I'd be out of position postflop if I did call. This is a straightforward fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind holding Queen-Eight offsuit. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. Given the pot odds (calling 200 more into a pot of 450) and the 3-handed nature of the game, Q8o is a standard defending hand against a Button open. Minni is relatively short-stacked with 3600 chips, and I have a comfortable stack. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate my position then.

On an Ace-high board like As 9c 3c, the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) has a significant range advantage. I have no piece of the board with Queen-high. Standard play out of position is to check to the raiser and see how they react.

Flop: As 9c 3c. I hold 3♦2♦ — middle pair (trey) with a weak kicker, but I’m in position and Flash’s caller range is wide (A‑x, mid pairs, suited connectors, some 2‑club combos). I want to get value from worse pairs and charge club/overcard draws while denying free cards that can outdraw me. A small bet (~1/3 pot) achieves protection and extracts value without bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: bet 200 to charge draws and weaker pairs; fold to a large check‑raise (preserve stack), if called proceed cautiously on the turn and reevaluate based on his range and action.

On this Ace-high board (As 9c 3c), the pre-flop aggressor has a significant range advantage. I have Queen-high with no draws and no pieces of the board. Although the bet of 200 into 650 is relatively small, I have very little equity and am out of position. It is best to fold and wait for a better spot rather than calling with air.

JJ is a premium pocket pair and I'm in the strongest position (Button). With 29,575 chips, I have a healthy stack relative to the effective stacks of my opponents (Flash: 6,125, Minni: 4,150). I need to call 100 to stay in, and raising is the standard play here. A 3x raise to 300 chips total is appropriate sizing - it builds the pot from my strong hand, likely wins the blinds if they fold, but isn't so large that it commits me to the hand. I expect to get value from weaker hands that call, and I maintain position for post-flop play if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit. Claude, the button, has raised to 300. This is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against a large stack like Claude. Calling would also invite Minni to squeeze or call from the Big Blind. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I’m on the big blind (100) facing Claude’s raise to 300 with 3h2d — a very weak, unplayable hand out of position. Pot is 450 and it’s 200 to call; calling 200 into 450 gives ~30.8% break-even equity (200/(450+200)). 3-2 offsuit has poor equity and terrible playability OOP against a button opening range, and I’ll be folding to continuation bets or getting exploited postflop. A 3-bet bluff here would be thin against a deep, experienced opener and unnecessary with 41.5BB — I can wait for a better spot. Fold and preserve stack for a profitable spot later.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (5s 5c). This is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game, particularly on the button. I want to enter the pot and ideally take the lead or see a flop with a hand that has set-mining value and some showdown equity as a pair. Raising is the standard play here to seize the initiative and potentially win the blinds or isolate one of the opponents.

I have pocket 6s in the small blind facing a 300 button open (I’ve already posted 50, so calling is 250 more to make 300 total). With 4100 effective I have good implied odds to set-mine. I’m out of position to the button and there’s a very deep big blind behind who may come along — 3-betting out of position (even to the min of 500) is marginal: it has limited fold equity and often just creates a bloated multiway pot I don’t want to play OOP with a medium pocket pair. I’ll call 250 more (300 total) to see a flop, look for a set or a favorable flop texture, and then play cautiously OOP — avoid bloating the pot unless I improve or get good read-driven spots.

K♠ 2♥ is one of the worst hands in poker. While facing only 200 more to call, this hand has poor equity against two opponents, terrible playability post-flop, and I'm out of position. No reason to put chips at risk with a weak hand when my stack is healthy and I've been playing disciplined poker. Fold is the clear choice.

I called preflop to set-mine. Flop is 10♦-7♦-2♦ and I hold 6♠6♥ — an underpair with no diamond on a monotone board. A large portion of the button’s opening range contains diamonds and tens, so many hands already beat me (made flushes or top pair). Betting here has almost no fold equity and only bloats a pot I'm out of position in, so I should control the pot size and gather information. Plan: check and either call a small c-bet (~≤1/3 pot ≈230) to see a turn (I have 2 outs to a set and some showdown value vs air), but fold to large overbets (>≈1/2 pot) unless I pick up a strong read. If checked to me, I’ll check back and try to realize equity or hit my set on the turn.

I have a pocket pair of fives, but the flop is monotone with all diamonds (2d, Td, 7d). I do not hold a diamond myself, meaning I have no redraw to a flush. While I currently have a pair, it is vulnerable to any higher card or any diamond. Minni checked to me after calling pre-flop. Checking back allows me to see a turn card for free and keep the pot small, which is preferable given my lack of a diamond and the high probability that Minni's calling range contains at least one diamond or a better pair.

Board: 2♦ T♦ 7♦ A♥; I hold 6♠6♥ — an underpair with no diamond and only two outs to hit a set on the river (2/46 ≈ 4.35%). Flash checked the flop and checked the turn card came A♥; I have very little fold equity OOP and betting now just bloats a pot I’m almost certainly behind in. Mathematically I would only be getting correct pot odds to call a bet of ~≤30 chips on the turn to chase my two outs, so I should not invest here. Plan: check and preserve my stack — fold to any meaningful bet and only continue if I actually hit a set on the river or see a highly unusual, tiny bet.

I hold pocket fives on a board of 2d-Td-7d-Ah. My hand is very weak as I have no diamond and the board contains three diamonds and two overcards to my pair. Minni checked for the second time. While the Ace on the turn is a good card for my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, betting into a monotone board without a flush blocker or a strong made hand is risky. Minni's range contains many single-diamond hands that will not fold to a single bet. Checking behind allows me to maintain the pot size and see if I can win at showdown against her missed draws or total air, or potentially catch a five on the river (though a five would still be vulnerable to any diamond).

I called preflop to set-mine but never improved. The flop was a monotone diamond board I don't have; the turn A♥ and river 5♥ don't help my 6♠6♥. Against the button's opening range (which contains many hands that beat me: diamonds, A, T, bigger pairs), I have very little showdown value and limited fold equity. Betting here mostly bloats the pot out of position and will get called by many hands I beat only rarely. I will check to go to showdown and conserve my stack — fold to any river raise.

On the river, I have improved to a set of fives. While the board is monotone with three diamonds (2d, Td, 7d), Minni has checked all three streets. This passivity suggests she likely does not have a made flush, or at least isn't playing one aggressively. My set is a very strong hand, and although it loses to any diamond, I should bet for value to get called by her marginal holdings like a Ten or a weak Ace that checked the turn. A bet of 400 into a 700 pot is a standard value sizing that can be called by her middle-pair or top-pair hands.

River decision: I hold 6♠6♥ on 2♦-T♦-7♦-A♥-5♥. I checked earlier and Flash (button) now bets 400. I have only two outs to a set (2/46 ≈ 4.3% equity) and this is the last street — no implied odds. Calling 400 to win the current pot (~1500 after a call) requires ~26–27% equity to be break-even, which I don’t have. Flash’s line (open, check-check through flop/turn, then a river raise) is consistent with many hands that beat me: diamonds, Tx, Ax, two-pair, or bigger pairs; there may be some occasional bluffs but not nearly enough frequency to justify a call. I planned to check and fold to any meaningful river raise. Preserve stack and fold.

Button with Q♥7♦ in a three-handed spot: this is a marginal hand but well within a wide button open-range in 3-max because of position and fold equity. Limping just invites multiway pots where Q7o loses value; a small but standard open both exerts pressure and keeps pot manageable. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) — it’s consistent with table sizing, gives me a chance to steal the blinds, and leaves plenty of stack to fold to a large 3-bet or continue in position if called. If either opponent 3-bets to a size that commits a significant portion of my 3.85k, I’ll fold; if called I’ll play postflop for position and pot control.

K9 offsuit is a marginal hand in the small blind facing a button raise. While I have a healthy stack and could theoretically 3-bet, K9o lacks the strength to play out of position against Minni's aggressive tendencies. The hand doesn't warrant defending my small blind ante given that I'd be out of position post-flop. This is a clear fold - I'll preserve my stack for stronger opportunities. With 29,575 chips, I can afford to be selective and wait for premium hands or better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 4c. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. This is a standard 3x open. My hand is extremely weak and does not play well out of position, especially given Minni's relatively shallow stack of 35.5 BBs which limits my implied odds if I were to hit a miracle flop. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

7s3c is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Despite being in position on the button, this hand has minimal equity and limited playability postflop. With a healthy stack (29,575) and no incentive to gamble with marginal holdings, folding is the optimal decision. The blinds need to act after me, so there's no advantage to calling with such a weak hand.

I am in the Small Blind with King of Spades and Jack of Diamonds. Claude (the button) has already folded. I have a strong heads-up hand against Minni in the Big Blind. Minni has a relatively short stack of 3900. I want to put pressure on the Big Blind or at least see a flop with a hand that is well ahead of an average range. Standard play here is to raise to 3x the big blind to take down the blinds or play a pot in position if called (though I am out of position post-flop, the hand strength justifies a raise).

I’m in the big blind with A♠3♥ facing a 3x open to 300 from the small blind heads‑up. A3o is a defendable hand heads‑up but is marginal to 3‑bet out of position with ~39bb effective. Calling keeps the pot under control, gives correct odds to see a flop versus a likely wide SB opening range, and preserves stack to play postflop. Plan: call to see the flop; extract value when I pair my Ace, otherwise proceed cautiously OOP and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

On a flop of 10-6-7 with two hearts, I have two overcards (King and Jack). This board texture is somewhat connected and can hit a Big Blind defending range, but as the pre-flop aggressor, I maintain a range advantage. I will place a standard continuation bet of 50% pot to test Minni's hand strength and potentially take the pot down immediately. If called, I still have six outs to top pair.

Flop: Tc 6h 7h. I have A♠3♥ — no pair but Ace-high and a single heart. Flash’s small, low-frequency bet of 300 into a ~900 pot is a standard 1/3 pot c‑bet; heads‑up SB will c‑bet a very wide range here (T, 7, 6, air, heart draws, broadways). Calling requires ~20% equity (300 to win ~1200), which A3o has versus a wide c‑betting range — plus I have outs to pair an Ace and a small backdoor heart possibility. Raising OOP with only Ace‑high is thin: I’d be trying to fold out some marginal hands but I’m vulnerable to calls and check‑raises by real pairs/draws. Folding is too weak given the price and villain’s range. Plan: call the 300 to keep the pot controlled and realize equity; on the turn re‑evaluate — fold to strong aggression on a threatening turn (hearts/paired board with big bets), value/raise if I pair my Ace or villain shows weakness (checks) and I can get value on later streets.

The turn 8d makes the board extremely coordinated. A lot of Minni's defending range (like 9x, 5x, or even 10x, 8x, 7x, 6x) has either made a straight, two pair, or a strong pair with a straight draw. My King-high and gutshot are unlikely to force a fold from anything that called the flop. Checking is the safest way to control the pot and potentially realize my equity if I hit a 9 or an overcard on the river, or to fold if Minni bets large.

Flash checked the turn after a small flop c-bet — that usually shows either a missed flop or pot-control with a marginal made hand. I hold A♠3♥: I’m not paired and the turn 8♦ effectively kills my backdoor heart (no realistic flush possibility now), so I have only one clear out (an A) to improve. Betting OOP here would be a thin bluff with very limited fold equity and exposes me to check‑raises or commitment vs hands that already beat me (T‑x, 9x, small pairs). Pot control is the correct line: check and see the river for a cheap showdown or a clearer read. If he bets the river I’ll decide based on size (call small/medium bets as a bluff‑catcher; fold to large polarized bets); if I hit an Ace I’ll extract value.

On the river, the board is highly coordinated with a possible straight (any 9) and a possible flush (three hearts). Although the Ace of hearts is a good card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, Minni has shown a tendency to call river bets with marginal hands in previous pots (e.g., Hand #105, Hand #107). My King-high has no showdown value, but the risk of a bluff being called is high given the board texture and Minni's stack size. Checking is the most prudent play to see if we can get to a cheap showdown or simply give up the hand without losing more chips.

I just paired the Ace on the river (A♠ in my hand with A♥ on board) giving me top pair after turn check-check. Flash’s line — small flop c-bet then checking turn and river — looks like a wide, often weak/pot-control range (Tx, small pairs, missed draws). Pot is 1,200. Betting a small value amount (~1/3 pot ≈ 400) extracts calls from worse hands (T-x, 7s/6s, small pairs, missed draws) while folding out nothing important and avoiding bloating the pot with a mediocre kicker. A larger bet risks folding out thin value or getting check‑raised by the few hands that beat me; checking lets him show down for free and misses value. I’ll bet 400 to get thin value and keep my stack intact; if he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a large, polarized raise).

On this highly coordinated board (Tc-6h-7h-8d-Ah), my King-high has no showdown value. The river Ace of hearts completes a potential flush and is an overcard that likely hit Minni's calling range from the flop. Minni's bet of 400 into a 1200 pot represents value, and while the odds are 4:1, I lose to any pair, any Ace, any 9 (straight), and any two hearts (flush). Given Minni's history of calling down, a bluff is risky and a call is losing. Folding is the best choice.

I am on the button with Q3s. While suited, this is a very weak hand to play from any position, even 3-handed. Minni and Claude have both shown aggression and a willingness to defend their blinds or 3-bet. Given my current stack size and the marginal nature of the hand, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up from the small blind with A8s (Ad8d). This is a strong, playable Ace suited in a heads-up spot — good equity, blocker to some premium Aces, and plenty of post‑flop playability. I want to build the pot and apply pressure to the big blind while keeping room to maneuver postflop, so I’ll raise rather than just complete. I’m sizing to 3x the BB (300 total) to reduce cheap flops and gain fold equity. Plan: if Claude calls I’ll continuation-bet on many favorable flops (especially ace or diamond-related textures) and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards; if he 3-bets big I’ll fold to large 3-bets (we’re likely dominated) and consider calling smaller 3-bets to see a flop given the suited Ace.

Folding A5o against Minni's small blind raise. While pot odds are 2:1, the hand is too weak and I'm severely out of position post-flop. A5 is vulnerable and difficult to play with positional disadvantage against an aggressive opponent. Preserving stack and waiting for stronger holdings is the correct strategy.

K♣9♣ on the button in a 3-way pot is a playable, positionally advantaged hand — suited king with decent connectivity and backdoor clubs. With 47bb effective, I want initiative and to apply pressure to the blinds rather than limp and face multiway flops out of position. A standard 3x-sized open to 300 (total) is the right sizing here: it buys fold equity, denies cheap odds to callers, and keeps postflop decisions straightforward in position. Plan: if I get one caller I’ll continuation-bet a high-frequency range on favorable flops and use position to control pot size; if both call I’ll proceed cautiously but still look to leverage my positional and flush equity. If I face a large 3-bet (size that commits a big portion of my stack), K9s is marginal and I’ll fold to preserve stack.

Calling with As4h from the small blind. Getting 1.8:1 pot odds with an Ace against a short-stacked button raiser's wide range. While As4o is weak, it has enough equity to call the additional 250 chips. Minni is short (4400) and likely pushing a wide range. The Ace has showdown value and the math supports calling.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten of Spades and Trey of Hearts. Minni raised from the Button to 300, and Claude called from the Small Blind. I need to put in 200 more to see a flop in a pot of 700. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.5:1, T3o is a very weak hand to play out of position against two active opponents, one of whom (Claude) has a massive stack and plays aggressively. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

As4h on 8h4d2s. I have a pair of fours with decent kicker. Minni is extremely short-stacked (4,400) and likely in push/fold mode. Checking is the optimal play because: (1) I gain information about her hand without committing chips, (2) my pair has showdown value on a dry board, (3) checking first from out-of-position allows me to make a better decision when she acts, and (4) if she checks through, I can extract value on later streets or see a cheaper showdown. This also controls pot size with a medium-strength hand.

I opened from the button and got one caller from the small blind. The flop 8h-4d-2s is extremely dry and rarely connects strongly with a typical SB calling range (mostly missed broadways, suited connectors and small pairs). I have two overcards (K9c) and a remote backdoor club plan, plus position — this is a high-frequency c-bet spot to take the pot down and charge any turn draws. I size to ~1/2 pot (350): large enough to fold out lots of unpaired hands and small pairs, but small enough that I can fold to a big check-raise without committing a huge portion of my 44bb stack. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and barrel selectively on safe cards; if Claude check-raises big I’ll give up K9s.

Calling 350 with As4h on a dry 8h4d2s board. I have a pair of fours with an Ace kicker against Minni's short-stacked wide range. Getting 3:1 pot odds and my pair is likely ahead of most hands she's shoving with. The min-raise could indicate weakness or medium strength, but either way, calling allows me to see the turn while maintaining good equity and pot control. This is optimal short-stacked play in the endgame.

Betting 700 on the turn with pair of fours and Ace kicker. Minni's check indicates weakness, and she's in push-fold mode with 4,050 chips. This bet extracts value from weaker holdings, gets borderline hands to fold, and puts pressure on her short stack. The dry board means she likely hasn't hit a strong hand. Bet sizing is 50% of pot, appropriate for this stack depth and situation.

I’m on the button with Kc9c. Flop (8h-4d-2s) was very dry; I c-bet 350 and Claude called. Turn brings Qc and Claude raises to 700. I have no pair — just K‑high — and only 6 immediate outs (three kings, three nines) → ~13% to improve on the river. Pot odds: calling 700 into roughly a 3500 pot (after my call) requires ≈20% equity to break even. Claude’s line (check-call flop then a small turn raise) is consistent with a polarized range: a lot of Qx / some pocket pairs for value and a portion of bluffs. The raise size is a modest probe, not a huge shove, so there will be river spots where his range contains bluffs I can exploit from position. Raising as a bluff here is too risky with no pair and opens me to a shove; folding surrenders too much equity given the pot odds and the fact I can use position on the river. I’ll call the 700, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the river — folding to heavy river pressure if I miss, or betting/bluffing when the river and his actions give me fold equity.

Betting 800 on the river with pair of fours and Ace kicker. Minni is extremely short-stacked (3,350) and checked the river, indicating weakness. This bet extracts value from her likely range of missed draws, marginal pairs, and weak holdings. Given her action pattern (aggressive pre and flop, call on turn with significant stack commitment), she likely has a medium-strength hand or complete miss. The bet sizing is approximately 28% of pot, encouraging calls from worse hands while maintaining proper value extraction in the late-stage endgame scenario.

Board is 8h-4d-2s-Qc-7c; I hold Kc9c and have missed — no pair and the river did not complete a five‑card club flush (only four clubs total). Claude’s line (check-call flop, small raise on the Q turn, small bet on the river) is far more consistent with value (Qx turned into a top pair, small sets, and now some made clubs or sevens) than with a wide bluff frequency. Calling 800 into a 3600 pot requires ≈18% equity; with no pair I have virtually zero showdown equity against his value range and it’s unlikely he’s bluffing often enough to justify a call. A raise as a bluff is too risky — it commits a large portion of my stack and will be called or shipped over by better hands. Fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

I'm on the button with pocket aces (AA), the best starting hand in poker. The blinds are 50/100 with Flash (SB) having 5,675 and Minni (BB) having only 3,250 chips. Both opponents have relatively short stacks. This is an ideal situation to build value. A standard 3x big blind raise to 300 total applies appropriate pressure while building the pot. AA should be played aggressively pre-flop to extract maximum value and reduce the chance of a lucky flop hitting an opponent. The raise also gives me positional advantage post-flop with two opponents.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. Claude, the chip leader on the button, has raised to 300. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button raise, especially with a short-stacked player in the Big Blind yet to act. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

Claude opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with 7♦7♠ and ~32.5BB effective. This is a clear defend — pocket sevens are a medium pair with good set-mining value (~12%) and strong implied odds given Claude’s very deep stack. A tiny 3-bet to 500 would give little fold equity and commits more of my stack out of position; I prefer to see a flop and play post‑flop. I’ll call the extra 200 (300 total) and proceed to evaluate on the flop: continue aggressively with a set or very favorable texture, otherwise control the pot or fold to heavy aggression.

Check. I called preflop as a set‑mine — on this Ace‑9‑5 rainbow flop my 7♣7♠ is an underpair with very little fold equity and poor redraws. Claude’s CO/button raising range contains lots of A‑x and broadways that connect with this board, and he’s extremely deep, so leading here just bloats a multi‑street pot out of position. I’ll control the pot and see a turn cheaply: if Claude checks back I keep the pot small; if he fires a small c‑bet (≈≤1/3 pot ≈215) I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn; if he barrels a large c‑bet (>≈1/2 pot ≈325) I’ll fold most of the time. If I hit a 7 on the turn I’ll shift to aggressive value lines.

I have pocket aces with one ace on the flop (5c 9d As), giving me a strong hand of trips (AAA). Minni checked on the flop despite being short-stacked, indicating weakness. This is an ideal spot to build value with my monster hand.

Minni has only 3,050 chips remaining and called my 300 pre-flop raise from the BB, suggesting he has some holding but likely not premium strength. His check on this relatively dry flop (no obvious draws) suggests he either whiffed completely, has a weak ace, or has a small pair.

I should bet to extract value. A bet of around 400 accomplishes several goals:
1. Builds the pot with my strong hand
2. Gets called by weaker hands (weak aces, small pairs, missed holdings)
3. Maintains appropriate sizing relative to his remaining stack and the pot
4. Avoids scaring him away completely while still applying pressure

This is standard value betting with a strong made hand on a favorable flop texture.

Claude opened from the button and now bets 400 into a 650 pot on A♠‑9♦‑5♣ (~61% pot). I have 7♦7♠ — an underpair to the board with no useful backdoor draws and only set‑value if I hit. Calling 400 would commit a meaningful chunk of my ~30.5BB stack for pot odds that require ~22% equity (calling 400 to win ~1450). Versus a button c‑bet/continuation range on this Ace‑high texture (lots of A‑x, some 9x, broadways and medium pairs), my sevens have well under that required equity unless Claude is bluffing extremely often. He’s deep and capable of barreling, but this size is weighted toward value on an A‑high flop. I said I’d fold most of the time to heavy aggression >~1/2 pot — that’s exactly this spot. Preserving the stack for better set‑mining or stronger holdings is the +EV line. Fold.

On the button with 3c-4c, this is a marginal suited connector that normally could be used as an opening raise in 3-handed play. However, Claude in the Big Blind has shown a high level of aggression and frequently contests pots, while Minni in the Small Blind is short-stacked (30BB) and likely to play a 'push or fold' strategy. Given these dynamics, opening such a weak hand into a dominant and aggressive chip leader like Claude is risky. I will fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening range.

Flash folded so it's heads-up vs the big blind (Claude). I'm on the small blind with K♦8♠ — a marginal but playable hand in position postflop. With 3,000 (≈30 BB) I want fold equity and to avoid playing a limp pot out of position; standard open-sizing vs a single big blind is ~2.5–3x. The min raise is 200, but sizing to 300 gives more fold equity and is my default here. Plan: take the pot if Claude folds; if he calls, play postflop in position and c-bet selectively; fold to a large 3-bet. Raise to 300 to apply pressure and play the hand in position.

I'm holding Qs2s, one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind, showing strength. I'm out of position and with a large stack already, there's no reason to get involved with this hand. Folding is clearly the most profitable decision here - Q2o has poor equity against any reasonable raise and I'm risking 200 to win only 400. This is a clear fold situation.

K3s on the button is a marginal hand but has positional and suited equity. I’ll open-raise to 300 (standard ~3x BB / table norm) to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds. Versus a large 3-bet I will fold this hand given my ~31.5bb effective stack; if called I’ll play postflop in position — c-bet on dry, high-card flops, and exercise pot control / check back on wet or highly connected boards while realizing backdoor club equity.

7-4 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Although I'm only facing a 250 chip call, this hand has very poor equity against any reasonable range. Minni's raise from the button suggests strength or a steal attempt, and calling with 7-4o would put me in a difficult post-flop position. With a healthy stack and 101 hands remaining, folding weak hands in early position (SB) is the correct long-term strategy to maximize expected value. The cost of folding (150 chips already invested) is lower than the expected loss from playing this marginal hand out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd7d. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300, which is a standard open. I have 200 more to call into a pot of 450, giving me 2.25:1 odds. J7 suited is a reasonable defending hand in a 3-handed game, especially since Claude (SB) has already folded. Minni's stack is relatively short (~28.5 BBs), which means I need to be careful with stack-off ranges, but the immediate pot odds and the playability of a suited connector-type hand justify a call to see a flop.

On this flop of 2d-6d-3h, I have a flush draw with Jack-high. As the player in the Big Blind, I should start by checking to the pre-flop raiser, Minni. My hand has significant equity, and checking allows me to see how she reacts to this low board. If she bets, I have the option to call and realize my equity or check-raise as a semi-bluff, but checking is the standard first move out of position.

I opened preflop and got one caller in the big blind. The flop (2♦ 6♦ 3♥) gives me middle pair (pair of 3s) with a King kicker — a marginal but real hand. The board is low and relatively dry, though two diamonds introduce some draw combinations (Adx, KdQd, etc.). As the preflop aggressor in position I should c-bet to get value from weaker holdings (A/K highs, floats) and charge diamond draws while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll make a half-pot continuation bet (~350 chips) — large enough to fold out pure air and punish draws, but small enough that I can fold to a strong check-raise or proceed cautiously if called. Plan after this: if Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll evaluate the turn (check to pot-control on a diamond or scary overcard, continue for value on blanks) and fold to significant aggression representing sets/two-pair.

I have a Jack-high flush draw on a low, disconnected board. Minni has bet 350 into a 650 pot. The pot odds are approximately 3.8:1 (350 to win 1350), and I have about a 35% chance to hit my flush by the river. Calling is a mathematically sound decision to realize my equity. Since Minni is relatively short-stacked, I don't want to bloat the pot with a check-raise right now and potentially face a shove, especially since my Jack-high currently has limited showdown value if the draw misses.

I have just completed a Jack-high flush on the turn with the Ace of Diamonds. The board is currently 2d-6d-3h-Ad. As I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check to her and allow her to continue her aggression, especially since the Ace is a card that likely hits her range as the button raiser. If she bets, I can then decide whether to call to keep her bluffs in or raise for value, considering her remaining stack of 2500.

Turn is Ad — an overcard to my King and the third diamond on board (2♦6♦3♥→Ad), which makes two‑diamond hole cards a completed flush. I have K♣3♣: middle pair with a King kicker, which is now reverse‑dominated by A‑x, vulnerable to sets/two‑pair and crushed by any two‑diamond hands. Flash checked the turn; given the scary nature of this card and my marginal holding, I want to keep the pot under control, realize equity, and allow bluffs to show themselves on the river rather than build a pot I’m unlikely to comfortably value‑bet. I’ll check back the turn. Plan: evaluate the river — bet small for thin value on very passive rivers, call small/medium bets if they look like bluffs or donk bluffs, and fold to large value-sized bets that represent a made flush/two‑pair/set.

I have a Jack-high flush on a board that is not paired, meaning I have a very strong hand. Minni's check on the turn, after the Ace of Diamonds completed the flush draw, suggests she either has a marginal hand (like an Ace) she wanted to take to showdown cheaply, or she was giving up on a bluff. By betting on the river, I am looking to get value from hands like Ax, sets, or two-pair. A bet of 750 into a 1350 pot is a standard value size that can be called by her holding an Ace or better.

Pot is 2100 and Flash leads the river for 750; calling 750 gives me ~26% break-even equity (750 / (2100+750) ≈ 26%). I hold K♣3♣ — second pair with a decent kicker but vulnerable to A‑x, any two‑diamond combos that now make a flush, and higher pairs. Flash’s line (call preflop, call my flop c‑bet, both checked the Ad turn, then a small ~1/3‑pot river stab) looks like a polarized small‑value / bluff mixture — small river sizing is frequently used as a probe or thin value bet. True nut flushes (two diamonds) would require both hole cards to be diamonds and would already have been made on the turn; while possible, those combos are relatively rare and he checked the turn which reduces the likelihood of a slow‑played nut flush. Raising here would be reckless with a marginal holding and would commit too many chips when behind a lot of the value range. Folding concedes too much equity given the price.

Given the pot odds and that I beat many missed overcards and weak Kx/Qx type holdings, I will call the 750 to catch bluffs and weaker value bets. If I lose I’ll muck; if I win I pick up a decent small pot without bloating the pot myself.

Raising with K♦ A♠ from the button is standard. With two relatively short-stacked opponents and my premium hand in position, a 3.5x BB raise to 350 is appropriate. This builds the pot, applies pressure, and keeps the action manageable while maintaining an aggressive posture. I'm well-positioned to win this pot preflop or take it down postflop if called.

I have Ace-Nine offsuit in the Small Blind. Claude, who is the table chip leader, has raised to 350 from the Button. While A9o isn't a premium hand, it is strong enough to play against a button open in a three-handed game. However, with Minni in the Big Blind being extremely short-stacked (only 1650 chips total), any raise from me might commit her or put me in a spot where I have to play for her entire stack. Given the stack dynamics and the strength of my hand relative to a typical button opening range, I will call the 300 to see a flop, keeping the pot manageable while remaining flexible.
