Facing a button open to 350 with the SB calling, I’m on the BB with 3♦4♣ — a very weak, unplayable hand out of position. Pot is 800 and I must call 250 (break-even equity ≈ 24%). Against a BTN open and an SB cold-call, my actual equity with 3-4off is well below that (likely in the low teens), and postflop I’ll be dominated or forced to fold to c-bets or face multiway action. A min-raise or shove is also unattractive — I’d be risking most of my short 1,650 stack with a hand that has poor showdown and fold-equity prospects. Preserve chips, wait for a better spot. Fold.

On this flop of Jh Qs 7d, I have Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw. Claude, as the pre-flop raiser, will likely have a range that hits this board well, containing many pairs (Queens, Jacks, Sevens) and straight draws (KT, T9). However, I have checked to him as the caller. I need to see how he reacts to this board. Since I am first to act on the flop and I missed, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. If he bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent move.

I have A♠ K♦ on a J♥ Q♠ 7♦ flop with Flash in the SB checking. This is an excellent spot to bet for value. I have premium cards with strong equity: overcards, a gutshot to the broadway straight (any T completes A-K-Q-J-T), and I'm in position against a single short-stacked opponent. Flash's check indicates weakness, and they called preflop from the SB, suggesting a medium-strength hand that likely whiffed this flop. A bet of 450 (roughly 56% of the pot) applies appropriate pressure, builds the pot, and keeps Flash's short stack committed if they want to continue. This represents balanced play—taking down the pot when possible while building it when called.

On a board of Jh-Qs-7d, the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) has a range that significantly overlaps with these high cards (Queens, Jacks, and straight draws like KT/T9). My Ace-high (Ah 9c) has very little equity against this range and no substantial draw. Claude's bet of 450 into 800 is a standard continuation bet, and given that I am out of position with a weak holding that didn't connect with the board, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on the button, which is a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is relatively short (67.75 big blinds), while Minni is very short-stacked (16 big blinds). Claude is the massive chip leader. Since I'm on the button, I should raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially isolate Minni or take down the blinds immediately. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate.

I’m in the small blind with K♦8♠ (marginal offsuit K) facing a button open to 3bb. Pot is 450 and it’s 250 to call; I only have ~16bb effective. Calling would leave me short and out of position vs the button and likely to face a cold-call or shove from the big blind, making postflop play difficult. A small 3-bet is half-hearted and a shove is too loose with K8o because the button will call wide from a deep stack. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold here.

Flash has a critically short stack (~21 big blinds) from the button and is in push/fold mode. TK is a decent broadway hand with good equity against a wide range. Rather than call and play postflop out of position, raising to 600 applies immediate pressure, either winning the pot or getting Flash all-in in a situation where I have reasonable equity. This is a standard aggressive play against short-stacked opponents in position.

I have AQo on the button, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. My initial open raise to 300 was met with a small 3-bet to 600 from Claude in the Big Blind. Claude is the massive chip leader and has been aggressive, but this sizing is an 'invitation' or a 'click-back'. Minni is very short with only 16BB. Given my stack size of 64.75BB, I have two main options: calling to play a pot in position or 4-betting. A 4-bet here likely commits me or sets up a shove on most flops. However, Claude's small 3-bet could be a trapping hand or just a standard aggressive move with a wide range. Calling is safe and allows me to use my positional advantage, but 4-betting for value is also strong. I will choose to 4-bet to 1600 to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand right now or narrow Claude's range.

TK is a decent broadway hand with reasonable equity (~42-50%) against Flash's likely 4-bet range from a short stack. The pot odds of 3.25:1 are favorable and require only 23.5% equity to be profitable. Calling allows me to see a flop and make better decisions with more information. Folding here unnecessarily gives up the pot when I have a playable hand with equity advantage or at worst near-parity against a wide range.

Flash is critically short-stacked with ~5,175 chips remaining. On the 8-7-A flop, TK represents broadway cards with 40-50% equity against his likely 4-bet range. Rather than check and allow a free card, betting 2,000 applies pressure that either wins the pot immediately or forces Flash all-in in a situation where I have near-parity equity at worst. This is a stronger play than checking, which would allow Flash to realize equity on the turn/river with hands I'm competing against. The bet amounts to about 61% of Flash's remaining stack, forcing a binary decision from a critically short opponent.

I 4-bet pre-flop with AQo and hit top pair on an Ace-high board. Claude's lead of 2000 into the 3250 pot is an unusual 'donk-bet' from the out-of-position caller in a 4-bet pot, which often indicates extreme strength (like a set of 7s or 8s) or a high-equity draw/marginal hand trying to set the price. However, Claude has consistently played very aggressively. With only 5175 chips remaining and the pot already at 5250, I am mathematically committed to this hand with top pair. Shoving now protects my hand against various draws and ensures I realize my equity. Folding is not an option given the pot odds and my hand strength, and calling leaves me with a awkward stack-to-pot ratio on the turn. I will move all-in to maximize pressure.

Call Flash's all-in raise. I have TsKd on an 8c-7s-As flop against a critically short-stacked opponent playing push-fold poker from the button. My broadway hand has 40-50% equity against his likely range, well above the ~19% equity needed to break even on pot odds of 4.3:1. This is a mathematically sound call with positive expected value.

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should be playing aggressively. 8s5s is suited and has good postflop playability and fold equity heads-up, so I’ll open rather than limp. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) — that applies pressure, narrows Claude’s calling range, and builds a pot I can play for position. Committing 300 leaves me with ~1250 behind; if called the pot will be ~400 and I’ll c-bet most flops, folding to heavy 3-bets or large turn/river pressure when I have little equity.

Raising to 800 with KsQs against a short-stacked opponent. Minni has only 1,300 chips total and just raised to 300, indicating desperation. This raise exploits their desperation while extracting value from my premium hand. The raise is substantial enough to put pressure on Minni while likely creating a critical decision point that favors my strong equity.

Claude 3-bet to 800 after my 300 open. I have 8s5s — a suited, playable hand heads-up with good postflop equity and position (I’ll be on the button after the flop). Folding is too weak given the pot odds and the hand’s playability; shoving to 1300 (the min-raise) would be an all-in bluff but has limited extra fold equity here because Claude only has to call an extra 500 out of a very deep stack. Calling 500 more (total committed 800) keeps me in position, realizes equity with flush/straight potential, and preserves 800 behind to leverage postflop — the most +EV line against a wide/aggressive 3-betting range. I’ll call.

On this flop of AcAh8c, my KsQs has missed significantly. I'm facing an opponent with only 800 chips remaining, and the presence of two aces on the board is extremely dangerous—Minni likely has at least one ace given their call of my preflop raise, or a pocket pair that would dominate my unpaired hand. Checking allows me to: (1) see how Minni acts with their short stack, (2) avoid bloating the pot further when I'm likely behind, and (3) potentially win chips if Minni has a complete miss and checks back. If Minni bets their remaining chips, I can evaluate the situation more clearly, but for now, a check is the most prudent play with a weak flop for my hand and a short-stacked opponent likely holding better equity.

I have 8♠5♠ on A♣ A♥ 8♣ — that’s two pair, Aces and Eights. It’s a very strong hand heads‑up but vulnerable to any Ace, any 8, and many high pocket pairs. Claude’s preflop 3‑bet makes his range heavy in Aces/premium pairs, but he checked the flop (could be slow‑play or a medium hand). I only have ~800 behind (≈8BB) and the pot is 1600. With such a short stack the correct line is to push now: an all‑in maximizes value against worse hands (Kx, broadways, medium pairs) and uses my remaining fold equity to win the pot immediately. A small bet or checking back surrenders fold equity, allows free cards, and leaves awkward turn/river decisions. Shoving 800 puts Claude to a 33% equity decision (call 800 to win 2400), which will make many of his non‑Ace/8/strong‑pair combos fold and extracts value from those that call. I’ll shove all‑in for 800.

Folding KsQs on AcAh8c flop. My hand has completely missed and is highly vulnerable to Minni's preflop call and flop aggression. With two aces on board and out of position, this is a clear fold situation. Minni's range heavily skews toward strong hands (Ax, high pairs), and I have minimal equity. No need to invest additional chips in a losing situation.

6-3 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. While I'm in position on the button with a significant chip advantage over Minni (37550 vs 2300), this hand simply doesn't warrant a call. Even in heads-up play where ranges are wider, 6-3o is too weak to profitably continue with. The hand plays poorly postflop and lacks both high-card value and straight/flush potential. With a commanding stack, I can afford to fold marginal and weak hands, waiting for better opportunities to accumulate chips against a short-stacked opponent. This is a clear fold.

2♠8♥ is one of the weakest hands in heads‑up play. Although I have position as the button, my stack is only ~24bb and Claude is a very deep, active defender who calls/3‑bets enough that my raise (to 200) would need >50% fold equity to be +EV — unlikely. Calling 50 to see a flop is cheap but this hand has very poor postflop playability and will be dominated most of the time. Preserve chips for better spots and concede the small blind rather than invest more. Folding minimizes downside and keeps me in position to pick better hands later.

I'm holding 7-2o, typically the worst starting hand in poker. However, this situation has several critical factors that make calling correct:

1. **Pot Odds**: I call 50 to win 150 (including the existing pot), giving me 3:1 odds - excellent equity for any showdown.

2. **Minni's Stack**: With only 2,300 chips remaining, Minni is extremely short-stacked relative to the blinds. This means they'll likely play wide ranges and potentially go all-in soon.

3. **Position Advantage**: I'm on the button (small blind) with position post-flop, allowing me to play more hands profitably against short stacks.

4. **Playability**: Even 7-2o has some showdown value and can connect with flops. More importantly, if Minni checks the flop, I can apply pressure with position and fold equity.

5. **Hand Selection vs. Short Stacks**: Against a short stack, traditional hand strength matters less - stack dynamics and fold equity become the primary factors. Calling 50 to see a cheap flop against a desperation stack is +EV.

I'll call the 50 and play the flop accordingly based on Minni's action and the board texture.

Heads-up, I’m in the big blind with 6♠2♥ — one of the worst hands. Pot is already 200 and there’s nothing to call, so I can either check or make a small minimum raise to 200. With my stack ~2300 (≈23bb) and Claude extremely deep, a min-raise here (only +100) has limited fold equity — he limped/completed, so he’s likely to call wide. I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with such a weak hand or open myself to uncomfortable post-flop decisions against a deep, active opponent. I’ll preserve my stack and see a flop for free, folding to significant aggression if the board and action warrant it.

I have 6♠2♥ on T♣4♠2♦ — bottom pair (deuces) with a 6 kicker. Claude limped preflop so his range is wide (lots of A/K/Q/Jx and weaker unpaired hands I currently beat) but also contains tens and many pocket pairs that dominate me. The pot is small (200) and my stack (~2,300 ≈ 23bb) means I should avoid bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand that can be check‑raised or played aggressively by a very deep opponent. Checking controls the pot size, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and allows me to call small/medium bets for thin value while folding to large aggression or a check‑raise. If Claude checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and look to extract or pot‑control depending on the card.

Flop shows 4s-Tc-2d and I have 7d-2c, giving me a pair of deuces. Minni checked from the big blind with only 2,300 chips remaining - classic weakness. Betting 150 applies solid pressure on this dry flop and their desperate stack situation. Either they fold and I win the pot, or they call and I have a reasonable hand to showdown. This is the correct play against a short-stacked opponent showing weakness on the flop.

Heads‑up on the button I have 6♠2♥ on T♣4♠2♦ — bottom pair with a 6 kicker. Claude called preflop (wide range) and c‑bet to 150 into a 350 pot, so his range contains a lot of missed overcards and bluffs as well as hands (Txx, pocket pairs) that dominate me. Raising OOP with this marginal holding against a very deep, loose opponent has little fold equity and risks bloating the pot with a hand that plays poorly facing resistance. Calling 150 gives ~30% break‑even pot odds (150/(350+150)=30%) to realize equity, keeps worse hands/bluffs in, and preserves stack for later decisions. Plan: call the flop, re‑evaluate on the turn — call small/medium bets for thin value, but fold to significant aggression or a check‑raise/shove.

I have 6♠2♥ on 4♠ T♣ 2♦ 3♣ — bottom pair (deuces) with a poor kicker. Claude barreled the flop (raised to 150) and I flat-called; out of position versus a very deep, loose/aggro opponent, leading or raising here is negative EV (little fold equity and high risk of bloating the pot with a marginal hand). I want pot control and to keep his bluffs/weaker hands in. I will check the turn. Plan: call small/medium bets for thin value or to catch bluffs (roughly bets up to ~1/3–1/2 pot depending on exact sizing), but fold to large, polarizing bets or big raises/shoves that strongly represent tens/sets or a made straight (A5 combos). If Claude checks back, I’ll re-evaluate the river for a possible small value bet or showdown.

Bet 350 on the turn. Minni's two consecutive checks indicate weakness from their short stack. My pair of deuces is likely ahead and I need to extract value while applying pressure. This bet size is proportionate to the pot (500) and their remaining stack (2,150), forcing them into a difficult situation. Against a desperation short stack, betting for value with reasonable showdown strength is correct poker.

I checked the turn with bottom pair (2♦) and a very weak 6 kicker out of position. Claude limped preflop, c‑bet the flop and now barreled the turn for 350. The table shows he’s an aggressive, deep opponent who often barrels and also often has real value here (T‐x, 3x, sets, A5/wheel combos). The pot is 850 and calling 350 would make the pot 1,200, so I need ~29% equity to justify a call. With bottom pair + terrible kicker OOP I’m behind most of Claude’s value hands and only beat many single overcards and pure bluffs. Raising as a bluff OOP is thin — I have almost no fold equity and would be risking a lot of chips with a poor hand. Given the large sizing, the line he’s taken, and my desire to control pot size with a marginal holding, folding is the +EV play. I preserve ~21.5bb to pick better spots against this very deep opponent.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Q♥7♦ — a marginal but playable hand. In heads-up I should be opening a wide range; a small, standard raise to 200 (2bb) seizes the initiative, generates fold equity against the big blind, and keeps the pot controllable with a hand that plays better in position. If Claude 3-bets big or shoves, I can fold and preserve my short stack; if he just calls I can leverage position postflop. Calling would be passive and folding is too tight, so I open to the min-raise to 200.

Calling 100 to win 300 at 4:1 odds with Td6d against Minni's wide short-stack range. My significant chip advantage, favorable post-flop position, and adequate equity make this a +EV decision. I can easily fold on the flop if I whiff, or extract value if I improve.

Betting 225 on the flop with top pair (Td6d) against a short-stacked opponent. The flop (3s 4h Th) is dry and I have a clear leading hand. Betting builds the pot, applies pressure to her marginal holdings, and gets value from her wide preflop raising range. Her 1950 chip stack makes this a critical spot - controlling the action and building the pot is key to extracting maximum value from a desperate short-stack situation.

I have Q♥7♦ on a T♥ 3♠ 4♥ flop — one overcard and a very small backdoor flush possibility, but no made pair. Claude’s small bet (225 into a 625 pot) is a standard heads‑up c‑bet and gives me ~26.5% pot odds to call (225/(625+225)). His range as a preflop caller is wide (Tx, small pairs, floats, A/K highs, some draws), so my hand has enough equity to continue: I beat a bunch of his bluffs and have reasonable equity vs his float/air. Raising to 450 as a bluff could fold some hands but will be called by many of Claude’s range (he’s deep), and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position or commit more without improving. I’ll call, keep the initiative in position, and re-evaluate on the turn (look to bet if I improve or use position/control pot if scare cards hit).

Betting 500 on the turn with top pair (Td) against a short-stacked opponent. The turn 9s doesn't significantly change the dynamics - I still have the leading hand against her wide range. This bet size applies pressure while allowing her to call with marginal holdings, extracting value from a desperate short-stack situation. The pot is now 1,350 post-bet, and her decision point is critical with only 1,225 remaining.

Fold. The turn 9♠ kills my backdoor heart plan — I’m left with Q-high and very little equity. Claude’s turn stab (500 into a 850 pot, pot now 1350) gives me ~27% pot odds to call (500 to win ~1850), but my realistic equity (only 6 clean outs to pair on the river, ~13% to hit, plus only limited fold equity vs his barreling range) is well below that. Claude called preflop and barreled flop and turn, which narrows his range toward many hands that already beat me (Ts, 9x, small sets, made pairs) or hands that won’t fold to a raise. A raise to 1,000 as a bluff would be committing a big chunk of my ~17bb stack and is likely to be called or shove‑folded by a deep opponent — poor risk/reward. Preserving my 1,725 stack for better spots is the right play, so I fold.

Playing AQ offsuit from the button in heads-up against a short-stacked opponent (Minni has only 1625 chips). This is a premium hand and I have positional advantage. With strong cards, I should raise to apply pressure and build the pot. A raise to 200 is reasonable - it represents a standard 2x raise over the big blind and will put pressure on Minni's short stack. This is a value-raising hand that benefits from aggressive play in heads-up poker.

Heads-up the SB opened to 200 (2x BB). I have 4♦3♦ — a small suited connector with decent flop playability. Pot is 300 and I need to call 100 more; that gives me ~3:1 immediate pot odds (25% break-even equity). With Claude likely opening a very wide range in position, calling preserves pot control, keeps my stack healthy (~16BB) and lets me realize my equity (backdoors, straights, occasional two-pair/sets). A light 3-bet here would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand and folding is too passive versus a wide opener. I'll defend by calling to see a flop and proceed based on texture.

Check. I have 4♦3♦ and flopped a modest made hand — one pair (treys) with a very shallow backdoor diamond possibility (two diamonds in my hand + 6♦ on the board). The flop is paired (6♥ 3♣ 6♦) so a big chunk of Claude's wide HU opening range (any 6, most pocket pairs, many overpairs) is ahead of me. With only ~15 BB effective and a 400-chip pot I want to keep the pot small and preserve my remaining stack to fold to heavy action. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me call small continuation bets to realize equity while folding to large bets/raises that represent strong hands.

I'm playing AQ against a short-stacked opponent (Minni has only 1,525 chips). On a paired board (66 with 3), my hand has good equity with two overcards and six outs to make a pair. Minni checked, indicating weakness or a missed flop.

In heads-up poker with a desperate short stack, I should apply continuous pressure. A bet of 250 accomplishes several goals:
1. Forces Minni to decide whether to commit more chips (nearly going all-in)
2. Builds the pot with a hand that has reasonable equity
3. Takes advantage of my positional strength and the fact that the paired board is unlikely to have helped them (most people wouldn't call preflop with weak hands)

This is aggressive but justified poker - premium hands in heads-up positions should be played with conviction, especially against short stacks.

I will call 250. I have second pair (3s) with two diamonds in hand and one diamond on board (a small backdoor flush possibility) — modest showdown value plus some turn/river improvement outs. The pot is 650 and calling 250 gives me ~27.8% break-even equity (250/(650+250)). Claude opened fairly wide preflop and c-bet a small-medium size on this paired board, so his range contains a lot of unpaired overcards and pure bluffs that my pair currently beats. Raising out of position with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and commit me when I’m likely behind to 6x/overpairs; folding would be too tight given the pot odds and my realizable equity. Calling preserves stack depth (~1,275 remaining), lets me realize equity and fold to heavy turn/river pressure if Claude shows strength, and allows me to extract value when he continuation-checks or barrels lightly and I improve. I'll call 250 and re-evaluate on the turn (fold to large turn shove/bet if I don’t improve and villain shows clear strength).

I have 4♦3♦ on a 6♥ 3♣ 6♦ 2♣ board — that’s two pair (6s and 3s) with a 4 kicker. Heads‑up Claude opened wide preflop and c‑bet a small amount on the paired flop, so his continuing range contains lots of overcards, middle pairs and pure bluffs as well as a smaller portion of hands that actually contain a 6 or a 3. There is only one card to come; the combos that beat me (a 6 in hand, A3/K3/etc., or the unlikely 33/66) are a minority (~16% combined), so I’m ahead of the majority of his range. I want to extract value from worse hands (Ax, Kx, smaller pocket pairs, bluffs) and deny a free river card that could give him trips/full house. I'll bet for value ~1/2 pot (450). If he calls I re‑evaluate on the river; if he raises big I will assume a very strong holding (6x/33/66) and fold to large commits (call only small thin raises depending on sizing and read).

Minni's short stack and turn raise suggests a made hand or committed range. With AQ offsuit against their likely holdings, I have reasonable equity (likely 35-50% depending on their exact hand). The pot odds of 4:1 (1,800 to call 450) are favorable enough to justify the call with two overcards and six outs. In heads-up poker against a desperate short stack, I should be calling down wider to capitalize on my position and chip advantage. Folding AQ here surrenders equity unnecessarily.

I’ve rivered two pair (6s & 4s) on 6♥ 3♣ 6♦ 2♣ 4♥ holding 4♦3♦. Heads‑up Claude opened wide preflop, c‑bet the paired flop and then called my turn raise — his continuing range is full of AX/KX, single pairs and some medium pocket pairs/bluffs; only a minority of combos (any 6, pocket 44/22, or rare quads/full houses) beat me. The pot is 1,800 and my remaining stack is 825, so I can’t make a small half‑pot river — the right line is to shove for value. Jamming maximizes value vs the many worse hands that will call (and extracts fold equity), while checking just surrenders value and allows river bluffs/turnovers to decide things. I accept that a caller/raiser with trips/full house beats me, but overall an all‑in here is +EV given the range and stack dynamics.

Heads-up all-in decision with AQ offsuit on river (6h 3c 6d 2c 4h). Minni is all-in for 825. Pot odds of 3.18:1 require only ~24% equity to break even. AQ has reasonable equity (35-45%) against Minni's likely range of weaker pairs, high-card hands, or bluffs given their short stack desperation. With two overcards and favorable odds, calling is the mathematically sound play.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with K♦J♦ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 to complete, but limping/passive play gives up initiative and invites the BB to take control. With ~34bb effective, I want to raise for value and to isolate the BB. A standard 3bb sizing (raise to 300) is consistent with the table, extracts value from worse hands, and leaves room to maneuver postflop. If Claude 3-bets big I can fold; if he calls I’ll play postflop aggressively in position.

Folding T2o is the standard play from the big blind against a short-stack raise. Despite 2:1 pot odds, this hand lacks showdown value and I'm out of position. With a large chip stack (36,450) and Minni critically short (3,150), I can afford to play tight and wait for better opportunities to accumulate chips.

Raising with KQ offsuit in heads-up on the button against a critically short-stacked opponent. With a 10:1 chip advantage and a premium hand, I should apply maximum pressure. A raise to 300 (3x BB) is standard and forces Minni to make an immediate decision from their desperate position. They'll likely fold most of their weak hands or be forced to shove with marginal holdings, where KQ has strong equity. This is optimal position and hand strength to exploit their situation.

Claude opened to 300 from the button (SB). I’m in the BB with K♠ T♦ offsuit — a strong heads-up hand but not strong enough to commit large stacks. Calling 200 more (total 300) keeps the pot controllable, realizes my equity vs Claude’s likely wide open range, and preserves stack depth (I have ~34.5bb). A tiny 3-bet to 500 would be too weak an isolation and a larger 3-bet or jam would risk too much with a marginal holding OOP. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — continue aggressively if I pair or get strong texture, otherwise fold to heavy aggression.

Preflop I flat-called Claude’s 300 open to keep the pot controlled with K♠T♦ out of position. The flop J♥‑8♥‑3♦ leaves me with only one overcard (K) and some very remote backdoor possibilities — no pair, no immediate draw. Claude, opening a lot from the button, will c‑bet a wide range here, so checking is the prudent play: it controls the pot with SPR ≈ 5, lets me realize my showdown equity, and induces bluffs I can call or punish on later streets. Plan: check and call a small continuation bet to see a turn, fold to heavy aggression that prices me out, and if I pick up a K or T on the turn or Claude checks back, take a value/pressure line accordingly.

Standard continuation bet on dry flop. With KQ against a short-stacked opponent who checked, applying pressure is optimal. Betting 400 (2/3 pot) forces them to make a decision while I maintain aggressive control with my massive chip advantage.

Claude opened from the button and I flat-called preflop to keep the pot controllable with K♠T♦. On J♥‑8♥‑3♦ I checked and Claude c‑bet 400 into a 1000 pot (to call 400). Calling keeps the pot small with a marginal, but playable, heads‑up hand OOP and lets me realize my equity vs Claude’s likely wide button range. Pot math: calling 400 gets me 1400 to win (≈28.6% required equity); my two‑card equity to pair by river with 6 outs is ≈24.1% — a little below break‑even, but Claude c‑bets a lot from the button and this size is small enough to justify a call to catch K/T or to fold to heavy aggression. I won’t inflate the pot OOP with a bluff‑raise here; if the turn bricks and Claude fires a large bet or min‑raises to 800+, I’ll fold. If I pair a K or T on the turn I’ll proceed aggressively for value; if Claude checks back I can take a stab later. Call 400 more to see the turn.

I just improved to top pair (T) with a K kicker on the turn (board J♥‑8♥‑3♦‑T♣). Claude opened wide from the button and c‑bet small on the flop, so his range is likely broad (bluffs, weak pairs, some Jx and heart draws). Heads‑up this is a clear spot to take the lead for value and protection: bet to charge heart draws and worse pairs/overcards and to extract thin value from Kx, weaker tens, and many Jx that will call. I’ll size to about half the pot — large enough to deny equity and get value, small enough not to commit my stack OOP if Claude jams. Betting 700 into the 1400 pot leaves me room to maneuver. Plan: bet 700; fold to an extremely large raise/jam given stack disparity, call a single‑raise if reasonably sized and re‑evaluate river if called.

Calling the 700 chip raise on the turn with KQ offsuit. Minni is desperately short-stacked (2,150 remaining) and just raised to 700. With my massive chip advantage (35:1 ratio), I can afford to play this situation out. Even if Minni shoves the remaining 1,450 on the river, I'll be getting 3:1 pot odds which requires only 25% equity. KQ against a short-stack shoving range has substantially more equity than that. This maximizes my positional and chip advantage while maintaining flexibility to see the river and make river decisions with more information.

I hold K♠T♦ — I turned top pair with a very strong kicker. Claude called my turn raise, so his continuing range is weighted toward hands that often beat me (Jx) plus some 8x/3x and missed heart draws. The river 5♦ is a blank that doesn’t change much. Betting now would only thinly extract value from a few worse tens or small pairs, while mainly getting called by Jx (which beats me) or pressured by Claude’s massive stack. With the pot already 2,800 and my stack only 2,150, leading the river risks committing a big portion of my stack for little extra EV and opens me to a big raise that’s hard to call. I’ll control the pot and take the showdown — check and show down my top pair. If Claude then barrels a small amount I’ll seriously consider a call for value; I’ll fold to an overbet/shove from him.

Checking the river with KQ high. Minni's turn raise (700 on Tc) indicated hand strength, and their river check suggests either a bricked draw or marginal made hand. With a 16:1 chip advantage and 22 hands remaining, I prioritize showdown certainty over bluffing marginal equity. KQ has decent showdown value as a bluff-catcher, and betting would only get called by superior hands given Minni's short-stack mentality. Checking realizes showdown and avoids turning my hand into a bluff against an opponent who already committed chips on the turn.

Heads-up on the button with Q♥2♠. In heads-up play the button can open very wide — Q2o is marginal but perfectly fine to raise for position and fold equity. The min-raise to 200 (2x BB) is standard: it pressures the big blind, keeps the pot manageable with my ~49BB stack, and lets me play postflop in position if Claude calls. If Claude 3-bets large I can fold and preserve my stack; if he calls I plan to c-bet many flops and use positional advantage to realize equity and fold out weaker holdings. Raise to 200 to seize initiative.

Folding Q6o. While I have a chip lead and Minni is short-stacked, this hand is too weak to get involved. Q6 has poor equity against a button raiser's range, and as the chip leader, I can afford to play selectively and win through attrition. No need to chase marginal spots when I have positional and chip equity advantages in general.

Raising with 6-5o from the button in heads-up play against a critically short-stacked opponent. The position advantage and Minni's chip desperation make this a clear raising spot. Even a weak hand like 6-5 benefits from the aggressive positional play and puts immediate pressure on her. A min raise to 200 forces her into a difficult decision while maintaining chip efficiency.

Heads-up I can defend fairly wide. Q9o is a reasonable defending hand against a 200 open from the button. The pot is 300 and a call costs me 100 (4:1 pot odds) — good odds to see a flop with a playable hand. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet; calling keeps his range wide and lets me realize equity cheaply. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed based on texture (value/bet top pairs, pot-control or fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, look for spots to bluff when equity improves).

Heads‑up on an A♣9♣8♠ flop I hold Q♦9♠ — middle pair (second pair) with a mediocre kicker and no club to contest the two‑club board. Claude opened wide from the button preflop, so his range contains a lot of A‑x, broadways and speculative hands; he will c‑bet frequently. Out of position I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that is often behind (any A, any 9 with a better kicker, or two‑pair/sets). Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me call down against bluffs and small/medium continuation bets where my pair has good showdown value, and fold to heavy aggression that polarizes to very strong hands. Plan: check; call small‑to‑medium c‑bets to realize equity and extract value from worse nines/8x/air, but fold to large raises or overbets. If Claude checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the turn and use pot‑control or value bets only when I improve.

Aggressive continuation bet with position and fold equity. I have 6-5o with no equity on A-9-8, so my only path to victory is forcing her to fold now. Minni's check from the big blind suggests weakness. A bet of 250 (62% of pot) applies strong pressure on her short stack. She's desperate and likely to fold most hands that aren't made. This is standard aggressive play in heads-up position rather than giving a free card to a desperate opponent.

Heads‑up, out of position on A♣‑9♣‑8♠. I hold Q♦9♠ — second pair with a decent kicker. Claude opened wide from the button preflop so his c‑bet range will include many A‑x, some better 9x/8x, and a lot of misses. He bet a fairly large sizing (~60% pot) but I’m getting ~2.6:1 pot odds to call (250 to win ~650), and calling is a low‑risk way to realize equity and extract value from worse 9s, 8x, and bluffs. Raising OOP with just second pair bloats the pot and commits me with a marginal hand; folding here would be too nitty given the price. Plan: call the flop to keep the pot manageable, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy, polarizing aggression (overbets/raises) or continue vs small/medium bets and look to value‑bet or bluff‑catch if I improve or the villain shows weakness.

I have Q♦9♠ (second pair) on A♣-9♣-8♠-10♣. Claude opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn. With no club in my hand my 9 is vulnerable to A/10/better 9x, sets, and the rare made two‑club flush (he’d need two clubs). Betting OOP here risks getting check‑raised or bloating the pot with a marginal holding against a deep, aggressive opponent. Pot control is the priority — my hand has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to build a big pot. I’ll check and evaluate river action: call reasonable bets from worse 9s/8x and missed draws, but fold to large overbets or polarized aggression.

Turn position bet applying fold equity pressure on short stack. Minni's turn check indicates weakness after calling flop. With 6-5 air, fold equity is critical to winning. Bet of 500 (55% pot) forces her to make a decision with a desperation stack. She either folds most holdings or commits remainder of stack, where I maintain positional and mathematical advantage.

I’m OOP with Q♦9♠ — second pair on A♣‑9♣‑8♠‑10♣. Claude opened wide preflop, c‑bet the flop and now bets the turn 500 into a 1400 pot. Calling 500 to win 1400 gives me roughly 26% break‑even equity. His turn bet range includes many Ax, 10x, better 9x, sets and two‑club flushes, but also a fair number of missed hands and bluffs. My hand has decent showdown value vs the bluffs/missed draws and enough raw equity (outs to Q/9/8) to justify a call at this sizing. Raising OOP with second pair would bloat the pot and commit with a vulnerable kicker; folding is too nitty given the price. Plan: call the turn to realize equity and keep the pot controllable; on the river I’ll fold to large polarized overbets/shoves, call small‑to‑medium bets, and extract value from worse pairs or missed draws if he shows weakness.

Board: A♣ 9♣ 8♠ T♣ K♥. I have Q♦9♠ — second pair (9s) with a mediocre kicker and no club. Claude opened wide from the button, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn; that line contains a lot of A‑x, T‑x, K‑x, 9x and two‑club flushes — many hands that beat my second pair. The river King only adds more Kx value hands and doesn’t meaningfully improve my equity.

Given the pot (1,900) and my short relative stack (4,100 vs his 34k), pot control is the priority. Betting here would bloa t the pot with a marginal holding and is unlikely to get called by many worse hands (and will get raised by hands that already beat me). Checking allows me to reach showdown cheaply against bluffs and missed hands; if Claude fires a smallish value/bluff I can call, but I can fold to a large polarized shove. Optimal line: check and let him show his hand or bluff — preserve stack and avoid committing with a vulnerable second pair.

River bluff with position and aggressive line established. Minni's river check indicates weakness after calling flop and turn. With 6-5 pure air, my only path to victory is generating folds. A bet of 1,200 (63% of current pot) applies maximum pressure while being reasonable in sizing. She has 4,100 left - this forces a significant decision on her short stack. Given my consistent aggressive line and her multiple checks, this bluff has strong fold equity. Standard heads-up aggressive play in this spot.

Heads‑up, out of position with Q♦9♠ (second pair, mediocre kicker). Claude opened wide preflop and barreled flop and turn; on A♣‑9♣‑8♠‑T♣‑K♥ his line and sizing (1,200 into ~1,900) is consistent with many value combos: A‑x, Kx, Tx, 9x (better kickers), sets and a lot of club flushes (the turn completed many club combos). Pot math: he bet 1,200 and calling would cost me 1,200 to potentially win a final pot of 4,300, so I need ~28% equity to break even. My read/estimate is that my second pair is behind the bulk of his betting range and only ahead of pure air/bluffs — not enough to meet the break‑even threshold. Raising as a bluff is unattractive (would commit a large portion of my 4,100 stack and is unlikely to fold enough better hands). Preserve the stack and wait for a clearer spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with J♥3♣. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to complete, but heads-up I should be opening a very wide range from the button to take initiative and use positional advantage. J3o is marginal but acceptable to open in position with 40bb effective. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to apply pressure and pick up the pot when Claude has a marginal defend; if he 3-bets heavily I will fold, if he calls I’ll use my position to continuation-bet dry boards and control pot size on wet, connected boards.

5-9 offsuit is too weak to play out of position against an aggressive short-stacked opponent. While the pot odds are 2:1, the hand has poor equity and I'll be out of position post-flop. My healthy stack (35,800) allows me to wait for better spots. Fold and preserve chips for premium hands.

I'm on the button with K-7 offsuit against a short-stacked opponent (Minni has only 4100 chips). This is a solid opening hand from the button position. Given that my opponent is significantly short-stacked, I should raise to take control of the pot and apply pressure. A raise to 250 is standard here - it's large enough to take down the blinds if Minni folds, but also gives good value if they call or shove. This is a profitable raise given the positional advantage and stack depth situation.

Claude (button/SB) opened to 250. I’m in the BB with Q♠2♦ — a weak, marginal hand but one that has some showdown value heads-up. The pot is 350 and it’s 150 to call, so the break-even equity is ~30% (150/(350+150)). Calling is cheap and keeps the pot small out of position; min‑raising to 400 would bloat the pot with a dominated hand and invite 3-bets or heavy post‑flop pressure from a much deeper-stacked opponent (Claude has 35,550 vs my 4,100). I’ll defend the blind by calling 150 more (250 total) and evaluate the flop: fold to strong continuation aggression if I miss, proceed cautiously with any Q or draws, and avoid building a big pot with a weak kicker.

Check. On a K–J–T flop (Jc Td Ks) holding Qs2d I have only a gutshot to Broadway (A or 9) and three queens to pair — no realistic flush draw — so my equity is modest (~20–25%). Claude opened preflop, so his range is wide but contains many hands that already beat or dominate my Q (Kx, KQ, QJ, JT, TT, AJ, etc.). Out of position with a marginal hand and a much deeper opponent, I should control the pot and realize my equity rather than bloating the pot with a bluff or a thin value line. Plan: check and call small continuation bets (give myself the chance to hit on the turn), fold to large/leveraged aggression unless I improve to a Queen or a completed straight, and only get aggressive if I pick up a clear improvement.

Betting 350 on the flop with top pair (K7) against a severely short-stacked opponent (3950 remaining). The broadway flop (JTK) hits my raising range naturally and gives Minni limited options. My bet applies pressure while extracting value - they must fold most hands or commit with marginal holdings given their chip situation. This is a value bet that continues the aggression from pre-flop.

Call. Claude’s 350 into a 850 pot gives me ~29% break‑even equity (350/(850+350)=29.2%). Holding Q♠2♦ on J♣–T♦–K♠ I have 8 clean straight outs (4 A, 4 9) ≈31.5% to make a straight by the river, plus three queens to pair (some of those can still be second‑best) — so my drawing equity justifies a call. I’m out of position and Claude is very deep, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand or bluff‑raise into a wide but strong range; calling controls the pot and lets me realize equity. Plan: call the 350 more (600 total committed), see the turn, and only get aggressive if I complete a clear improvement (A/9 for the straight or a queen in a favorable spot); fold to large, committed aggression if I miss.

Check. I have Q♠2♦ on J–T–K–8: no made hand, only a gutshot to a Broadway straight (8 outs: any A or 9) plus three queens to pair (≈11 total outs ≈24% to improve on the river). Claude checked the turn after betting the flop, so he either missed or is trapping — I have no fold equity worth charging him and I don’t want to bloat a pot against a hugely deeper opponent who can pressure me off my draw. Checking lets me see a free river and realize my equity; plan on the river is to value-bet or raise only if I pair/improve to a clear winner (Q, 9 or A completing the straight), call small river bets with a made hand, and fold to large committed aggression if I miss.

Betting 500 on the turn with top pair (K-7) against a short-stacked opponent who just checked. Minni has only 3600 chips remaining and their check indicates weakness. This bet applies maximum pressure while extracting value from their likely marginal holdings. The pot is 1200, so this sized bet builds it meaningfully while keeping their remaining stack manageable for potential all-in decisions on the river.

Claude bets 500 into a 1700 pot — I’m getting 500/(1700+500) ≈ 22.7% pot odds. With Q♠2♦ on J–T–K–8 I have 8 clean straight outs (4 A, 4 9) plus 3 queens to pair ≈ 11 outs → ~24% to improve by the river, so my raw equity is just above the break‑even point. I’m out of position vs a very deep, aggressive opponent who has shown wide ranges and big bets; raising here with Q2o would bloat the pot and is likely to get me 3‑betted or played off my draw. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity, and preserves chips — I can fold to heavy river aggression if I miss or extract value if I hit a Queen/9/A that makes a clear winner. Therefore I will call the 500.

Board: Jc Td Ks 8c Ts. My Q♠2♦ missed — no pair, no flush, no straight. Claude led the river with a small 500 into 1700 (a line consistent with thin value hands like a Ten/J/K or occasional bluff), and I called the river earlier, so there is no outstanding bet to face now (To Call = 0). With essentially no showdown value and a very deep, aggressive opponent, bluff‑raising here would be a thin, high‑variance play that risks a large portion of my 3,100 stack for little fold equity. Checking to go to showdown preserves chips and is the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line given my hand and stack size. If Claude had a real value hand (T/J/K, two pair, trips) I’m beat; if he was bluffing I’ve already denied him the pot by calling. I’ll check to the river and see the showdown rather than bloat the pot with a marginal bluff attempt.

River value bet with top pair. Minni is extremely short-stacked (3100 chips) and checked, indicating weakness. My K♦ 7♠ makes pair of Kings on the J♣ T♦ K♠ 8♣ T♠ board, which is very likely the best hand. A 700 bet extracts value from marginal hands while applying pressure on their short stack. This bet sizes gives them reasonable odds to call with weaker holdings but commits a significant portion of their remaining chips.

Board: Jc Td Ks 8c Ts. My hand Q♠2♦ has missed — no pair, no straight, no flush. Pot is 2900 and Claude bet 700, so calling 700 to win a 3600 pot requires ~19.4% equity. On this river there are no cards left to improve; I only win if Claude is bluffing or value‑betting extremely thin. His line (preflop raise, continued bets on flop and turn, small river stab) is highly consistent with thin value hands (T‑x, Jx, Kx, two‑pair combos) rather than a bluff. Given his range and the small river sizing (a probe/thin‑value size that gets called by worse), I estimate his bluff frequency is below the ~19% needed — calling here is negative‑EV. Preserving my 3,100 stack for better spots and avoiding buying a coin‑flip against a very deep, aggressive opponent is the correct play. I’ll fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind). 8d3s is a very weak hand, but in a two-player pot position + fold equity make a light open profitable. I’ll make a standard, small heads-up open to 2xBB (200 total) to apply pressure and steal the blind when Claude folds while keeping the pot manageable if called. With 3050 (~30 BB) I can afford this size; I’ll fold to a sizable 3-bet and use position postflop to realize equity or continue as a c-betting range when appropriate.

With AJ from the Big Blind against a min-raising short stack, I should raise to apply pressure and build a pot in a favorable position. My massive stack advantage (36.8k vs 2.9k) allows me to play aggressively and extract value. A raise to 700 (600 more) is appropriate sizing that forces Minni's hand while building a pot I'm happy to play in.

8♦3♠ is a limp/steal open only — extremely weak, unsuited, and dominated by almost any reasonable 3‑bet range. Claude’s raise to 700 is a sizable 3‑bet relative to my 29BB stack (I must call 500 to keep 24BB effective). Calling OOP with this hand puts me in very difficult postflop spots with poor equity; shoving my stack is pure gamble against his deep stack and will be called by many better hands. I planned to fold to a sizable 3‑bet, so I’ll preserve my 29BB for a better spot and fold here.

Folding 7-10 offsuit on the button. This is a weak, unmade hand with poor showdown value. While Minni is short-stacked at 2,800, I don't need to take marginal spots with a dominated hand. With 37,050 chips and 16 hands remaining, I can afford to be selective and wait for premium hands to build my stack. Minni will be pushing wide, but I want to let better hands exploit that dynamic.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 9♠6♦ — a marginal but playable hand with position. In heads-up play I want to take the initiative rather than limp and concede the betting lead; a standard small open-raise keeps my range wide and gives me fold equity. Raising to the min-open (200 total) is size-efficient: it’s only a small build to the pot, preserves about 29 BB behind, and keeps postflop maneuverability. If Claude 3-bets big I can fold this marginal hand; if he calls I can use position to realize equity or apply pressure on favorable flops. Total to commit (including my posted 50) = 200.

Td7h is a weak offsuit connector with poor postflop playability. While Minni is short-stacked and likely playing desperately, I'm out of position and will face difficult decisions postflop. With a significant chip lead (36,950 vs 2,750) and 15 hands remaining, there's no reason to engage in marginal spots with weak holdings. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

With 9-6 offsuit on the button in heads-up play, this would normally be a marginal hand. However, the critical factor here is stack dynamics: Minni has only 2,950 chips remaining, which is approximately 30 big blinds. This is a critical short-stack situation where aggressive play is warranted.

Key factors supporting a raise:
1. Position advantage: I'm on the button with position for the entire hand
2. Short-stack pressure: Minni's 2,950 puts him in a difficult spot regardless of hand strength
3. Stack advantage: My 36,900 vs Minni's 2,950 is a massive advantage
4. Fold equity: Even if called, 9-6o has reasonable equity in heads-up play against a random hand
5. Game progression: We're at Hand 136 of 150, so late-stage aggression in favorable spots is important

A min-raise to 200 applies pressure while keeping pot odds reasonable if Minni calls. If Minni folds, I've gained uncontested equity. If Minni calls or goes all-in (likely with his short stack), I maintain position and have a chance to win a significant pot relative to his stack.

Heads-up vs a standard 2bb open to 200 from the button. Q♦6♥ is a marginal holding but worth defending from the big blind given the tiny price: pot is 300 and I need to call 100 more (≈25% pot odds). Claude has been opening frequently, so his range is likely wide enough that Q6o has reasonable equity to see a flop. With ~29.5 BB effective, I don’t want to bloat the pot or make a thin 3-bet bluff with a weak hand; I’ll call to see a flop and play straightforwardly. Postflop plan: act based on texture — fold to strong pressure on dry boards where I miss, continue more aggressively if I hit a Q or pick up good backdoors, and avoid bloating the pot out of position against a deep-stacked opponent.

Out of position with Q♦6♥ on T♠‑3♥‑3♦. I’ve got only one real equity line (three queens to make top pair) and no realistic backdoor draws, so this is a marginal holding. With ~28–29 BB effective and the pot small (400), I want to control the pot and avoid bloating it OOP — checking is the straightforward line. Plan: check and call small continuation bets to realize equity (call small bets ~≤~25–40% pot), fold to large c‑bets that look like value (roughly ≥1/2–3/4 pot or bigger), and if Claude checks back consider a delayed bet on a very favorable turn (a Q or other safe card) rather than barreling off with nothing.

Checking with 9-6o on a paired board (Ts3h3d). I have no made hand or meaningful draws. While Minni's check suggests weakness, betting here with such a weak holding is speculative. Checking lets me see another card for free, gather more information if Minni bets, and preserve chips. My position and stack advantage are sufficient without risking chips on a bluff with this specific hand.

Turn Q♠ pairs my Q♦ giving me top pair with a weak 6 kicker on T♠‑3♥‑3♦‑Q♠. Claude opened frequently and both of us checked the flop, so his range is wide and contains many hands I beat (worse Qx, Tx, overcards that will fold). This is a good “safe” card to take a small delayed-value stab to extract from worse pairs and thin value hands while keeping the pot manageable out of position. I’ll bet about one-third of the pot to realize value but avoid bloating the pot with a marginal kicker — 150 into 400. Plan: fold to a large turn check‑raise (he can have sets/two‑pair), call single raises only if very small and reassess on the river (check/call small bets, fold to big river aggression).

Calling 150 with 9-6o is justified by pot odds (3.67:1, need 21% equity). In heads-up position on the button with a massive stack advantage (36,750 vs 2,700), seeing the river is prudent. My position allows me to control the river decision, and Minni's short stack is in critical territory regardless. This hand will be decided on the river card and subsequent action - calling gives me the best information and maximum options while respecting pot odds.

River brings 6♦ and I now have two pair, Queens and Sixes (Q♦6♥ on T♠-3♥-3♦-Q♠-6♦). That’s a very strong hand heads‑up — the only real hands that beat me are full houses/quads (combinations with a 3 plus Q/T/6, or pocket pairs that turn into a boat), which are relatively rare given both checked the flop and Claude merely called my small turn bet. Claude’s line (wide open preflop, check on the flop, call of a 150 turn bet) looks like a lot of Qx, Tx and single pairs in his range that will happily call a value bet. I want to extract value without bloating the pot against the small chance of a full house, so I’ll fire a half‑pot value bet (350 into 700) to get called by worse Qx/Tx/6x hands. If he shows heavy raise/shove (polarized) I’ll treat it as likely boat/quads and fold accordingly.

Folding with 9-6 on a paired board (Ts3h3dQs6d). Although I have the minimum pair of sixes, this hand is too weak to justify calling 350 on the river against Minni's multi-street aggression. His betting pattern (raise on turn, raise on river) indicates a real hand—likely a set, two pair, or strong overpair. My pair of sixes lacks sufficient equity (~15-20%) to meet the required 27% for profitable calls at these pot odds (2.71:1). While my stack advantage is significant (36.6k vs 2.35k), poker decisions must be made on hand strength relative to opponent range, not stack dynamics alone. Folding preserves chips for better spots ahead.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 3♠9♠. This is a marginal but playable, suited hand with good postflop playability and extra fold equity heads-up. Limping/passive play here is easily exploited; I want to take the initiative and either win the pot preflop or play in position if called. A standard open to 300 (total) is consistent with my table raises and gives meaningful fold equity vs Claude without overcommitting from my ~33.5BB stack. If he 3-bets big I’ll fold or re-evaluate; if called I’ll C-bet favorable flops and control pot size on dry boards. Raise to 300.

6d-Tc is a weak hand that struggles against Minni's raising range. While I'm getting 2:1 odds on a 200 chip call, the fundamental weakness of this hand makes it unprofitable to contest the pot. In heads-up play, I should maintain discipline and fold marginal hands rather than chase pots with weak holdings. Better situations will arise.

Folding 7-2 (the worst hand in poker) from the small blind. Despite the 4:1 pot odds, this hand has poor equity and I'll be out of position post-flop. With a strong chip advantage over my short-stacked opponent (36,450 vs 3,400), I can afford to wait for premium hands and maintain a tight, disciplined approach.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. T‑2o is weak but playable in HU because of position and significant fold equity. I want a standard, market sizing that wins many pots preflop but keeps the pot manageable if called given my 35BB stack. Raising to 300 (total) is consistent with my table sizing and puts pressure on Claude's BB. Plan: if Claude 3‑bets big I will fold; if he calls I will use positional advantage postflop to continue or control the pot.

Folding 8-4o from the big blind. While Minni is short stacked and desperate, this is genuinely one of the worst starting hands. Being out of position with a weak hand against a short stack that will be forced into plays makes folding the prudent choice. With only 11 hands remaining and a significant chip lead, I can wait for better spots.

Heads-up play with positional advantage on the button. Opponent is critically short-stacked with only 3550 chips. Standard heads-up strategy requires playing a much wider range from the button. 4d6s is weak in absolute terms but profitable to raise here due to the chip advantage and position. Raising to minimum (200 total) applies pressure while managing risk.

Heads-up vs the button (Claude) who made a small open to 200. I have A♦2♦ — a strong heads-up hand with nut-ace potential and backdoor flush equity. Pot is 300 and I need to put 100 more (200 total). With ~35.5bb effective and facing a wide open from the button, calling is the best line: I realize equity, keep weaker hands and bluffs in his range, and avoid bloating an out-of-position pot by 3-betting. Plan is to evaluate the flop (attack A or diamond textures, defend selectively vs c-bets, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards).

Out of position with Ad2d on Qh Jc 5s — nut Ace but no pair and only a backdoor diamond draw. Claude opened wide from the button so his continuation-bet frequency will be high and his range contains many Qx/Jx/broadways that beat me. Leading here bloats the pot with a marginal hand and forces tough decisions if he continues; checking preserves pot control and keeps his bluffs/weaker hands in. Plan: check and evaluate — call small c-bets to realize ~12% chance to pair the Ace by river (plus ~5% to hit a backdoor diamond flush by river), fold to heavy overbets or strong turn aggression, and if he checks back take free cards and look to bet A/diamond turns or bluff later.

Checking is optimal here. With 4-6 offsuit on a Qh Jc 5s flop, I have no made hand and poor equity. Minni's flop check indicates weakness. Betting a weak hand that will often be called only by better holdings is unprofitable. By checking, I can see additional cards for free while maintaining my massive chip advantage. If I need to win, my superior stack position in this short-hand match makes checking safer than risking chips with marginal holdings.

Preflop Claude opened to 200 on the button and I defended with Ad2d. We both checked the flop (Qh Jc 5s) so his range is wide and includes many missed broadways and medium pairs — he checked rather than firing a c‑bet. Turn 3c is a blank for my hand: I still only have Ace‑high and no diamond on board. With one card to come I have ~3 outs to an Ace (~6.5%) and ~6 outs to pair either card (~13%), so very limited improvement chances. Pot is 400 and I’m out of position with ~34.5bb effective; betting here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and will get called by many better hands or check‑raised by the strong holdings I’m behind. Checking preserves pot control, keeps worse hands and bluffs in his range, and lets me realize equity cheaply. Plan: check and evaluate his turn action — call small bets on the river if he bluffs, fold to heavy aggression. I check now.

Checking is optimal with 4-6o on a QhJcJs3c board in heads-up play. I have no made hand and minimal equity. Minni's consecutive checks indicate weakness. My 10:1 chip advantage allows me to simply check down and win if Minni misses, rather than risking chips with a bluff that only wins against the weakest holdings. This preserves my dominant stack position while seeing the river.

Check. On the river (Qh Jc 5s 3c Qc) I only have Ace‑high — no pair and no meaningful blocker to Q/J that would make a bluff credible. Claude checked both flop and turn, so his range is wide: either missed broadways/Kx/AX or slow‑played Qx (which now becomes trips). The board pairing on the river reduces my fold equity dramatically (Qx won't fold, many other pairs/Jx/5x still beat me), so betting to bluff is unlikely to fold enough hands to be profitable. Conserving chips and forcing him to show or bluff is the superior long‑term line: check and fold to a value bet, or take the free showdown if he checks back.

With 4-6 offsuit on Qh Jc 5s 3c Qc, I have queen-high (worst hand). Minni's three-street check pattern (flop, turn, river) indicates significant weakness. From a heads-up perspective with positional advantage and massive chip lead (36:1), betting the river is profitable against his weak checking range. A pot-sized bet of 400 applies sufficient pressure on his short stack to fold marginal holdings while extracting value from missed draws.

I defended the big blind with Ad2d, checked down a dry board (Qh Jc 5s 3c Qc) and now face a 400 river bet into the 400 pot. Calling 400 to win 800 requires ~50% equity — I have no pair (just Ace-high) and very limited outs; most of Claude’s continuing range that checks down (Qx that now made trips, Jx, 5x, slow-played pairs) beats me. His small river bet sizing is consistent with thin value as well as some bluffs, but I don’t have enough blocker equity or showdown equity to reliably call. Raising would be a pure bluff with poor fold equity against a deep, aggressive opener and could get crushed by any Qx or Jx he turns into a value line. This meets my earlier plan: check for pot control and fold to a clear value bet on the river. I fold to conserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I already posted SB), K9o is a clear open-raise hand — good playing strength in position. I want initiative and fold equity, and a standard 3xBB open is appropriate here. I’m 34BB deep so no reason to shove; min-raising to 200 is too small (invites exploitation and cheap 3-bets), while 300 builds the pot enough to fold out weak hands but keeps worse hands in to play postflop. If Claude 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on size and his line (likely fold to a large 3-bet, consider calling a small one to play position). Limping is too passive. Raise to 300 total.

With 6s-3c from the big blind, I hold one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and while she has a short stack (3,150), that doesn't change the fundamental problem: 6-3 offsuit has minimal equity against any reasonable raising range. Even with 2:1 pot odds (call 200 to win 400), this hand plays poorly postflop and will be dominated by most hands Minni would raise with. Given my strong chip position (36,450) and the weakness of this specific hand, folding is the mathematically correct decision. I can afford to wait for better spots against a short stack.

Heads-up button position with significant chip advantage (36400 vs 3450). While 3-10 offsuit is marginal, the situation dictates action. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds and can leverage position postflop. Minni's short stack means she's likely playing wider. I should call to maintain aggression and control smaller pots with positional advantage rather than fold and cede small blinds.

Heads-up in the big blind with 8c8d. Claude (button) merely limped/called 50, which is a very wide range. With ~34.5BB effective I want to isolate and get value from worse hands (Axs, Kx, broadways, small pairs, suited connectors) while keeping the pot manageable. A standard heads-up raise to 300 total (3x the big blind) achieves that: it prices in calls from worse hands, gives some fold equity, and keeps SPR in a comfortable zone to play postflop. Plan: if Claude folds I scoop the 200. If he calls I’ll c-bet appropriate textures (bet dry flops for value, check/control on heavy overcard boards), and call modest 3-bets to realize equity — fold only to extreme shove sizes that aren’t offering correct pot odds. Raise to 300 to isolate and take the initiative.

With a significant chip advantage (36350 vs 3250) and position on the button heads-up, I should call this raise. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (200 to win 400) which is favorable for a marginal hand like 3-10o. In heads-up play, positional advantage is paramount, and Minni's short stack means she's likely playing a wide range. While 3-10o isn't strong in isolation, the situation is favorable - I can control the flop with position and leverage my chip advantage. Folding would cede too much ground in small blind situations against a short stack. This is a profitable call with good implied odds.

Preflop I isolated to 300 and Claude called with a very wide, caller-heavy range. I hold 8♣8♦ — a middle pair — and the flop T♦ K♦ 6♣ is a textbook “heavy overcard” texture that hits his calling range much harder than mine (lots of Kx/Tx, A♢x, broadways and some two-diamond combos). With ~32–33BB effective and the pot already 600, I want pot control and clear information before committing chips. C-betting here would be thin value (he calls with better and folds worse inconsistently) and makes life difficult if he raises. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity vs worse hands, and induces bluffs or thin bets I can call or fold depending on sizing. Plan: check — call a modest bet to see a turn and re-evaluate; fold to large raises or overbets that polarize to better made hands; if he checks back I will consider a delayed small bet on safe turns or check down to showdown.

Betting the flop despite weak hand (T-high). Minni's short stack (3,250) combined with my massive chip lead (11:1 ratio) makes aggressive play with positional advantage profitable. She checked, indicating a weak hand or miss. Betting approximately 60% pot (350 chips) applies pressure she likely cannot withstand given her stack size. Even if my hand is weak in isolation, winning this pot immediately has significant value in heads-up play. The goal is fold equity rather than showdown value given her desperate position.

I checked the flop to keep the pot under control out of position with an underpair (8♣8♦) on T♦ K♦ 6♣. Claude bets 350 into the ~600 pot (pot now 950), so calling 350 gives me roughly 350 / (950+350) ≈ 27% pot‑odds. Versus Claude’s wide calling range from the button (lots of Kx/Tx, some diamonds, broadways and small pairs), my underpair has borderline but sufficient equity (I estimate ~25–30%), so a call is justified. Raising here would be thin and likely commit more when I’m behind; folding relinquishes too much equity for the price. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to large turn overbets/raises that strongly polarize, but if I hit an 8 or pick up a favorable texture I can be aggressive and extract value.

Check. I have 8♣8♦ on Td Kd 6♣ Qh — an underpair with another overcard on board. I checked the flop and called Claude’s flop c-bet to keep the pot manageable; the turn queen only increases the portion of his range that beats me (Kx/Tx/Qx and broadways). Betting now would be thin value (better hands call, worse hands fold) and would unnecessarily bloat a pot OOP with a marginal hand. With 2,900 behind and a 1,300 pot I prefer pot control: check to realize equity vs his bluffs and weak pairs and commit chips only with a clear improvement or a free showdown. Plan: call modest turn/river bets from bluffs or thin value hands, fold to large polarized overbets/shoves, and if Claude checks back re-evaluate the river and possibly take a small value stab on safe runouts or check to showdown.

Top pair (tens) with short-stack opponent sitting on 2,900 chips (11:1 disadvantage). Turn is a blank that likely doesn't improve her range after her flop check. Aggressive betting continues to apply maximum pressure. A bet of 800 maintains the pressure established pre-flop and flop, forcing Minni into a critical chip decision. My substantial chip lead and positional advantage combined with a strong relative hand make this a profitable continuation. She'll fold most of her weaker range and go all-in with marginal holdings - both outcomes favor my position in heads-up play.

Call 800. I have pocket eights on T♦ K♦ 6♣ Q♥ — an underpair on a two‑overcard, two‑diamond texture. Claude limped/called wide preflop and barreled flop/turn with relatively standard sizing, so his range contains lots of Kx/Tx/Qx and some weaker hands/bluffs (A‑high, missed broadways, single diamonds). Facing 800 into a 2,100 pot gives ~27% pot odds and my hand has roughly 25–30% equity versus his wide continuing range, so a call to realize equity is +EV. Raising OOP with just an underpair would be thin and likely fold out only worse hands while getting value from better hands; folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: call to see the river, fold to a polarizing shove unless I hit an 8 or the river is a very favorable runout, and on a blank river I’ll reassess (call small/medium bets, fold large overbets).

I check to go to showdown. I hold pocket eights and the river (Td Kd 6c Qh 6s) gives me two pair (8s and 6s) — a decent holding but a vulnerable one: a huge portion of Claude’s continuing range (Kx, Tx, Qx, KQ, TT, pocket 6s, J9 straights, etc.) beats me. Claude led the flop and turn, then checked the river; that line is consistent with pot‑control from medium‑strength value hands or a balanced checking range, not an obvious bluff candidate. Betting here is thin: a small value bet will mostly get called by hands that already beat me and will rarely extract value from the pure air hands that likely folded earlier, while also exposing me to a raise that I’m uncomfortable committing to out of position. With the pot already 2,900 and no river bet to call, checking preserves my stack and forces a showdown without bloating the pot when I’m often behind. If Claude bets the river I’ll call small/medium sized bets to realize equity and fold to large polarized overbets/shoves unless the action or board (an 8) changes the picture.

River decision with top pair (tens with poor 3-kicker) against extremely short-stacked opponent (2,100 chips remaining). Minni has checked the river after calling two consecutive aggressive raises (flop and turn), which suggests she either has a moderate hand or is giving up. Checking is the prudent play here for several reasons: (1) My T3 has weak showdown value—if she called two bets, she likely has at least a pair, and my 3-kicker is vulnerable; (2) She's already extremely short and committed; (3) With 35,000 vs 2,100 (16.7:1 chip advantage), winning the pot with top pair at showdown is sufficient; (4) Betting risks going all-in only to lose to a better hand after she's already called aggressively twice. The river check secures the hand against downside risk while maintaining my dominant position. Lock up the pot and move to showdown.

Heads-up on the button with 8♦5♦ — suited and playable. With 20.5BB and the pot at 150, a small open to the min-raise (200 total) is standard: it buys fold equity, isolates the big blind’s wide defending range, and keeps the initiative while I still have position postflop. If Claude folds I take the pot; if he calls I can use position to continue on favorable flops or pot-control on bad ones. If he 3-bets big I’ll fold this marginal hand given stack depth (only continue vs a very small 3-bet or shove if appropriate).

7-2 offsuit is the weakest hand in poker. Despite having a large stack advantage over Minni (37,800 vs 1,900) and decent pot odds of 3:1, this hand has very poor equity in a heads-up situation. Even against an aggressive short stack, 7-2o is too weak to play profitably long-term. With 7 hands remaining and a strong chip position, there's no value in gambling with this hand. Folding is the correct play to preserve equity and wait for better opportunities.

Raising with QQ on the button is standard play. This is a premium starting hand and I have excellent position with a deep stack against Minni's 2,100 chip stack. A 3x raise to 300 applies appropriate pressure, builds the pot when ahead, and either wins the blinds or sets up a favorable post-flop scenario. The raise also helps define hand ranges and maximizes value.

Heads-up vs Claude (BTN) who opened to 300. I’m in the BB with K♦2♦ and 2,100 chips (~21BB). Calling 200 (300 total commitment including my BB) is the right play: K2s is a defendable heads-up hand and the pot odds are strong (calling 200 to win ~600 requires ≈25% equity). Shoving is too committal against a very deep, aggressive stack who will call light; a tiny squeeze to 500 won’t fold enough and puts me out of position in a bloated pot. I’ll call, see a flop, and proceed cautiously — continue with top pair/strong draws, pot-control or fold to heavy action on dry, bad boards.

Out of position with K♦2♦ on 8♣7♣7♠ — no pair, no club draw, essentially air. Claude opened button to 300 preflop and is very deep and capable of calling/raising light, so a flop bluff or sticky call is low-expected-value. With only ~19BB and a dry, paired board, I want pot control and to avoid bloating a pot OOP with no equity. Check and plan to fold to any meaningful c-bet; if he checks back I can take a free turn and re-evaluate.

Betting 450 is the correct play with QQ on this 8c 7c 7s board. The paired flop doesn't help most hands, and Minni's check indicates weakness. With their short stack (1,900 remaining), a moderate bet applies pressure and builds value. This is a standard continuation bet that extracts value from weaker holdings while defining my hand strength. Given Minni's desperation situation, they'll likely fold or push all-in with marginal holdings, both scenarios being favorable for my strong pair.

Fold. I’m out of position with K♦2♦ on 8♣7♣7♠ — no pair, no meaningful club draw. Claude opened preflop and now bets 450 into a 1050 pot; calling requires ≈30% equity (450/(1050+450)=30%). My real equity versus a realistic heads‑up c‑bet range (sets/7x, 8x, overpairs, club combos and many continuation bets) is well below that. With a short ~19BB stack I don’t want to bloat a pot OOP with a naked high card; raising would be too committal and unlikely to fold enough better hands. Fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Qh3d and ~18.5BB effective. In heads-up play you should be opening a very wide range — Q3o is marginal but fine to play aggressively from the button. I want to seize the initiative, build some fold equity and keep the pot manageable relative to my shortish stack, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x the BB (300). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call or shove depending on sizing and reads; if called, c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously with this marginal hand.

7-4 offsuit is the worst hand in poker and this is a clear fold from the big blind. While Minni is short-stacked at 1,600 and has been an aggressive raiser, I have 38,000 chips and can pick better spots. Even with decent pot odds, 7-4o has poor equity against any reasonable range and I'm only 5 hands away from the end of the session. No need to get involved in marginal situations when I have chips and position advantages coming up.

In this heads-up situation on the button, I'm facing Minni in the big blind with a critical short stack of only 1,900 chips. Despite holding a relatively weak hand (As5o), this is an excellent spot to apply pressure.

Key factors:
1. **Stack advantage**: I have 37,950 vs Minni's 1,900 - roughly 20:1 chip advantage
2. **Position**: I'm on the button, which gives me significant positional advantage
3. **Minni's desperation**: With such a short stack, Minni is forced to take marginal spots
4. **Hand dynamics**: In heads-up play, As5o is playable from the button, especially when I can force folds or have position

In heads-up poker strategy, aggressive button play with a big chip advantage is standard. A min-raise to 200 puts the onus on Minni to decide whether to commit a larger percentage of their short stack. If Minni folds, I win the blinds; if they call, I have position and initiative post-flop.

This is a profitable aggression spot that should help me finish the tournament strong.

Heads-up vs Claude (button) who opened to 200. I’m in the big blind with K♠9♣ and have to call 100 more (pot is 300). With ~1,900 behind (~19bb) calling keeps options and lets me realize equity — K9o is a solid hand heads-up against a wide button opening range and the pot odds are ~3:1 (I need ~25% equity). A small 3-bet to 300 wouldn’t credibly polarize and shoving is too polar (and unnecessarily risky) with this holding. Plan: call, see the flop and continue when I hit top pair, good draws or favorable texture; fold or proceed cautiously to heavy aggression if I miss.

I have K♠9♣ on Q♥7♠6♠ — just king-high with only weak backdoor outs. I don’t have a pair and my only meaningful draws are a backdoor spade flush (needs both turn+river, ≈4%) and the chance to hit a K or 9 by river (≈24%). Claude was the preflop raiser and will c-bet a wide range, but with ~19bb effective I don’t want to polarize or shove a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands in (and bluffs get through), and lets me see a turn for relatively cheap. Plan: check — call small/medium continuation bets to realize equity, fold to large overbets or shove if board and action change in my favor.

Continuation betting on the flop after Minni's check. With my 20:1 chip advantage and position, this aggressive play applies maximum pressure on Minni's short stack. A 250 bet (60% of pot) forces Minni to commit a significant percentage of remaining chips. Even if called, I retain position and equity. This is a standard aggressive play in heads-up short-stack poker designed to win the pot immediately or improve my position post-flop.

Heads-up vs the button who opened and c-bet small. I checked and Claude bet 250 into a 650 pot. I have K♠9♣ — no pair but an overcard (K) and the K♠ gives a backdoor spade possibility. Calling 250 is cheap: I’m getting ~3.6:1 on a call (need ~25–28% equity) and my hand has roughly that showdown equity vs a wide c-bet range. Raising or shoving with mere K9o is unnecessary and would overcommit; folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: call to see the turn — fold to large aggression if I don’t improve, continue/aggress when I pair the K/9 or pick up a spade or a favorable turn texture.

Turn analysis: board Q♥ 7♠ 6♠ 4♦, my hand K♠9♣. The turn kills my backdoor (I can no longer make a flush) — I have no pair and only ~6 clean outs (3 Ks + 3 9s) to pair on the river ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% equity. Pot is already large (900) and there is no bet to face right now.
